About Us

YIAN GROUP

YIAN GROUP is an online learning initiative focused on biostatistics and statistical programming. We are building a professional online space where learners, researchers, analysts, and working professionals can access practical training resources.

Contributors: Jin Shi, Jun Ke
Built with a quiet focus on practical clinical data learning.

SAS Training

Programming Language Track

This track will focus on practical SAS skills for clinical statistical programming: DATA step logic, PROC SQL, macros, formats, logs, dataset comparison, transport files, and production-ready programming habits.

Module 01 Getting Started in the SAS Interface Program Editor, Log, Results, Explorer, libraries, Viewtable, and the basic code-submit-check workflow.

Module 01. Getting Started in the SAS Interface

This module starts with the SAS workspace itself. Before writing longer programs, learn where code is submitted, where the log records problems, where results appear, and where data sets live. The working habit is simple: write a small section, submit it, read the log, inspect the data or result, then continue.

0. Interface Map: What You Are Looking At

SAS Display Manager is a Windows-style application. Use the simulator below to click through the main areas: Explorer, Log, Editor, Output tabs, toolbar buttons, and the command box.

0.2 Practice Run: Log vs Output vs Results

Use this second simulator to run a small SAS program and observe the difference between the Log, Output, and Results windows. The main teaching point is that Log tells you whether the run is trustworthy, while Output/Results show what SAS produced.

1.1 The SAS Language

SAS programs are built from statements. Most statements end with a semicolon. A DATA step usually creates or modifies a data set; a PROC step usually analyzes, prints, sorts, or reports a data set.

1.1 SAS Language.sas
/* Every SAS statement ends with a semicolon. */
data work.demo;
  length usubjid $12 trt01p $20;
  usubjid = '101-001';
  trt01p = 'Placebo';
  output;
run;

proc print data=work.demo;
run;
1.2 SAS Data Sets

A SAS data set is a table with observations and variables. Clinical programmers often think in terms of one row per subject, one row per event, or one row per test result.

1.2 SAS Data SetsWORK.ADSL_TRAIN
data work.adsl_train;
  length usubjid trt01p saffl $12;
  input usubjid $ trt01p $ age saffl $;
  datalines;
101-001 Placebo 62 Y
101-002 Active  55 Y
101-003 Active  71 N
;
run;

proc contents data=work.adsl_train;
run;
1.3 DATA and PROC Steps

DATA steps build data. PROC steps do something with data. A common beginner mistake is mixing DATA step statements inside PROC steps or forgetting where a step ends.

1.3 DATA and PROC StepsPattern
data work.safety;
  set work.adsl_train;
  where saffl = 'Y';
run;

proc freq data=work.safety;
  tables trt01p;
run;
1.4 The DATA Step's Built-in Loop

The DATA step runs once for each input observation unless you tell it otherwise. This built-in loop is why one short IF statement can derive a value for every row.

1.4 Built-in LoopDerivation
data work.adsl_flags;
  set work.adsl_train;

  /* SAS repeats this logic for each observation. */
  if age >= 65 then agegr1 = 'Older';
  else agegr1 = 'Younger';
run;
1.5 Choosing a Mode for Submitting SAS Programs

SAS code can be submitted interactively, in batch, or through a scheduler. Training often starts interactively; production clinical work usually requires reproducible batch runs.

1.5 Submission ModeBatch-friendly setup
/* Batch-friendly programs should define inputs, outputs, and logs clearly. */
options nodate nonumber mprint mlogic;

/* In production, paths are usually controlled by project setup macros. */
%let study=YGN101;
%let outds=adsl_train;
1.6 Windows and Commands in the SAS Windowing Environment

Classic SAS has separate windows for program code, log messages, output, explorer, and viewtable. Even in SAS Studio, the same mental model helps: code, log, results, data.

1.6 SAS WindowsCommands
/* Common windowing commands typed into the command bar. */
/* log      - show the SAS log window                         */
/* output   - show classic listing output                     */
/* explorer - browse libraries and data sets                  */
/* viewtable work.adsl_train - open a data set in table view  */
1.7 Submitting a Program in the SAS Windowing Environment

After submitting code, always read the log before trusting the output. A table can be produced even when the log contains warnings that make it unsuitable for delivery.

1.7 Submit ProgramWorkflow
/* Training habit: run a small section, then check the log. */
data work.check_submit;
  set work.adsl_train;
run;

proc print data=work.check_submit obs=5;
run;
1.8 Reading the SAS Log

The log is the first QC tool. For regulated programming, ERROR, WARNING, uninitialized variables, conversions, repeats of BY values, and unexpected record counts all require review.

1.8 Read the LogQC habit
/* Example issue: AGEGR1 is misspelled, creating an uninitialized variable note. */
data work.log_check;
  set work.adsl_flags;
  if agegrp1 = 'Older' then seniorfl = 'Y';
run;

/* Expected log review: search for ERROR, WARNING, uninitialized, converted. */
1.9 Viewing Your Results

Results can appear as listing output, HTML, PDF, RTF, or data sets created from procedure output. In production, output data sets are useful for QC checks.

1.9 View ResultsODS
ods output OneWayFreqs=work.trt_counts;
proc freq data=work.safety;
  tables trt01p;
run;
ods output close;

proc print data=work.trt_counts;
run;
1.10 SAS Data Libraries

A library is a shortcut to a storage location. WORK is temporary. A named library can point to permanent project data such as raw, sdtm, adam, or output folders.

1.10 SAS LibrariesLIBNAME
/* WORK disappears after the SAS session. Project libraries are persistent. */
libname raw "C:\\study\\raw";
libname adam "C:\\study\\adam";

data adam.adsl_train;
  set work.adsl_train;
run;
1.11 Viewing Data Sets in the Viewtable Window

Viewtable is useful for quick inspection, but it is not a substitute for programmed checks. Use it to understand shape, then use PROC PRINT or PROC CONTENTS to document findings.

1.11 ViewtableInspect data
/* Windowing command: viewtable work.adsl_train */

proc print data=work.adsl_train obs=10;
  var usubjid trt01p age saffl;
run;
1.12 Viewing the Properties of Data Sets with SAS Explorer

Dataset properties tell you variable names, types, lengths, labels, formats, sort order, and observation counts. These details often explain merge issues and truncation.

1.12 Dataset PropertiesPROC CONTENTS
proc contents data=work.adsl_train varnum;
run;

/* Quick check: type and length must match before merges. */
proc sql;
  select name, type, length, label
  from dictionary.columns
  where libname = 'WORK' and memname = 'ADSL_TRAIN';
quit;
1.13 Using SAS System Options

System options control how SAS runs and how output/logs look. For training, options help make results readable; for production, they make runs traceable and easier to QC.

1.13 SAS System OptionsOPTIONS
options nodate nonumber validvarname=upcase;
options mprint mlogic symbolgen;

proc options option=validvarname;
run;

/* After debugging macros, turn noisy macro options off if team standard requires it. */
options nomprint nomlogic nosymbolgen;

Statistical Programming Training

Training Library

Follow the modules in order. Each module builds on the previous one, moving from the clinical research setting to source data, analysis datasets, outputs, and quality control.

Reference List of Acronyms Quick definitions for regulatory, CDISC, dataset, output, and quality control terms used in the lessons.
Regulatory, Submission, and Programming Workflow
Acronym Full Term Programmer Focus
21 CFRTitle 21 of the Code of Federal RegulationsU.S. regulation framework; Part 11 is about electronic records and electronic signatures.
ADRGAnalysis Data Reviewer's GuideExplains ADaM datasets, analysis choices, validation issues, and reviewer context.
CSRClinical Study ReportThe final study report where TFL outputs support efficacy, safety, and interpretation.
EMAEuropean Medicines AgencyEuropean regulatory agency; often referenced with Annex 11 and submission expectations.
FDAU.S. Food and Drug AdministrationMajor regulatory reviewer for U.S. submissions.
GCPGood Clinical PracticeQuality and ethics standard for clinical trial conduct, data integrity, and reporting.
GDPRGeneral Data Protection RegulationEuropean privacy regulation for personal data protection.
HIPAAHealth Insurance Portability and Accountability ActU.S. privacy regulation relevant to protected health information.
ICHInternational Council for HarmonisationGlobal guideline body; E3, E6, E8, E9, and E14 appear throughout clinical work.
ICH E3Structure and Content of Clinical Study ReportsGuideline that drives CSR structure and many table/listing numbering expectations.
ICH E6Good Clinical PracticeGuideline for trial conduct, quality, documentation, and data integrity.
ICH E8General Considerations for Clinical StudiesGuideline for study design quality and critical-to-quality thinking.
ICH E9Statistical Principles for Clinical TrialsCore statistical guideline for trial design, analysis planning, and interpretation.
ICH E10Choice of Control GroupGuideline used when thinking about comparator choice and trial interpretability.
ICH E14QT/QTc EvaluationGuideline for cardiac repolarization safety assessment.
ICH E17Multi-Regional Clinical TrialsGuideline for global study design and regional consistency.
ICH S7BNonclinical Evaluation of QT/QTc ProlongationNonclinical cardiac safety guideline often discussed together with E14.
P21Pinnacle 21Validation tool used to check SDTM, ADaM, Define.xml, and submission metadata rules.
PHIProtected Health InformationPatient-identifying information that requires strict privacy and access controls.
QCQuality ControlIndependent checks, log review, compare checks, and review processes that protect output quality.
SDRGStudy Data Reviewer's GuideExplains SDTM datasets, data issues, standards decisions, and validation explanations.
SOPStandard Operating ProcedureCompany-controlled procedure that governs how regulated work must be done.
Core Documents, Systems, and Output Terms
Acronym Full Term Programmer Focus
aCRFAnnotated Case Report FormVisual map from collected CRF fields to SDTM variables and domains.
CRFCase Report FormThe form used to collect clinical study data from sites or study systems.
DTSData Transfer SpecificationVendor file specification describing layout, variables, formats, timing, and transfer rules.
eCRFElectronic Case Report FormElectronic version of the CRF used in an EDC system.
EDCElectronic Data CaptureSystem used by sites and data management to collect clinical data.
ePROElectronic Patient-Reported OutcomePatient-entered source data, often transferred from a vendor instead of collected in EDC.
CSVComma-Separated ValuesCommon text file format used for transfers or intermediate review, but not a final submission dataset format.
PROPatient-Reported OutcomeAssessment reported directly by the patient.
PDFPortable Document FormatCommon format for aCRFs, shells, reviewer guides, and reports.
SAPStatistical Analysis PlanStudy-level analysis rulebook for populations, endpoints, methods, and output planning.
SASStatistical Analysis SystemCommon programming language and platform in regulated statistical programming.
TFL / TLFTables, Figures, and Listings / Tables, Listings, and FiguresFinal reporting outputs. Some teams say TFL, others say TLF; this site uses both where context requires.
XMLExtensible Markup LanguageFormat behind Define.xml and other structured metadata files.
XPTSAS Transport FileRequired transport format for many regulatory SDTM and ADaM dataset submissions.
CDISC Standards and Controlled Terminology
Acronym Full Term Programmer Focus
ADaMAnalysis Data ModelCDISC standard for analysis-ready datasets used to generate TFLs.
BDSBasic Data StructureCommon ADaM structure used for many analysis parameter datasets.
CDASHClinical Data Acquisition Standards HarmonizationCDISC collection standard for CRF data capture.
CDISCClinical Data Interchange Standards ConsortiumStandards organization behind CDASH, SDTM, ADaM, controlled terminology, and metadata standards.
CTControlled TerminologyApproved terms and codelists that prevent private recoding.
EVSEnterprise Vocabulary ServicesNCI service where CDISC controlled terminology is published.
ISO 8601International date/time representation standardStandard format used for SDTM character dates such as YYYY-MM-DD and partial dates.
LOINCLogical Observation Identifiers Names and CodesStandard terminology commonly associated with laboratory tests and observations.
MedDRAMedical Dictionary for Regulatory ActivitiesCoding dictionary for adverse events and medical history.
MSGMetadata Submission GuidelineCDISC guidance for metadata submission and aCRF annotation expectations.
NCINational Cancer InstitutePublisher of CDISC controlled terminology through EVS.
SDTMStudy Data Tabulation ModelCDISC standard for tabulation datasets describing what happened in the trial.
SDTMIGSDTM Implementation GuideDomain-specific implementation rules for SDTM datasets.
WHODrugWorld Health Organization Drug DictionaryCoding dictionary commonly used for concomitant medications.
Common SDTM Domains and Trial Design Domains
Acronym Full Term Programmer Focus
AEAdverse EventsSafety event domain; common source for ADAE and safety tables.
CEClinical EventsClinical event domain for events that are not adverse events or medical history.
CMConcomitant MedicationsMedication domain usually supported by WHODrug coding.
DMDemographicsSpecial-purpose subject-level domain with one record per subject.
DSDispositionSubject disposition events such as completion, discontinuation, or informed consent milestones.
DVProtocol DeviationsProtocol deviation records.
ECExposure as CollectedCollected exposure information before final exposure standardization.
EGElectrocardiogram Test ResultsFindings domain for ECG test results.
EXExposureActual treatment administration records.
FAFindings AboutFindings collected about another event, intervention, or finding.
HOHealthcare EncountersHealthcare encounter records such as hospitalization-related information.
IEInclusion/Exclusion Criteria Not MetSubject-level eligibility exceptions.
LBLaboratory TestsLab findings domain; often transferred from a central lab vendor.
LCLaboratory Tests in Conventional UnitsParallel lab findings domain used for conventional-unit standardized results when required.
MBMicrobiology SpecimenMicrobiology specimen records.
MHMedical HistoryMedical history domain, often coded with MedDRA.
MIMicroscopic FindingsMicroscopic findings records.
PCPharmacokinetic ConcentrationsPK concentration findings.
PEPhysical ExaminationPhysical examination findings.
PRProceduresProcedures performed during the study.
QSQuestionnairesQuestionnaire findings, including some patient-reported outcomes.
RELRECRelated RecordsRelationship dataset used to link related records across domains.
RSDisease ResponseResponse assessment records, often used in oncology studies.
SCSubject CharacteristicsSubject-level characteristics that are not core DM variables.
SESubject ElementsActual subject movement through planned trial elements.
SUSubstance UseRecords for tobacco, alcohol, or other substance use.
SVSubject VisitsActual subject visits, including missed or unscheduled visits.
SUPP--Supplemental QualifiersVertical structure for non-standard qualifiers that do not fit a parent SDTM domain.
TATrial ArmsPlanned arm paths through elements and epochs. Trial design domains are not mapped from CRF fields.
TDMTrial Design ModelSDTM design package describing planned elements, arms, visits, criteria, and study metadata.
TETrial ElementsPlanned study elements such as screening, run-in, treatment, washout, and follow-up.
TITrial Inclusion/Exclusion CriteriaProtocol-defined eligibility criteria.
TSTrial SummaryStudy-level summary parameters for submission metadata.
TVTrial VisitsPlanned visit structure and timing windows.
VSVital SignsFindings domain for measurements such as blood pressure, weight, height, and temperature.
ADaM Datasets and Frequent Analysis Variables
Acronym Full Term Programmer Focus
ADAEADaM Adverse EventsAnalysis-ready adverse event dataset.
ADAEDIFFADaM Adverse Event Difference DatasetCustom or study-specific analysis dataset for AE difference-style summaries.
ADEGADaM Electrocardiogram DatasetAnalysis-ready ECG dataset, often used for QT/QTc analyses.
ADLBADaM Laboratory DatasetAnalysis-ready lab dataset.
ADSLSubject-Level Analysis DatasetOne record per subject; foundation for populations, treatment, and key dates.
ADVSADaM Vital Signs DatasetAnalysis-ready vital signs dataset.
ABLFLAnalysis Baseline FlagMarks the record selected as analysis baseline.
ADTAnalysis DateNumeric SAS analysis date used in ADaM derivations.
ANL01FLAnalysis Record Flag 01Study-defined flag used to select records for a specific analysis.
APHASEAnalysis PhaseBroad analysis phase such as screening, treatment, or follow-up.
APERIOD / APERIODCAnalysis Period / Analysis Period CharacterPeriod assignment used heavily in crossover and multi-period studies.
ASPER / ASPERCAnalysis Subperiod / Analysis Subperiod CharacterFiner assignment inside an analysis period.
AVAL / AVALCAnalysis Value / Analysis Value CharacterNumeric and character analysis result variables.
AVISITAnalysis VisitAnalysis-facing visit label, which may differ from raw visit names.
BASEBaseline ValueBaseline used for change-from-baseline calculations.
CHGChange from BaselineDerived value commonly calculated as AVAL minus BASE.
FAS / FASFLFull Analysis Set / Full Analysis Set FlagPopulation or flag for subjects included in the main efficacy analysis set.
ITT / ITTFLIntent-to-Treat / Intent-to-Treat FlagPopulation or flag based on randomized subjects, depending on the SAP definition.
PARAM / PARAMCDParameter / Parameter CodeAnalysis parameter label and short code.
PP / PPSFLPer-Protocol / Per-Protocol Set FlagPopulation or flag for subjects meeting protocol-defined evaluability criteria.
RANDDT / RANDFLRandomization Date / Randomized FlagSubject-level randomization variables commonly stored in ADSL.
SAF / SAFFLSafety Analysis Set / Safety Analysis Set FlagPopulation or flag for subjects included in safety analyses.
TRTA / TRTxxAActual Treatment / Actual Treatment by PeriodTreatment actually received or assigned for analysis.
TRTxxPPlanned Treatment by PeriodPlanned treatment for period xx, such as TRT01P and TRT02P.
TRTSEQA / TRTSEQPActual / Planned Treatment SequenceTreatment sequence variables often used in crossover designs.
Common SDTM Variable Fragments and Safety Terms
Acronym Full Term Programmer Focus
--DTCCharacter Date/TimeSDTM ISO 8601 character date/time variable pattern, such as AEDTC or VSDTC.
--ORRESOriginal ResultCollected result before standardization, such as VSORRES or LBORRES.
--SEQSequence NumberProgram-derived record sequence within a subject and domain; not mapped from CRF fields.
--STRESC / --STRESNStandardized Result Character / NumericStandardized result values after unit conversion or controlled format rules.
AEDECODDictionary-Derived TermMedDRA preferred term for an adverse event.
AEBODSYSBody System or Organ ClassMedDRA system organ class used for AE grouping.
AESERSerious Event FlagIndicates whether an adverse event is serious.
AESEVSeverity / IntensityAdverse event severity, usually controlled terminology.
AETERMReported Term for the Adverse EventVerbatim or collected adverse event term.
ARM / ARMCDPlanned Arm / Planned Arm CodeSubject's planned treatment arm label and code.
ETCDElement CodeTrial element code used in TE and TA.
EPOCHEpochPlanned study phase such as screening, treatment, washout, or follow-up.
DOMAINDomain AbbreviationTwo-letter SDTM domain code such as AE, DM, LB, or VS.
IDVAR / IDVARVALIdentifying Variable / Identifying Variable ValueSUPP-- variables that point back to the parent domain record.
ORRESU / STRESUOriginal Result Unit / Standard Result UnitCollected and standardized units for findings results.
QNAM / QLABEL / QVALQualifier Name / Label / ValueSUPP-- variables used to store supplemental qualifiers.
STAT / REASNDStatus / Reason Not DoneCommon variable fragments used when a test or assessment was not performed.
SOC / PTSystem Organ Class / Preferred TermMedDRA hierarchy levels used in safety summaries.
STUDYIDStudy IdentifierStudy-level identifier in SDTM and ADaM datasets.
TAETORDTrial Arm Element OrderOrder of planned elements within an arm in TA.
TEST / TESTCDTest Name / Test CodeFindings domain test label and short controlled code.
TRTEMFLTreatment-Emergent Analysis FlagADaM flag identifying treatment-emergent adverse events.
USUBJIDUnique Subject IdentifierStudy-wide unique subject key used across SDTM and ADaM.
VISIT / VISITNUMVisit Name / Visit NumberVisit label and numeric visit order.
Other Clinical and Safety Terms
Acronym Full Term Programmer Focus
ATCAnatomical Therapeutic Chemical ClassificationWHODrug-related medication classification often used for concomitant medication summaries.
ANCOVAAnalysis of CovarianceCommon statistical model used for adjusted treatment comparisons.
BMIBody Mass IndexCommon derived clinical measurement.
BPBlood PressureVital signs measurement; commonly split into systolic and diastolic BP.
DHTDigital Health TechnologyDevice or technology source that may produce external clinical data.
ECGElectrocardiogramCardiac safety data source used in EG/ADEG and QT analyses.
IPInvestigational ProductStudy treatment under investigation.
IRTInteractive Response TechnologySystem commonly used for randomization and drug supply assignment.
MADMultiple Ascending DoseEarly-phase design with escalating repeated doses.
PKPharmacokineticsDrug concentration and exposure analysis area, often using PC data.
QT / QTc / QTcFQT Interval / Corrected QT / Fridericia-Corrected QTCardiac repolarization measures often analyzed under ICH E14 thinking.
SADSingle Ascending DoseEarly-phase design with escalating single doses.
SMARTSequential Multiple Assignment Randomized TrialAdaptive design where later treatment paths may depend on earlier response.
Module 01 Clinical Research Environment & Regulatory Compliance Clinical context, compliance expectations, core documents, data flow, and programming standards.

1. Regulatory Framework & Red Lines

Clinical trial data collection, programming, analysis, and reporting must follow international quality standards and local regulatory requirements. For statistical programmers, compliance is not separate from programming; it shapes how data is collected, transformed, reviewed, and submitted.

1.1 Global Regulatory Framework

  • International Council for Harmonisation Good Clinical Practice (ICH-GCP) E6(R2/R3): Good Clinical Practice is the international ethical and quality standard for designing, conducting, recording, and reporting clinical trials. In programming work, GCP means that data manipulations, analyses, and reporting outputs must protect subject safety and preserve trial integrity.
  • 21 Code of Federal Regulations (21 CFR) Part 11 and European Medicines Agency (EMA) Annex 11: These regulations address electronic records, electronic signatures, system access, audit trails, and data reliability. Programming environments must support controlled access, traceable changes, secure storage, and reproducible execution.
  • Data privacy regulations: The Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act (HIPAA) in the United States and the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) in the European Union protect patient privacy. Protected Health Information (PHI) should not appear in analysis-ready programming environments unless specifically permitted and controlled. De-identification, pseudonymization, date handling, and access control are part of responsible data handling.

1.2 Electronic Records: 21 CFR Part 11 & EMA Annex 11

In a digital clinical trial environment, electronic data, source code, execution logs, dataset timestamps, review comments, and electronic signatures are regulated records. The core expectation is that these records must be authentic, reliable, attributable, protected from unauthorized change, and reproducible during an inspection.

  • No manual overwrites: Programmers should not open a SAS dataset, R dataframe, CSV file, or Excel listing and directly alter a data point. Even an obvious typo must be corrected through the proper data management query process, fixed in the source system when appropriate, and then reprocessed through the controlled pipeline.
  • Traceable code changes: Code updates must be tracked in a validated version control or programming environment. A commit message such as "fixed bug" is not enough for regulated work; the change should explain why it was made, what logic or output was affected, and which protocol, SAP, shell, specification, or review finding required the update.
  • Unique user accountability: User IDs and passwords must not be shared. Server audit trails tie logins, file edits, program executions, and approvals to a specific person. Programmers should also lock their workstation whenever they step away, because work performed under their account is attributed to them.
  • Controlled environments: Production data and production outputs are usually protected by read-only access, release controls, and validated execution paths. Programmers should not install unapproved R, Python, or SAS components on production systems or bypass Dev-to-Prod promotion workflows.
  • Clean, retained logs: SAS logs, R outputs, validation reports, and execution metadata are inspection evidence. Errors, warnings, automatic type conversions, uninitialized variables, and unexplained record count changes should be investigated and resolved rather than hidden or deleted.

1.3 Data Privacy: HIPAA, GDPR, and Patient-Level Data

Statistical programmers are one of the final safeguards against privacy leakage. HIPAA, GDPR, and sponsor policies require patient-level data to be minimized, pseudonymized, access-controlled, and transferred only through approved channels.

  • Remove direct identifiers: Analysis-ready datasets should not contain patient names, full addresses, phone numbers, email addresses, national identifiers, medical record numbers, or other direct identifiers unless a specific controlled process requires them.
  • Use pseudonymized subject keys: Subjects should be tracked with consistent study identifiers such as USUBJID, commonly formed from protocol, site, and subject number. The same pseudonymized key must remain consistent across SDTM, ADaM, listings, and reconciliation outputs.
  • Handle dates carefully: Exact birth dates and full calendar dates can become indirect identifiers, especially in rare disease or small studies. Programmers may need to derive age, study day, or shifted dates according to the protocol, data transfer agreement, SAP, and privacy requirements, then remove the unnecessary high-risk source date.
  • Audit free text: Verbatim fields and comment fields can contain accidental identifiers, such as a patient name inside an adverse event term or a protocol deviation description. These fields should be reviewed, queried, masked, or redacted through a controlled process before delivery.
  • Keep data inside approved systems: Patient-level SAS datasets, XPT files, CSVs, Excel listings, screenshots, and extracts should not be saved to personal drives, unsecured local folders, external drives, personal email, or chat attachments. Use secure project directories and permission-controlled file links.

1.4 Where Data Standards Fit

Programmers do not need to learn every data standard on the first day. The first idea is simpler: clinical trial data cannot stay as a pile of site forms, EDC exports, vendor spreadsheets, and one-off file layouts. Before analysis and submission, the data must be reorganized into a standard path so the study team and regulators can follow where each value came from and how it was used.

The standard language behind that path is CDISC. CDISC, the Clinical Data Interchange Standards Consortium, is a global, non-profit organization that defines data standards for clinical research. Think of CDISC as the universal grammar for clinical trials: because of it, a reviewer at the FDA or EMA can open a submission dataset and know where to find a subject's age, adverse events, lab results, exposure records, and analysis variables.

Raw Data Collected
Site forms, EDC exports, and vendor files.
RAW_VS_EDC_EXPORTEDC CSV export
SUBJIDVISITFORMVSDATTESTRESULTUNIT
002BASELINEVITALS2026-01-08Weight82kg
002WEEK 4VITALS2026-02-05Weight80.4kg
002WEEK 4VITALS2026-02-05Systolic BP128mmHg

Raw data looks like a site or vendor export: form names, local visit text, and original units are still visible.

CDASH
Input standard for cleaner CRF and EDC collection.
CDASH_VScollection-standard dataset
STUDYIDSITEIDSUBJIDVISITVSDATVSTESTVSORRESVSORRESU
ABC-123101002BASELINE2026-01-08Weight82kg
ABC-123101002WEEK 42026-02-05Weight80.4kg
ABC-123101002WEEK 42026-02-05Systolic BP128mmHg

Note: CDASH is often a collection design standard rather than a separate dataset deliverable. Many companies map directly from raw EDC/vendor data to SDTM, while using CDASH principles to make CRF fields and raw exports easier to standardize.

SDTM
Tabulation standard showing what happened to the subject.
vs.xptSDTM VS domain
STUDYIDUSUBJIDVSTESTCDVSORRESVSORRESUVSSTRESNVSSTRESU
ABC-123ABC-123-002WEIGHT80.4kg80.4kg
ABC-123ABC-123-002SYSBP128mmHg128mmHg

SDTM looks like a submitted standard dataset: standard domain, standard variables, and traceable subject records.

ADaM
Analysis-ready datasets with derivations and population flags.
ADVSanalysis-ready dataset
USUBJIDPARAMCDAVISITBASEAVALCHGSAFFL
ABC-123-002WEIGHTBaseline82.082.0.Y
ABC-123-002WEIGHTWeek 482.080.4-1.6Y

ADaM starts to look like the dataset a table program can use: baseline, change, visit labels, and population flags are ready.

TFLs
Final tables, listings, and figures for reporting.
Table 14.1.1.1 Change in Weight at Week 4 Safety Analysis Set
Placebo
(N=42)		Active
(N=44)
Baseline, kg
n4244
Mean (SD)82.1 (12.4)81.7 (11.9)
Median81.880.9
Min, Max58.2, 112.459.1, 110.8
Change from baseline, kg
n4244
Mean (SD)-0.4 (2.1)-1.8 (2.4)
Median-0.2-1.5
Min, Max-5.1, 4.2-7.0, 2.8

TFLs should feel like the final report output, not like a web table. The layout, title, columns, and footnotes are part of the deliverable.

Practice Quiz: Data Flow and Standards

Use this short quiz to check the difference between raw data, CDASH, SDTM, ADaM, and TFLs.

  • CDASH is the input standard: It helps design CRF and EDC fields so data is collected consistently from the beginning.
  • SDTM is the tabulation standard: It organizes collected clinical data into standard domains such as DM for demographics, AE for adverse events, LB for labs, and VS for vital signs. At this stage, the goal is to represent what happened to the subject, not to perform statistical analysis.
  • ADaM is the analysis standard: It uses SDTM and SAP rules to build analysis-ready datasets, including baseline values, change from baseline, imputation rules, analysis visits, and population flags. Final TFLs are generated from ADaM datasets.

This pipeline explains why private shortcuts are forbidden. A shortcut means bypassing part of the approved chain, such as pulling raw EDC data directly into a final table because it feels faster. That may save time today, but it breaks the ability to trace a final number back to ADaM, SDTM, and the original source record.

We will introduce the detailed programming specifications for CDASH, SDTM, and ADaM later in the data mapping lessons. For this first compliance lesson, remember the practical rule: respect the pipeline, document every derivation, and protect the chain of custody from source data to final tables.

1.5 Red Lines: Compliance Boundaries

The following red lines are practical boundaries for clinical programming. Crossing them can compromise data integrity, patient privacy, study validity, or regulatory credibility.

  • ALCOA+ data integrity: Data and programming actions should be attributable, legible, contemporaneous, original, accurate, complete, consistent, enduring, and available. Untraceable data changes, back-dated sign-offs, and undocumented fixes are not acceptable.
  • No hardcoded source data fixes: Programmers should not directly correct subject data inside SAS, R, or Python code, such as forcing a single subject's value to a desired result. Source data corrections should go through appropriate data management processes and audit trails.
  • Blinding integrity: In blinded studies, programmers should not access randomization or unblinded treatment information unless formally assigned to an unblinded role. Premature or unauthorized blind-breaking analyses can damage the interpretability of the trial.
  • Patient privacy: Names, initials, national IDs, exact addresses, and other direct identifiers should not be included in SDTM, ADaM, TFLs, or general analysis environments unless a controlled and approved process requires it.
  • Independent Quality Control (QC): Primary and QC programming should remain independent where required. Copying production code into QC work to force a match defeats the purpose of validation and can hide logic errors.
  • Validated tools and audit trails: Submission-critical work should use approved and validated systems, macros, packages, and workflows. Logs should not be deleted or edited to hide errors, warnings, or important notes.

2. Bridge to the Study Documents

The compliance rules above may sound abstract at first. In daily programming, they become much more concrete because programmers do not make rules from memory. They check approved study documents, then turn those instructions into traceable code.

For the first lesson, only remember the basic job of each document. The next lesson will show how to read them in detail.

  • Protocol: Explains what the trial is trying to study, who can join, what visits happen, and which assessments are collected.
  • SAP: Explains how the study will be analyzed, including populations, baseline rules, endpoints, missing data rules, and statistical methods.
  • TLF shells: Show what the final tables, listings, and figures should look like, including titles, rows, columns, and footnotes.
  • aCRF and vendor specs: Help programmers find where source data came from, whether it was entered in the EDC or transferred from an outside vendor such as a lab or ECG provider.

This is the key transition into Module 02: if Module 01 teaches what not to violate, Module 02 teaches where to find the approved instructions before writing code.

3. End-to-End Clinical Data Flow

Before working with datasets, a programmer should understand how clinical data moves from patient visits to analysis outputs.

Clinical Site / Hospital
Doctors and site staff collect patient data during study visits.
eCRF / EDC
Patient data is entered into an electronic data capture system.
Raw Datasets
Data management teams clean, query, and export source data.
SDTM Datasets
Raw data is standardized according to CDISC SDTM structures.
ADaM Datasets
Analysis-ready datasets are derived for statistical analysis.
Tables, Listings, and Figures
Final outputs support clinical study reporting and submission packages.

4. SOPs & Coding Standards

In a regulated industry, code is not just a personal tool. It is a collaborative, permanent corporate asset. If a programmer goes on vacation, leaves the company, or joins another study, a teammate should be able to open the program and understand the purpose, inputs, outputs, assumptions, and review history within minutes.

  • SOPs control the process: Standard Operating Procedures define how study folders are created, who can move files from Development to Production, how review comments are documented, how data issues are escalated, and how final deliverables are archived.
  • Training sign-off matters: Before touching active study data or production programs, team members usually complete a training matrix and sign off that they have read and understood the required programming SOPs.
  • Coding standards control the script: Standards cover headers, indentation, naming conventions, comments, log checks, output locations, and version history so code remains readable, reproducible, and reviewable.

A compliant program usually begins with a required header block. This is not decoration; it tells the reviewer what the program does, which data it uses, what it creates, who wrote it, and why later changes were made.

SAS Program Header Example .sas 
/*****************************************************************************
* Company Name: YIAN GROUP Training
* Study Protocol: ABC-123
* Program Name: adae.sas
* Purpose: Create the Adverse Event Analysis Dataset (ADAE)
* Inputs: sdtm.ae, sdtm.dm, adam.adsl
* Outputs: adam.adae
*-----------------------------------------------------------------------------
* Author: John Doe (j.doe@company.com)
* Creation Date: 2026-06-28
*-----------------------------------------------------------------------------
* Modification History:
* Date        Author       Reference      Description of Change
* ----------  -----------  -------------  ------------------------------------
* 2026-07-15  Jane Smith   Ticket #442    Updated missing onset date imputation
*                                         per SAP Amendment 2.
*****************************************************************************/

Practical rule: never start functional SAS, R, or Python code on line 1. First give the reviewer a map of the program.

4.1 Readable Code Habits
  • Use consistent layout: Follow the team standard for indentation, spacing, dataset names, variable names, and section breaks. A reviewer should not need to decode your personal style.
  • Remove dead code: Do not leave large blocks of commented-out experimental code in final production programs. Version control keeps history; production programs should show the current approved logic.
  • Comment the reason, not the obvious action: Comments should explain why logic exists, especially for protocol exceptions, SAP rules, data anomalies, or sponsor decisions.
SAS Comment Examples Good vs Bad 
/* Bad: repeats the code without explaining the reason. */
/* Substringing the variable */
age_num = input(substr(ageres, 1, 2), best.);

/* Better: explains the study rule and document source. */
/* Remove the "YEARS" suffix from AGERES before numeric conversion,
   per ADaM specification page 12. */
age_num = input(scan(ageres, 1, " "), best.);
4.2 Log Checking: The Ultimate Defense

A program that runs is not automatically correct. The execution log is often the first place where data integrity problems appear. Before submitting work for review, programmers should run the team log-check utility or inspect logs line by line.

Critical Keywords to Search and Resolve
In SAS Environments In R / Python Environments Action Required by the Programmer
ERROR: Error: Critical failure. The program stopped executing. Must be fixed immediately.
WARNING: Warning: Potential data corruption, such as dropped variables, length mismatches, or failed calculations. Must be resolved.
uninitialized NaN / NA creation A variable was called but does not exist, or a calculation created missing values unexpectedly. Check spelling, input structure, and upstream data changes.
w.d format too small Numerical truncation or coercion warnings Data values may be clipped, rounded, or converted incorrectly on export. Use explicit formats and verify displayed values.
MERGE statement has repeats Many-to-many merge or join warning Unexpected data multiplication may have occurred. Confirm unique key variables before merging and compare record counts before and after the join.
4.2.1 Manual SAS Log Review Checklist

A log scan is more than a keyword search. Each message should be reviewed for its likely programming impact, then traced back to the code, input data, and expected output record counts.

Review Dimension Target Objective Keywords & Actions Training Notes / Best Practices
01. Critical Failures Identify blocking program errors that can invalidate the output. Search for ERROR:. Confirm whether the step stopped, skipped output creation, or produced partial results. Zero tolerance: production code cannot be released with active errors. Resolve the root cause, rerun the affected program, and confirm downstream outputs were regenerated.
02. System Warnings Catch execution, syntax, format, and environment risks. Search for WARNING:. Review every warning, even if the program completed. Investigation required: warnings may indicate missing formats, invalid options, truncated output, unresolved macro variables, or unexpected data behavior.
03. Variable Definition Detect uninitialized or misspelled variables before they propagate. Search for uninitialized. Compare variable names against the SDTM/ADaM specification and input dataset metadata. Typo alert: this is often caused by misspelled variable names, missing input variables, or conditional logic that references a variable before assignment.
04. Type Conversion Monitor character/numeric mismatches and hidden precision problems. Search for converted and review automatic character-to-numeric or numeric-to-character conversions. Data integrity: implicit conversion can create missing values, strip leading zeros, change date interpretation, or affect precision. Use explicit conversion rules when needed.
05. Merge Logic Validate key uniqueness and prevent unintended row multiplication. Search for repeats of BY values and MERGE statement has repeats. Check key counts before and after merge. Logic check: many-to-many relationships during a merge can duplicate subjects, visits, or events. Confirm the intended grain of each input dataset.
06. Data Integrity Cross-check record consistency across input, intermediate, and final datasets. Compare input vs. output observation counts, duplicate keys, expected population counts, and major derivation flags. Final gatekeeping: verify that actual row counts match the expected business logic before QC compare, table generation, or delivery.

Practice Quiz: SAS Log Scan

Use the quiz below to practice identifying common SAS log issues, deciding whether they block delivery, and choosing the correct follow-up action.

4.3 Independent QC and Compare

Regulated programming relies on independent validation. The production programmer writes the main program, and a QC programmer independently interprets the same SAP and specifications. The goal is not to copy the production code; the goal is to catch misunderstanding before delivery.

Production deliverables should remain as produced and archived. In QC, compare directly against the production dataset and prepare only the independent QC dataset if sorting or key alignment is needed.

Production Programmer
Creates the production dataset or output from approved specifications.
QC Programmer
Builds an independent QC version from the same documents.
Automated Compare
Compares records, variables, labels, formats, values, and types.
Resolution
Any mismatch is investigated, documented, corrected, and rerun.
SAS PROC COMPARE Example QC 
/* Do not modify, overwrite, or pre-sort the production deliverable.
   Compare directly against adam.adae. */
data qc_adae;
  set qc.adae;
run;

proc sort data=qc_adae;
  by usubjid aeseq paramcd avisitn;
run;

proc compare base=adam.adae
             compare=qc_adae
             criterion=1e-10
             listall;
  id usubjid aeseq paramcd avisitn;
run;
Onboarding Takeaway

SOPs protect the company from regulatory findings, but day-to-day coding standards protect the people who must read, validate, rerun, and explain the program later. A clean program should make the data source, derivation logic, sorting rule, and output location easy to defend without relying on memory or informal handoff notes.

In practice, write every program as if the next programmer will open it during an audit or database lock review. Use clear section headers, stable naming, traceable intermediate datasets, and comments only where they explain a decision that would not be obvious from the code itself.

Module 02 Clinical Trial Documents Programmers Must Read Protocol, Statistical Analysis Plan (SAP), Tables, Listings, and Figures (TLF) shells, annotated Case Report Form (aCRF), Data Transfer Specifications (DTS), and Define.xml.

1. Why Documents Come Before Programming

Statistical programming is specification-driven work. Every dataset, derived variable, output title, footnote, population flag, and quality-control check is governed by approved study documents. These documents are the inputs, rules, and blueprints for compliant programming.

A programmer should not code from memory or from an informal chat message. When a rule is unclear, the correct response is to identify the controlling document, record the question, and confirm the decision with the statistician, data manager, or study team.

2. Document Dependency Map

Protocol
Clinical intent, visits, assessments, endpoints, eligibility, and trial conduct.
SAP
Analysis populations, estimands, baseline rules, missing data rules, and statistical methods.
SDTM / ADaM Specs
Excel-level programming details: variables, labels, types, origins, derivations, and algorithms.
aCRF / DTS / Dictionaries
Source fields, vendor transfer rules, MedDRA, WHODrug, and coded safety terms.
Code to SDTM / ADaM / TFLs
SAS or R programs create standardized datasets and final outputs.
P21 Report
Automated validation checks identify rejects, errors, warnings, and notices.
Define.xml + SDRG / ADRG
Submission metadata and reviewer guides explain the package to regulators.

3. Core Documents and What to Look For

Document Statistician Focus Programmer Focus
Protocol Study objectives, estimands or endpoints, visit schedule, populations, and key clinical rules. Visit structure, treatment arms, endpoint timing, inclusion/exclusion logic, and assessment schedule.
SAP Analysis populations, endpoint definitions, statistical methods, missing data handling, and sensitivity analyses. Derivation rules, population flags, baseline rules, visit windows, output requirements, and analysis variables.
TLF Shells Output intent, population, endpoint, treatment comparison, statistics, and footnote interpretation. Output layout, row and column structure, formats, titles, footnotes, and QC targets.
SDTM / ADaM Specs How study rules become dataset-level metadata and variable-level derivations. Variable names, labels, types, lengths, origins, controlled terminology, and derivation algorithms.
Annotated CRF How clinical concepts are collected and where source evidence originates. Source fields, CRF page names, raw variable origin, and SDTM domain mapping clues.
Data Transfer Spec Expected vendor data content, transfer frequency, and scope of externally supplied data. File names, formats, key variables, codelists, units, date formats, and reconciliation checks.
Define.xml Submission metadata, dataset purpose, controlled terminology, and reviewer traceability. Dataset labels, variable attributes, origins, derivations, value-level metadata, and links to codelists.
SDRG / ADRG How reviewers should understand unusual SDTM or ADaM data, decisions, and known issues. Data explanations, standard deviations, unresolved validation issues, reviewer notes, and analysis caveats.
P21 Report Which automated validation rules are failing or need explanation. Rejects, errors, warnings, notices, rule IDs, affected datasets, affected variables, and resolution status.

4. Clinical Study Protocol: The Master Blueprint

The protocol is the legal and operational blueprint of the clinical trial. It defines why the study is being run, who can enter, what treatment they receive, which assessments are collected, and what clinical questions the study is meant to answer.

  • Schedule of Activities: Use the SoA to understand which assessments are expected at Screening, Baseline, treatment visits, follow-up, and End of Study. It is the first source for visit timing and expected data.
  • Objectives and endpoints: Read the primary, secondary, and exploratory endpoints before building endpoint variables. The endpoint wording often determines the analysis value, baseline definition, and target visit.
  • Eligibility rules: Inclusion and exclusion criteria support eligibility checks, protocol deviation review, and population definitions.
  • Programming impact: The protocol drives visit-window logic, expected assessment checks, treatment period definitions, unscheduled visit handling, and questions such as whether an ECG, lab, or vital sign record is expected at a specific visit.

5. Statistical Analysis Plan: The Daily Programming Rulebook

The SAP turns the clinical intent of the protocol into executable analysis rules. For ADaM and TFL programming, this is usually the most frequently opened document.

  • Analysis populations: Translate population definitions into precise ADSL flags such as SAFFL, FASFL, ITTFL, PPSFL, or other study-specific flags. Do not infer a population from the shell alone.
  • Baseline definitions: Implement the exact SAP rule, including date and time logic. If baseline is the last non-missing value before first dose, a Day 1 post-dose value must not accidentally become baseline.
  • Missing data and imputation: Follow the specified approach for missing efficacy, safety, dates, severity, relationship, or endpoint values. LOCF, multiple imputation, non-responder imputation, and partial date imputation are not interchangeable.
  • Derived variables and windows: Use SAP rules for study day, change from baseline, BMI, endpoint derivations, visit windows, analysis visit assignment, censoring, and sensitivity analyses.

6. TLF Mock Shells: The Target Output Design

Mock shells show what the final tables, listings, and figures should look like. They are not just formatting examples; they contain programming instructions in titles, subtitles, column headers, row labels, and footnotes.

  • Titles and headers: Confirm table number, endpoint, visit, treatment groups, population, and whether the output is descriptive, inferential, safety, efficacy, or listing-based.
  • Rows and columns: Match grouping, sorting, indentation, decimal precision, statistics, denominator rules, and treatment comparisons.
  • Footnotes: Read every footnote before coding. Footnotes often define source datasets, analysis populations, statistical models, date cutoffs, denominator logic, and abbreviation meanings.
  • Programming impact: Final output code should match the shell layout closely, but the source and logic must still be reconciled against the SAP and specifications.

7. SDTM / ADaM Programming Specifications: The Code Translator

The SAP gives the statistical rule, but the programming specification turns that rule into dataset-level and variable-level instructions. In many projects, the spec is a large Excel workbook that programmers open constantly while writing SDTM, ADaM, and TFL code.

  • Metadata columns: Learn to read variable name, label, type, length, format, controlled terminology, role, origin, and comments.
  • Derivation or algorithm column: This is the direct bridge to code. It should explain how each variable is assigned, copied, merged, derived, imputed, flagged, or populated conditionally.
  • Spec versus SAP: The SAP defines the analysis intent. The spec translates that intent into implementable dataset logic. If they conflict, stop and raise a question instead of choosing one silently.
  • Programming impact: Do not build production code from an unfinished or unapproved spec unless the team has explicitly authorized that development stage. Draft specs often change and can cause avoidable rework.

8. Annotated CRF: The Source Data Map

The annotated CRF is the bridge between what the site entered in the EDC and how the data is represented in SDTM. It helps programmers understand where each source field came from and how it should be interpreted.

  • Domain markings: Page and field annotations point to domains such as DM, AE, CM, LB, VS, ECG, or exposure-related domains.
  • Variable annotations: Field-level labels indicate target SDTM variables or mapping clues, such as DM.SEX, VSORRES, VSPOS, or AETERM.
  • Origin metadata: Determine whether a value is collected on the CRF, derived by the system, assigned by the sponsor, or imported from an external source.
  • Programming impact: If a raw variable is not annotated or an annotated CRF field has no raw data, flag the discrepancy. Do not silently map unmapped data or leave collected data behind without explanation.

9. Data Transfer Specifications: Vendor Data Rules

Many important data streams do not come directly from the EDC. Central labs, ECG vendors, PK labs, imaging vendors, ePRO vendors, and biomarker labs often transfer data using a Data Transfer Specification.

  • File structure: Check file names, file type, delimiter, encoding, expected worksheets, header rows, required columns, and transfer frequency.
  • Merge keys: Confirm subject ID, site, visit, sample ID, accession number, specimen collection date/time, and any vendor-specific identifiers used for reconciliation.
  • Units and codelists: Validate units, result codes, normal ranges, flags, test names, date formats, missing value conventions, and version-controlled dictionaries.
  • Programming impact: Incoming vendor files should be checked against the DTS before production processing. A changed column name or date format can silently corrupt study day, visit assignment, or merge results.

10. MedDRA and WHODrug: External Coding Dictionaries

Safety programming depends heavily on coded medical terminology. Site-entered adverse events, medical history terms, and concomitant medications often begin as verbatim text, then Data Management codes them using controlled external dictionaries before analysis.

  • MedDRA: Used for adverse events and medical history. It provides hierarchy such as System Organ Class and Preferred Term, which often drive safety table grouping and sorting.
  • WHODrug: Used for concomitant medications and includes drug names, ingredients, and ATC classifications.
  • Programming impact: ADAE, ADCM, and safety TFLs frequently rely on coded variables such as AEBODSYS, AEDECOD, CMDECOD, and ATC classes. Frequency counts and grouping logic should use the approved coded terms, not raw verbatim text.
  • Review habit: If dictionary-coded fields are missing, inconsistent, or unexpectedly changed between transfers, raise the issue with Data Management instead of recoding terms manually in analysis code.

11. Define.xml and Submission Metadata

Define.xml explains the submitted datasets to reviewers. It documents dataset purpose, variable labels, variable attributes, origins, derivations, value-level metadata, controlled terminology, and links to reviewer guides or external codelists.

  • Use it as a traceability check: The final SDTM and ADaM datasets should agree with the metadata that define.xml describes.
  • Check variable-level details: Labels, types, lengths, codelists, origins, and derivations should be consistent with specifications and actual datasets.
  • Programming impact: If a submitted variable is derived, the derivation should be understandable from the metadata and traceable to source records or documented rules.

12. SDRG and ADRG: Reviewer Guides

Define.xml is metadata, but it does not tell the whole story. Reviewer guides explain the study data package in plain language. The SDRG explains SDTM data, while the ADRG explains ADaM data and analysis-specific decisions.

  • SDRG: The Study Data Reviewer's Guide explains SDTM package structure, known data issues, conformance notes, special study design considerations, and unresolved validation findings.
  • ADRG: The Analysis Data Reviewer's Guide explains ADaM datasets, analysis derivations, traceability, departures from standard expectations, and how analysis results should be interpreted.
  • Programmer responsibility: Programmers are often major contributors to the ADRG. If code intentionally handles unusual data, deviates from a common standard because of study design, or leaves a validation issue unresolved with justification, the reviewer guide must explain it.
  • Practical rule: Anything that would make a reviewer ask "Why did the data look like this?" probably belongs in SDRG, ADRG, or a documented response trail.

13. Pinnacle 21 Validation Report: The Automated Rule Check

After datasets and metadata are built, validation tools such as Pinnacle 21 are used to scan SDTM, ADaM, and Define.xml against conformance rules. The resulting report is a core review document for programmers, not an afterthought.

  • Severity levels: Learn to triage Reject, Error, Warning, and Notice findings. High-severity findings can block submission readiness, while lower-severity findings still need review and disposition.
  • Resolution loop: Read the P21 rule, identify the affected dataset or variable, check the spec and code, fix the program when appropriate, rerun validation, and document any finding that remains by design.
  • Connection to reviewer guides: If a finding cannot or should not be fixed because it reflects the protocol, data reality, or approved study design, the explanation should be captured in SDRG or ADRG.

14. How to Resolve Document Questions

A good programmer does not guess when documents seem unclear. The usual approach is to identify the controlling document, record the question clearly, and confirm with the statistician, data manager, or study team as appropriate.

  • Clinical timing: Start with the Protocol and Schedule of Assessments.
  • Analysis rule: Start with the SAP and programming specifications.
  • Output layout: Start with TLF shells and output specifications.
  • Source field: Start with annotated CRF, raw data, and data transfer specifications.
  • Safety coding: Check coded terms, MedDRA, WHODrug, and Data Management coding decisions.
  • Submission metadata: Check Define.xml, SDRG, ADRG, and the study metadata package.
  • Validation finding: Check the P21 report, rule details, specification, code, and reviewer guide explanation.

15. Summary of Document Dependencies

Document Primary Question It Answers When to Open It
Protocol What was the clinical intent, timing, and planned conduct of the trial? When defining visits, windows, endpoints, eligibility checks, or data anomalies.
SAP What exact analysis rule or algorithm should be implemented? Before programming ADaM variables, population flags, endpoints, or analysis outputs.
SDTM / ADaM Specs How should each dataset and variable be built in code? Before writing production SDTM or ADaM programs and whenever derivation logic changes.
TLF Shells What should the final report look like, and which source and population does it use? While planning and programming TFL production and QC outputs.
Annotated CRF Where in the source data was the clinical information collected? When mapping raw EDC fields to SDTM domains and checking source traceability.
DTS How should external vendor data be parsed, validated, merged, and reconciled? When importing lab, ECG, PK, biomarker, imaging, or other non-EDC data.
MedDRA / WHODrug How are verbatim safety and medication terms coded for analysis? When programming ADAE, ADCM, safety summaries, SOC/PT counts, drug classes, or coding checks.
Define.xml How will reviewers understand the submitted datasets and variables? When validating submission metadata, derivations, codelists, and traceability.
SDRG / ADRG How should reviewers understand data issues, analysis choices, and validation explanations? When packaging submission data, explaining nonstandard decisions, or documenting unresolved findings.
P21 Report Which automated validation findings must be fixed or explained? After dataset or metadata creation, before delivery, and after any major code or spec update.

Project Exercise: Protocol Amendment 02 Chain Reaction

A realistic project does not stand still. Imagine the study team releases Protocol Amendment 02. The Week 12 visit window changes, a new ECG assessment is added, and the primary efficacy endpoint wording is updated.

  1. Which documents must be checked first?
  2. Which specifications may need updates before programming can safely continue?
  3. Which datasets, outputs, validation reports, and reviewer guides may be affected?
Suggested Update Order
  1. Read the protocol amendment and confirm the operational changes with the study team.
  2. Check whether the SAP needs an amendment or clarification for endpoints, windows, and analysis rules.
  3. Update or review SDTM and ADaM specs before changing production code.
  4. Confirm aCRF, DTS, and vendor transfer changes for any new ECG or visit data.
  5. Update SDTM, ADaM, and TFL programs, then rerun QC and compare outputs.
  6. Run P21 validation again and review new or changed findings.
  7. Update Define.xml, SDRG, and ADRG if metadata, derivations, validation explanations, or reviewer notes changed.

Practice Quiz: Mock TLF QC Detective

Use this interactive quiz to practice cross-checking a mock TLF shell against the SAP, Protocol, ADaM specification, and programming action before output delivery.

Module 03 Understanding Case Report Forms (CRF) and Annotated CRFs (aCRF) How clinical source data is collected, exported, annotated, and prepared before Study Data Tabulation Model (SDTM) mapping begins.

1. What the CRF Controls

A Case Report Form is the study's clinical data collection instrument. In an electronic data capture system, the eCRF is what site staff use to enter visit data, adverse events, medications, vital signs, labs, and other patient-level observations.

For a programmer, the CRF is not just a screenshot of a form. It explains what was intended to be collected, which questions belong together, what units or choices were available, and which values are true site-entered source data.

2. Raw eCRF Export vs Annotated CRF

Item What It Shows Programmer Use
Blank CRF / eCRF The question text, field labels, response options, units, and page layout. Understand the clinical meaning of the data before reading raw variables.
Raw eCRF export Database field names, collected values, audit keys, form repeats, and EDC-driven structure. Inspect actual source variables, missing patterns, units, date components, and repeated records.
Annotated CRF Visual mapping notes that connect CRF fields to SDTM domains and variables. Find where collected fields should flow before writing SDTM mapping code.

3. SDTM MSG 2.0 Visual aCRF Specifications

Before writing code, programmers must learn to read the annotated CRF. MSG 2.0-style visual annotations help reviewers understand source, domain, and variable mapping without immediately opening Define.xml.

What the aCRF Connects

The aCRF maps clinical data collection fields to the corresponding SDTM variables or discrete variable values. It is a reviewer-facing map, not a database extract and not a place for temporary programming notes.

Blank CRF
Question text and choices Raw EDC
Database fields and values aCRF
Visual SDTM annotations SDTM
Submitted domains and variables
How Reviewers Navigate an aCRF PDF
Bookmarks By Visit Screening Baseline Week 4 By Form Demographics Adverse Events Vital Signs
DM = Demographics SC = Subject Characteristics DS = Disposition
Enrollment and Demographics Form
Date of Birth BRTHDTC
Sex Male Female SEX
Race: check all that apply Asian White Other RACE RACE1 in SUPPDM
Informed consent signed? Yes No DSDECOD

A strong aCRF is easy to navigate. It uses bookmarks, clear page-level domain labels, readable annotation boxes, and enough context for a reviewer to understand the mapping without guessing.

AE = Adverse Events SUPPAE = Supplemental AE
Adverse Event Log Form
Adverse event term AETERM
Start date / start time AESTDTC
Severity Mild Moderate Severe AESEV
Action taken with study drug: Other, specify AEACN = OTHERAEACNOTH in SUPPAE
AE row number on screen Repeatable form row Do not annotate AESEQ to CRF
CM = Concomitant Medications
Concomitant Medication Log Form
Medication name CMTRT
Dose / unit / frequency CMDOSECMDOSUCMDOSFRQ
Route Oral IV Other CMROUTE
Fixed medication checklist Aspirin Acetaminophen CMTRT = "ASPIRIN"CMOCCUR
VS = Vital Signs
Vital Signs Form
Systolic blood pressure ____ mmHg VSTESTCD = SYSBP VSORRES
Diastolic blood pressure ____ mmHg VSTESTCD = DIABP VSORRES
Temperature ____ deg C VSTESTCD = TEMP VSORRESVSORRESU
Position Sitting Supine VSPOS
LB = Laboratory Tests
Central Laboratory / Local Pregnancy Test
External source: central lab transfer Check Data Transfer Specification
Was pregnancy test performed? Yes No LBSTAT / LBREASND
Pregnancy result Positive Negative LBTESTCD = HCGLBORRES
Central hematology and chemistry results Not entered on eCRF Origin: central lab transfer
QS = Questionnaires
Daily Symptom Diary
External source: ePRO vendor Not site-entered eCRF data
Pain severity today 0 1 2 3 4 QSTESTCD = PAINSEVQSORRES
Diary completion timestamp Device timestamp QSDTC
Missed diary reason QSSTATQSREASND
PC = Pharmacokinetic Concentrations PP = Pharmacokinetic Parameters
PK Sample Collection Form
Nominal time point Pre-dose 1h 2h PCTPT
Actual collection date / time PCDTC
Concentration result Bioanalytical lab file PCORRES from vendor
Operational Fields Not SDTM Observations
EDC Workflow and Administrative Fields
Form complete status Complete Incomplete [NOT SUBMITTED]
Query opened / query closed timestamp EDC audit trail [NOT SUBMITTED]
Investigator signed date [NOT SUBMITTED]
Trial Design Metadata
What Should Not Be Drawn as a CRF Field
Protocol arm structure Protocol schema TA is not mapped to CRF
Planned elements and epochs Study design metadata TE is not mapped to CRF
Planned visit schedule Schedule of Assessments TV is not mapped to CRF
Subject-level and milestone forms
DM: Date of Birth BRTHDTC

Collected birth dates map to SDTM character dates. Do not impute missing month or day in SDTM.

DM: Sex SEX

Fixed choices must align to controlled terminology such as M, F, U, or UNDIFFERENTIATED.

DM / SUPPDM: Race RACE RACE1 in SUPPDM

A standard race value and extra checked categories are separate mapping concepts.

SC: Reproductive Status SCTESTCD = CHILDPOT SCORRES

Subject characteristics often use test-code plus result annotations.

IE: Inclusion Criteria IETESTCD = I01 IEORRES

Each criterion should remain traceable to the exact protocol criterion.

DS: Informed Consent DSDECOD DSSTDTC

Consent, screen failure, randomization, completion, and discontinuation are usually DS concepts.

DS: Discontinuation DSDECOD DSTERM in SUPPDS

A standard reason may go to DSDECOD; free-text detail may need supplemental handling.

SV: Visit Date SVSTDTC

Visit dates may be collected, while VISITNUM and planned visit structure usually come from metadata.

Events and history logs
AE: Event Term AETERM AESTDTC

AE rows are repeatable. AESEQ is program-assigned and should not be drawn as a CRF annotation.

AE: Severity / Seriousness AESEV AESER

Severity and seriousness are different; keep response choices visible.

AE: Relationship / Action AEREL AEACN

Causality and action taken often drive safety summaries and reviewer questions.

AE: Other Specify AEACN = OTHER AEACNOTH in SUPPAE

The selected category and the free-text specify box are separate targets.

MH: Medical History MHTERM MHSTDTC

Medical history may be MedDRA coded later; the aCRF still shows the SDTM target.

CE: Clinical Event CETERM CESTDTC

Some collected events are clinical events rather than adverse events.

DV: Protocol Deviation DVTERM DVSTDTC

Deviation category, description, date, and source should be easy to inspect.

HO: Healthcare Encounter HOTERM HOSTDTC

Hospitalizations may map to HO when collected as encounters rather than AE attributes.

Interventions, medications, and exposure
CM: Medication Log CMTRT CMSTDTC

CM forms repeat. CMSEQ is assigned in code and is not mapped from the CRF.

CM: Dose / Route CMDOSE CMROUTE

Dose, unit, frequency, route, indication, and dates should not be squeezed into one box.

CM: Fixed Drug Checklist CMTRT = "ASPIRIN" CMOCCUR

Horizontal Yes/No columns can become one SDTM record per checked medication.

EX: Dosing Record EXTRT EXSTDTC

Exposure may come from EDC, IRT, dispensing, or administration records.

EC: Exposure as Collected ECTRT ECSTDTC

EC can preserve collected exposure before final EX derivations.

DA: Drug Accountability DATESTCD = RETURN DAORRES

Returned kit counts or pill counts often use a findings-style structure.

PR: Procedures PRTRT PRSTDTC

Procedures are interventions. Confirm PR versus findings before mapping.

SU: Substance Use SUTRT SUOCCUR

Smoking, alcohol, or caffeine questions may map to SU when collected as exposure history.

Findings pages and value-level patterns
VS: Blood Pressure VSTESTCD = SYSBP VSORRES

Show the test identity and result variable separately, like the sample aCRF style.

VS: Height / Weight VSTESTCD = HEIGHT VSORRES

Units such as cm, kg, lb, or inch are source context and must remain visible.

VS: Temperature VSTESTCD = TEMP VSLOC

If location or method is collected, annotate it separately from the numeric result.

EG: ECG Interval EGTESTCD = QTCF EGORRES

ECG pages often combine interval values, interpretation, method, and date/time.

PE: Physical Exam PETESTCD = SKIN PEORRES

Body-system rows are usually value-level findings, not custom variables.

LB: Hematology Origin: central lab LBTESTCD = HGB

External lab values may not appear on eCRF pages; point to transfer specs and DTS.

LB: Pregnancy Test LBTESTCD = HCG LBORRES

Pregnancy testing can be local CRF data or vendor data. The origin must be clear.

FA: Targeted Finding FATESTCD = LESION FAORRES

FA can hold targeted assessments that do not fit a standard findings domain cleanly.

Questionnaires, ePRO, and patient-reported data
QS: Questionnaire Item QSTESTCD = ITEM01 QSORRES

Do not write SQL-like "where" annotations; show item code and response target separately.

QS: Total Score QSTESTCD = TOTAL QSSTRESN

Calculated scores need derived styling and algorithm documentation in specs.

RS: Response Assessment RSTESTCD = OVRLRESP RSORRES

Response assessments should align with the response criteria used in the study.

ePRO Diary Origin: ePRO vendor QSDTC

The source may be an ePRO transfer, not a site-entered CRF page.

ePRO Compliance QSSTAT QSREASND

Missed diary entries and not-done reasons need consistent mapping rules.

Wearable Device Origin: device transfer FATESTCD = STEPS

High-frequency device data may need external data documentation and reviewer notes.

External data, operations, and non-submitted content
PK Sample Collection PCDTC PCTPT

Collection timing may be CRF-collected while concentration results come from the lab.

Imaging Vendor Origin: imaging transfer TRTESTCD

Tumor measurements may be outside EDC and tied to an imaging charter and transfer spec.

IRT Randomization RANDDT from IRT ARM

Treatment assignment often comes from IRT or randomization metadata, not manual CRF entry.

EDC Control Status [NOT SUBMITTED]

Form complete status, save status, and page lock flags are usually operational metadata.

Query History [NOT SUBMITTED]

Query open, answer, and close timestamps usually belong to EDC audit trails.

Investigator Signature [NOT SUBMITTED]

Signature workflow fields are operational unless study standards require submission.

Skip-Logic Prompt [NOT SUBMITTED]

A pure navigation prompt can be non-submitted only if it has no independent clinical meaning.

Trial Design Domains Do not map TA / TE / TV / TI / TS to CRF

Trial Design domains come from protocol and metadata, not subject-level CRF observations.

Important: Trial Design domains such as TA, TE, TV, TI, and TS are not mapped from CRF fields. They describe the study design from the protocol and metadata package. Do not annotate CRF pages as if these domains were subject-level collected data.

Annotation Patterns Programmers Should Recognize
Annotation Type Pattern Example How to Read It
Domain DOMAIN = Domain Label AE = Adverse Events This CRF page or section maps to AE.
Variable VARNAME AETERM The collected field maps directly to one SDTM variable.
Variable + Value VARNAME = VALUE DSTERM = "RANDOMIZED" The annotation points to a variable and its expected value.
Supplemental QVAL Business rule + SUPP-- destination RACE = MULTIPLE; RACE1, RACE2 in SUPPDM Use a reviewer-readable note for supplemental values, instead of showing raw QNAM/QVAL mechanics as the main CRF annotation.
Value-Level TESTCD note + result variable VSTESTCD = SYSBP VSORRES For repeated findings rows, annotate the test identity and the result field separately so the page stays readable.
Unsubmitted Data [NOT SUBMITTED] [NOT SUBMITTED] The CRF field was collected but intentionally not submitted.
Solid, Dashed, SUPP, and NOT SUBMITTED
Collected Field AESEV

Solid border: the site entered the severity directly on the CRF.

Assigned or Derived USUBJID

Dashed border: do not hunt for a raw field. Look for derivation logic.

Supplemental Data AEACN01 in SUPPAE

The value is clinically useful but not a standard AE variable.

Not Submitted [NOT SUBMITTED]

The field is collected but omitted from SDTM. The reason belongs in SDRG.

Value-Level Mapping VSTESTCD = DIABP VSORRES

Show the test code and result variable as separate annotations, like the sample aCRF.

Bad Annotation --DTC (IEDTC, DMDTC, SVSTDTC...)

Too vague and pseudo-code-like. It does not tell the reviewer the mapping clearly.

What Not To Do
SV = Subject Visits
Visit Form
Visit Date --DTC (IEDTC, SVSTDTC, DMDTC, SCDTC)
Visit Name SCREENING raw_visit_label
Internal page status EDC_STATUS
SV = Subject Visits DM = Demographics
Visit Form
Visit Date SVSTDTC
Visit Name SCREENING VISIT
Internal page status [NOT SUBMITTED]

The aCRF should not display raw database names as the main annotation, use pseudo-code as a shortcut, or leave collected fields unexplained. It should show clean SDTM mapping decisions.

Complex Pages: Repeats, Horizontal Forms, and Other Specify
Log Lines / Repeatable Forms Warning: --SEQ is not a CRF annotation

AE, CM, and MH forms repeat. The aCRF maps collected fields such as AETERM, CMTRT, and MHDECOD. The SDTM program assigns stable --SEQ values.

Horizontal CRF to Vertical SDTM CMTRT = "ASPIRIN"

A CRF column such as Aspirin Yes/No may become one CM row with a fixed treatment value.

Other, Specify AETERM = OTHER Specify text in SUPPAE

The checkbox may drive a main topic value, while the free-text specify field often belongs in SUPP--.

Mapping Fixed CRF Options & Controlled Terminology (CT) Compliance

Fixed Case Report Form (CRF) options are design decisions, not free text. When a page uses checkboxes, radio buttons, or fixed drop-down choices for sex, race, severity, route, frequency, or Yes/No responses, the submitted Study Data Tabulation Model (SDTM) value must follow the study standard and the exact Clinical Data Interchange Standards Consortium (CDISC) Controlled Terminology release assigned to the project.

The rule is simple: preserve the source meaning, but submit the controlled value. Do not recode by personal preference, raw database convenience, prior project habit, or intuition. The reviewer should be able to trace the CRF choice, raw stored value, mapping specification, Define.xml codelist, and submitted SDTM value without seeing a different interpretation at each step.

How to read the implementation guide: when the controlled terminology column shows a highlighted codelist name such as AESEV, NY, ACN, or LOC, it is a lookup instruction. Open the study-approved CDISC CT package and use the official submission value for that codelist. If the IG shows *, it still signals controlled terminology. The allowed values are usually sponsor-defined and must be documented in the study specification.
1. Inspect the CRF Design

Identify fixed selections, displayed text, allowed multiple selections, and the underlying raw database value.

2. Check Spec and CT Version

Find the target variable and codelist name in the SDTM specification, then confirm the exact submission value in the approved CDISC CT release.

3. Document Explicit Logic

Write the mapping rule directly in the specification so production, quality control, Define.xml, and validation checks use the same logic.

ePRO and External Data Are Source Data Too

Not every source record is entered by site staff on the EDC eCRF. Patient diaries, ePRO/eCOA instruments, central labs, ECG vendors, PK labs, imaging vendors, IRT/randomization systems, and wearable devices may all provide source data through external transfers. Programmers should not expect every SDTM variable to be visible on an eCRF page.

Vendor / ePRO Source
Patient or vendor system DTS
File layout and transfer rules Raw Transfer
CSV, SAS, XML, or XPT SDTM Mapping
Domain and variable logic Define.xml Origin
eDT, vendor, or derived
Source Type Common SDTM Domains Programmer Should Check
ePRO / eCOA diary or questionnaire QS, FA, CE, sometimes custom sponsor mapping Instrument version, question text, visit/window, time zone, score derivation, and whether source is appended to aCRF or documented separately.
Central laboratory LB, MB, PC, PP depending on test type Lab units, reference ranges, specimen date/time, LOINC or local test codes, normal/abnormal flags, and reconciliation with EDC collection status.
Central ECG vendor EG and sometimes SUPPEG Triplicate readings, measurement number, QT correction method, interpretation text, date/time precision, and vendor algorithm version.
IRT / randomization or drug supply system DM, DS, EX, TA-related metadata support Randomization date, treatment assignment, kit dispensation, exposure reconciliation, and whether data are subject-level or trial-design metadata.
Imaging, wearable, or device vendor MI, FA, VS, QS, or study-specific mapping Acquisition date/time, device ID, algorithm version, repeated measurements, quality flags, and traceability to vendor source files.

Practical rule: if data are not on the eCRF, do not mark them as missing from the aCRF too quickly. First check the Data Transfer Specification, vendor sample files, Define.xml origin, SDRG explanation, and whether the source pages or instrument screenshots should be appended to the aCRF.

When Is [NOT SUBMITTED] Appropriate?
CRF Field Type Typical Decision Mentor Warning
EDC control fields such as form status, page complete flag, query history, or internal audit labels Often [NOT SUBMITTED] These usually describe the data capture system, not the subject's clinical condition.
Operational signatures or system dates used only for workflow control Often [NOT SUBMITTED] Confirm with study standards and reviewer guide expectations before omitting.
Skip-logic questions with no independent clinical meaning May be [NOT SUBMITTED] Do not assume. If the response supports interpretation, it may need SDTM or SUPP--.
Collected clinical data that is not used in analysis Not automatically [NOT SUBMITTED] If it is valid clinical data, it may still belong in a standard domain or SUPP--.
The aCRF Is a Bridge, Not an Isolated Task
Protocol
Clinical intent CRF
Data collection aCRF
Visual mapping design SDTM Spec
Detailed metadata SAS / R Code
Implementation
