In real projects, the aCRF and SDTM specification are often developed together. Before coding, place the aCRF, SDTM spec, and raw data side by side and cross-check field meaning, domain, variable name, controlled terminology, and derivation notes.

4. FDA-Style PDF and aCRF Quality Checks

A submission aCRF is not only about content. It must also behave like a clean reviewer document. The PDF should be searchable, bookmarked, readable, and easy to inspect page by page.

File identity: the submission file is usually named acrf.pdf. Searchable text: reviewers can search variable names such as AETERM or VSORRES. Bookmarks: navigation works by visit and by form. No obstruction: annotations do not cover CRF question text or response choices. Complete coverage: all collected data points are annotated or marked NOT SUBMITTED. Consistent syntax: annotations follow the same pattern across pages. Domain clarity: every represented domain is clear on the page. No pseudo-code: aCRF notes should be reviewer-readable, not programmer shorthand.
Count It Are all blank input fields, checkboxes, and free-text boxes either annotated or intentionally marked as not submitted?
Search It If you search the PDF for AETERM, AESEV, or VSORRES, does the document take you to the expected CRF page?
Compare It Do the aCRF domain names, variable names, and controlled terms match the SDTM specification and Define.xml metadata?

Reference for this section: Pinnacle 21 Best Practices for Annotated CRFs .

5. What to Check Before SDTM Mapping

  • Form context: Confirm visit, repeat number, assessment name, and whether a record is scheduled, unscheduled, or conditional.
  • Question meaning: Read the CRF prompt and response choices, not only the raw variable name.
  • Data type and units: Check whether results are character, numeric, coded, free text, unit-bearing, or collected as separate date/time components.
  • Annotation type: Distinguish collected variables from assigned or derived variables before searching the raw database.
  • Cross-document consistency: Compare the aCRF against the SDTM specification, Define.xml, and reviewer guide notes. A visually clean aCRF is still wrong if its metadata disagree.

Practice Quiz: AE CRF Mapping

Use the quiz below to check whether the CRF option, controlled terminology lookup, and submitted AE dataset logic are aligned.

Practice Workshop: Vital Signs CRF to SDTM VS

This practice belongs in the eCRF-to-SDTM section because it connects a source document, a CRF page, and the first SDTM mapping decision. Review the Schedule of Assessments below, then use the embedded Vital Signs CRF to decide how the collected fields should flow into SDTM VS.

Protocol Schedule of Assessments
Vital Signs CRF

Use the blank CRF page as the source form. Identify the visit, assessment date, vital signs test names, original results, and units before mapping anything to SDTM.

  1. Identify which visits require systolic and diastolic blood pressure collection.
  2. Find the CRF questions that capture assessment date, time, position, and blood pressure.
  3. Decide which SDTM domain should receive the blood pressure records.
  4. List the key SDTM VS variables needed for the mapping, including original result and unit.
Answer Key
  1. Blood pressure is collected at Visit 1, Visit 2, Visit 3, Visit 4, Visit 5/EOS, and unscheduled Visit X.
  2. Date and time come from Q3 and Q4, subject position from Q5, systolic blood pressure from Q6, and diastolic blood pressure from Q7.
  3. Blood pressure is a Findings assessment, so the SDTM domain is VS.
  4. Typical VS variables include USUBJID, VISIT, VSDTC, VSTESTCD, VSTEST, VSPOS, VSORRES, VSORRESU, and VSSTRESN or VSSTRESC after standardization.
Module 04 Introduction to Study Data Tabulation Model (SDTM) Why SDTM mapping exists, how source data becomes submission-ready, and how SDTM specifications connect raw data to Define.xml.

SDTM Modules: How Submission Data Is Organized

SDTM is where raw clinical chaos becomes standardized regulatory currency. Raw eCRF exports, vendor files, and data management transfers are reshaped into tabulation datasets that reviewers can navigate consistently across studies. Programmers should first learn the major domain families before trying to memorize individual variables.

Why SDTM Mapping Exists

Clinical trials are designed to answer practical questions: is the drug working, are there side effects, and is it safe for subjects to continue in the trial? Those questions can only be answered through clear, comprehensive data analytics. Before any reviewer, statistician, or programmer can trust those analytics, the collected data must first be organized into quality Study Data Tabulation Model (SDTM) datasets.

Raw clinical data rarely arrives in submission-ready form. It may come from electronic Case Report Form (eCRF) exports, vendor transfers, laboratory files, electronic patient-reported outcome sources, device feeds, or manual spreadsheets. Each source may use different variable names, date formats, units, visit labels, coding conventions, and file structures. The work of converting those sources into the SDTM format is called SDTM mapping.

Why the Structure Matters for Submission

Clinical Data Interchange Standards Consortium (CDISC) standards were put in place so regulatory reviewers, including the United States Food and Drug Administration (FDA), can analyze clinical trial data quickly and consistently. The reviewer should not have to guess where demographics, adverse events, vital signs, laboratory results, exposure records, or disposition information are located. SDTM gives submission data a predictable structure.

For clinical trial data to be accepted by regulatory reviewers, the submission must be in the correct format. If the data are poorly structured, the sponsor may lose time and money resolving validation issues, reviewer questions, and traceability gaps. In severe cases, unresolved data structure problems can jeopardize the usefulness of the submission package.

Programming takeaway: SDTM is not just a file format. It is the regulated tabulation layer that lets another person understand what happened to each subject without reading every raw CRF page or vendor spreadsheet.
What SDTM Mapping Requires

Mapping to CDISC SDTM format is not simple, whether the mapping is planned before data collection or performed after data have already been collected. A programmer must connect source fields to SDTM domains, variables, values, controlled terminology, timing rules, and derivation notes. This often means reading the annotated CRF, raw data extracts, Data Transfer Specifications (DTS), SDTM specifications, Define.xml metadata, and reviewer-guide notes together instead of treating any one document as the full answer.

A good SDTM mapping specification is the bridge between source data and programming. It explains the target domain, target variable, source field, condition, derivation, controlled terminology, expected type, expected length, origin, and any special handling needed for partial dates, supplemental qualifiers, split domains, external vendor data, or value-level metadata.

The SDTM Mapping Specification Document

SDTM mapping can be a complicated task, so the team should plan the conversion before programming begins. The SDTM mapping specification document specifies how raw data will be converted into SDTM and is used by both the SDTM programmer and the testing team. Without a clear specification, data can be lost, distorted, or interpreted differently by production and QC programmers.

The specification document can be created manually as follows:
  1. Examine the CRFs and raw data and identify which SDTM domains you need.
  2. Against each SDTM domain, note which raw dataset will provide the input data.
  3. Against each SDTM domain, list all variables and describe how they are to be programmed.

This planning step is where text values, controlled terminology, source fields, target variables, conditions, derivations, and origins are resolved before programming begins.

  1. Determine the SDTM domains needed Review CRFs and protocols. Map out required standard domains, such as DM, AE, and LB.
  2. Note the relevant raw dataset for each Map raw source tables to target domains. Identify multi-source inputs if applicable.
  3. List and describe programming for all variables Specify target variables and origins. Define derivation rules and text values.

A real SDTM specification is often maintained in Excel. The dummy example below shows the kind of information a programmer expects to see for a Demographics (DM) domain. The exact column names may vary by company, but the intent is the same: every SDTM variable needs a source, an origin, and a programming rule. The Programming Derivation column should point the programmer to raw datasets, raw variables, merge keys, conditions, and derivation logic. The Define.xml Spec column should stay concise and reviewer-facing.

Dummy SDTM Specification Example: DM
Dataset Variable Name Variable Label Type Length Controlled Terms / Codelist Origin Programming Derivation Define.xml Spec
Core DM Variables
DM STUDYID Study Identifier Char 12 Assigned Set DM.STUDYID from RAW_STUDY.PROTOCOL_ID. If the study setup table is not provided, assign the protocol constant "DUMMY-101" from the approved study metadata. Assigned study identifier representing the protocol or study under which the subject participated.
DM DOMAIN Domain Abbreviation Char 2 DM Assigned Set DM.DOMAIN = "DM" for every output record. No raw variable is required. Assigned domain abbreviation identifying records as Demographics.
DM USUBJID Unique Subject Identifier Char 30 Derived Derive from DM.STUDYID, RAW_SUBJ.SITEID, and RAW_SUBJ.SUBJID using the company standard delimiter. Confirm the same key is used across all subject-level domains. Derived unique subject identifier constructed to be unique across the study and consistent across all submitted subject-level domains.
DM SUBJID Subject Identifier for the Study Char 20 CRF Map RAW_SUBJ.SUBJID to DM.SUBJID from the Electronic Data Capture (EDC) subject table. Preserve the source value and leading zeros. Collected subject identifier as recorded for the subject within the study.
DM SITEID Study Site Identifier Char 10 CRF / Assigned Map RAW_SUBJ.SITEID to DM.SITEID. If site attributes are stored separately, merge RAW_SITE by RAW_SUBJ.SITEKEY and confirm the site number matches. Study site identifier associated with the subject's enrolling or participating site.
DM AGE Age Num 8 Derived Calculate from RAW_SUBJ.BRTHDTC and DM.RFSTDTC according to the study age rule. Use only valid date components; leave missing if the birth date or reference date is insufficient. Derived age at the study reference start date or other protocol-defined reference date, calculated only when sufficient date information is available.
DM AGEU Age Units Char 5 AGEU Assigned If DM.AGE is populated, set DM.AGEU = "YEARS". If AGE is missing, leave AGEU missing. Assigned unit for age, populated when age is reported.
DM SEX Sex Char 1 SEX: F / M / U CRF Map RAW_SUBJ.SEX to DM.SEX using the study controlled terminology map, for example "Male" or "M" to "M", "Female" or "F" to "F", and unknown to "U". Query unmapped values. Collected sex value standardized to the study's CDISC controlled terminology values.
DM RACE Race Char 40 RACE CRF Map RAW_SUBJ.RACE to DM.RACE using the study controlled terminology map. If RAW_RACE contains multiple selected races, follow the study rule for DM.RACE and create supplemental race qualifiers as specified. Collected race value standardized to the study's CDISC controlled terminology values; supplemental race records may be used when multiple race selections are collected.
DM COUNTRY Country Char 3 ISO 3166-1 alpha-3 Assigned Merge RAW_SITE.COUNTRY to the subject by RAW_SUBJ.SITEID, then convert to ISO 3166-1 alpha-3 using the approved country lookup table. Country of the subject's study site, represented using the standard three-letter country code.
DM RFSTDTC Subject Reference Start Date/Time Char 19 ISO 8601 Derived Derive as the earliest non-missing ISO 8601 date/time from RAW_EX.EXSTDTC for the subject. If no exposure exists, use RAW_RAND.RANDDTC only when the specification defines that fallback. Derived subject reference start date/time in ISO 8601 character format, generally based on the first protocol-defined exposure or study reference event.
DM RFENDTC Subject Reference End Date/Time Char 19 ISO 8601 Derived Derive as the latest non-missing ISO 8601 date/time from RAW_EX.EXENDTC. If no exposure end date exists, use the final study contact date from RAW_DS.DSDTC according to the study rule. Derived subject reference end date/time in ISO 8601 character format, generally based on the last protocol-defined exposure or study reference event.
DM ARMCD Planned Arm Code Char 20 Study-specific Derived Map RAW_RAND.ARMCD to DM.ARMCD. For screen failures identified in RAW_DS.DSDECOD, assign "SCRNFAIL" only if required by the study convention. Planned arm code representing the subject's intended treatment assignment or screen failure category according to the study design.
DM ARM Description of Planned Arm Char 200 Study-specific Derived Map DM.ARMCD to the planned arm label from the trial design lookup or TA metadata. Do not free-type arm names in the program. Planned arm description corresponding to the subject's intended treatment assignment in the study design.
DM ACTARMCD Actual Arm Code Char 20 Study-specific Derived Derive from actual exposure records in RAW_EX.EXTRT, RAW_EX.EXSTDTC, and RAW_EX.EXENDTC using the treatment rules in the specification. Apply study rules for untreated, mis-dosed, or switched subjects. Actual arm code representing the treatment pattern the subject actually received, derived according to protocol and study-specific treatment rules.
DM ACTARM Description of Actual Arm Char 200 Study-specific Derived Map DM.ACTARMCD to the corresponding actual arm label using the same treatment lookup used for ACTARMCD. Actual arm description corresponding to the subject's actual treatment experience.
DM DTHFL Subject Death Flag Char 1 NY: Y Derived Set to "Y" if RAW_DS.DSDECOD indicates death, RAW_AE.AESDTH = "Y", or a separate death source contains a death record for the subject. Otherwise leave missing and reconcile conflicting sources. Derived subject death flag indicating whether the subject is reported as deceased in submitted study data.
Variable Structural Roles and SDTM Variable Ordering

The first SDTM task is not to copy raw columns. It is to decide each variable's clinical role according to the SDTM Implementation Guide (SDTMIG). Once the role is clear, the dataset becomes easier to read, validate, and review. This is also why SDTM variable order has a pattern: variables are not arranged randomly or alphabetically.

Role Plain-English Purpose Examples
Identifier Variables Build the unique key system for the study, subject, domain, and record. STUDYID, DOMAIN, USUBJID, --SEQ, --GRPID, --REFID, --SPID.
Topic Variables Define what each record is mainly about. AETERM in AE; VSTESTCD and VSTEST in VS; EXTRT in EX.
Qualifier Variables Describe the topic record from different angles: result, coded term, severity, method, position, evaluator, location, and other details. VSORRES, VSORRESU, VSSTRESN, AEDECOD, AEBODSYS, AESEV, AESER, VSPOS.
Timing Variables Represent when the event, assessment, intervention, or observation happened. AEDTC, VSDTC, --STDTC, --ENDTC, --DY, --STDY, --ENDY using ISO 8601 where applicable.
Trial Design / Reference Variables Connect subject records to planned study structure or reference timing when applicable. TAETORD, EPOCH, RFSTDTC, RFXSTDTC, RFPENDTC, ARM, ACTARM.
Why Variable Order Matters

In submission datasets, variable order is part of readability. Reviewers expect to see the key identifiers first, then the topic of the record, then the qualifiers that describe it, then the timing variables. This order helps a reviewer scan a dataset without searching across dozens of columns. Company standards and SDTMIG domain models should drive the final order; do not sort variables alphabetically just because it is easy in code.

Ordering Step Typical Position Example in VS Why It Helps
1. Identifiers First STUDYID, DOMAIN, USUBJID, VSSEQ Reviewer can immediately identify the study, subject, domain, and record.
2. Topic After identifiers VSTESTCD, VSTEST Reviewer sees what the row is about, such as SYSBP, DIABP, WEIGHT, or HEIGHT.
3. Qualifiers After topic VSPOS, VSORRES, VSORRESU, VSSTRESC, VSSTRESN, VSSTRESU Reviewer sees the result, unit, position, and standardized values near the test name.
4. Timing Usually near the end of the domain model VISITNUM, VISIT, VSDTC, VSDY Reviewer can determine when the observation occurred and how it relates to the study timeline.

Programmer habit: when creating a final SDTM dataset, apply the approved metadata order from the study specification or define metadata. A dataset can contain the right variables and still feel unreviewable if the columns are out of expected SDTM order.

Why Sponsors Invest in CDISC Standards

Implementing CDISC standards has practical benefits for the submitter. It creates greater efficiency, supports full traceability from the beginning to the end of the clinical research process, improves data quality, enables data sharing, reduces downstream cost, streamlines processes, and allows teams to innovate without inventing a private data language for every study.

EfficiencyTeams spend less time interpreting one-off structures and more time checking clinical meaning.
TraceabilityValues can be followed from source collection to SDTM, ADaM, and final outputs.
Data QualityStandard domains, variables, and controlled terminology make unexpected values easier to detect.
Submission ReadinessReviewers receive familiar datasets that align with Define.xml, reviewer guides, and validation rules.
Module 05 Study the SDTM Domain Families Trial Design, Special Purpose, Interventions, Events, Findings, Relationship, Supplemental Qualifiers, and ISO 8601 date handling.
Study the SDTM Domain Families Use the introduction above as the context for why SDTM mapping matters. Then open each topic below to study one SDTM area at a time.
Topic 1. Trial Design Domains (TDM)>

Trial Design domains describe the planned structure of the study. They are built from the protocol, schedule of assessments, treatment plan, inclusion and exclusion criteria, and study metadata. They are not subject-level CRF records and should not be annotated on CRF pages as if they were collected fields.

TA TE TV TI TS
Domain What It Represents Source
TA Trial Arms: planned treatment arms and study paths. Protocol design, not CRF collection.
TE Trial Elements: planned periods such as screening, treatment, follow-up. Protocol and study design metadata.
TV Trial Visits: planned visit schedule and visit timing. Schedule of Assessments and protocol visit plan.
TI Trial Inclusion/Exclusion Criteria. Protocol eligibility criteria.
TS Trial Summary: study-level characteristics and parameters. Study metadata and submission package.
Protocol-to-TDM Thinking

Trial Design domains are not a copy of operational data. They are a structured description of the planned study design. The programmer must read the protocol like a map, identify the planned study periods, arms, visits, and eligibility rules, then encode those concepts into standard SDTM metadata.

Protocol
Study schema Elements
Planned periods Arms
Allowed paths Visits
Schedule TDM
TA / TE / TV / TI / TS
The Core Idea: Planned Design vs. Subject Reality

Trial Design Model is the planned architecture of the trial. It answers questions such as: What paths can a subject take? What elements make up each path? Which visits are planned? What criteria determine eligibility? What study-level parameters describe the submission? Subject-level datasets then show what actually happened to each subject.

Concept Planned Design Metadata Actual Subject Data Plain-English Difference
Arm TA describes the planned arm path. DM may store assigned arm or actual arm information. TA is what the protocol allows; DM is what happened to a subject.
Element TE defines planned elements such as Screening, Treatment, Washout, Follow-up. SE records the subject's actual entry into elements and dates. TE is the menu of planned periods; SE is the subject's timeline.
Visit TV defines planned visits and timing windows. SV records actual visits, including missed or unscheduled visits. TV is the schedule; SV is attendance history.
Eligibility TI defines the planned inclusion/exclusion criteria text. IE records subject-level answers or failures against criteria. TI is the rule; IE is the subject's result against the rule.
Study Parameters TS stores submission-level study characteristics. Subject datasets usually do not repeat these study-level parameters. TS describes the trial as a whole, not individual observations.
TDM Vocabulary for Programmers
Term Meaning Where It Appears Example
Element A basic planned period of the study. TE and TA Screening, Treatment A, Treatment B, Washout, Safety Follow-up.
Arm A planned path through one or more elements. TA Placebo arm, Drug A arm, Drug A then Drug B crossover sequence.
Epoch A broad phase grouping elements for interpretation. TA/TE and often SE alignment Screening, Treatment, Follow-up.
Transition Rule The rule for entering or leaving an element. TE start and end rules Start treatment after randomization; end follow-up after final safety contact.
Planned Visit A visit expected by the protocol schedule. TV Screening, Day 1, Week 4, End of Treatment, Safety Follow-up.
Eligibility Criterion A protocol rule that determines inclusion or exclusion. TI Age at least 18 years; no prohibited prior therapy.
Domain-by-Domain Build Detail
Domain Main Job Typical Variables or Concepts Programming Source Quality Check
TE Define the planned building blocks of the study. Element code, element name, start rule, end rule, planned duration. Protocol schema, treatment plan, follow-up rules, schedule of assessments. Every element used in TA should be defined clearly in TE.
TA Define each planned arm as an ordered path through elements. Arm code, arm name, element order, element code, epoch. Protocol study design diagram and randomization/treatment sections. Each arm path should be readable without opening subject-level data.
TV Define planned visits and their timing. Visit number, visit name, planned study day or timing window. Schedule of Assessments, visit window table, protocol visit definitions. Visit names and numbering should align with SV and downstream ADaM visit logic.
TI Store the planned inclusion and exclusion criteria. Criterion code, criterion text, inclusion/exclusion category. Protocol eligibility section. Criterion text should match the approved protocol and should not be rewritten casually.
TS Store study-level summary parameters. Study phase, blinding, randomization, therapeutic area, indication, design descriptors. Protocol synopsis, registry metadata, study standards, submission metadata. Use controlled terminology where required; do not invent parameter values casually.
Mini Example: Parallel Two-Arm Study

Suppose the protocol has a screening period, randomization on Day 1, a treatment period, and a safety follow-up visit. The planned study has two treatment arms: Placebo and Drug A. A clean TDM build separates the shared elements from the two arm paths.

Piece Example Records or Values Teaching Point
TE SCRN = Screening; PBO = Placebo Treatment; DRUGA = Drug A Treatment; FU = Safety Follow-up. Elements are reusable building blocks. Screening and Follow-up may be shared by both arms.
TA Placebo arm path: SCRN -> PBO -> FU. Drug A arm path: SCRN -> DRUGA -> FU. TA orders the elements for each arm. It does not say which subject actually followed the path.
TV Screening, Day 1, Week 2, Week 4, End of Treatment, Follow-up. TV comes from the planned visit schedule, not from what any one subject attended.
TI Inclusion 01: subject is at least 18 years old. Exclusion 01: prior prohibited therapy. TI stores the criteria; IE stores each subject's criterion-level result.
TS Randomized, double-blind, parallel-group, Phase 2, therapeutic area and indication values. TS describes the study as submitted, using standard metadata values.
Step What to Extract from the Protocol Where It Usually Goes Programming Warning
1. Identify Elements Distinct planned periods such as Screening, Run-in, Treatment A, Treatment B, Washout, Follow-up. TE Do not create an element just because a site collected a form. Elements describe planned design periods.
2. Identify Epochs High-level study phases used to organize subject experience, such as Screening, Treatment, Follow-up. TE / TA support and SE alignment Epoch is broader than a single visit. It helps connect planned design to actual subject elements.
3. Build Arms Permitted treatment paths, sequences, crossover paths, or add-on therapy paths. TA TA is the planned route. Actual subject movement is represented later through subject-level data such as SE and disposition.
4. Define Visits Visit names, planned timing, visit windows, and whether visits are screening, treatment, or follow-up. TV Do not rely only on raw visit labels. Protocol visit timing and naming must drive TV.
5. Encode Criteria Inclusion and exclusion criteria exactly as used to determine eligibility. TI IE contains subject responses; TI contains the planned eligibility criteria from the protocol.
6. Add Study Parameters Study title, phase, design, blinding, therapeutic area, randomization, and other submission-level descriptors. TS TS is often built from protocol metadata and controlled terminology, not from eCRF rows.
Complex Designs: What Changes?

Simple parallel studies are easier because each arm usually follows one clean path. Complex trials require more careful TDM thinking. The same planned element may appear in multiple arms, and a subject's actual path may differ from the planned arm because of discontinuation, rescue therapy, crossover, re-randomization, or extension phases.

Design Situation What to Read in the Protocol How to Build the TDM How Subject Data Connects Later Common Programming Trap
Crossover study Look for treatment sequences such as A then B, B then A, washout duration, period definitions, and period-specific visits. Define separate TE elements for each treatment period and washout. TA should show the allowed planned paths and the order of elements for each arm. SE later records which elements the subject actually entered and the actual dates. EX records what treatment was actually received. Do not create one generic "Treatment" element if the protocol distinguishes Treatment A, Washout, and Treatment B.
Dose escalation / dose cohort study Find planned cohorts, dose levels, sentinel dosing, dose escalation rules, expansion cohorts, and stopping rules. Represent planned cohort or dose-level paths clearly in TA/TE. TS may describe study design, phase, and dose-escalation characteristics. DM/SE/EX show actual subject assignment and exposure. A subject assigned to a cohort is not the same thing as a new Trial Arm unless the protocol defines it that way. Do not let raw cohort labels drive TA blindly; confirm whether cohort is a design arm, treatment group, analysis group, or operational enrollment label.
Rescue or add-on therapy Find criteria for rescue, add-on treatment, switch treatment, background therapy, or treatment intensification. If the rescue/add-on path is planned by protocol, represent it as a possible element or path. If it is simply medication use, it may remain subject-level CM/EX. Actual rescue use is reflected in EX, CM, SE, DS, or analysis flags depending on study standards. Do not force every subject-level rescue event into Trial Design. TDM describes planned structure, not every event that happened.
Open-label extension Check whether the extension is optional, separate protocol, separate part, rollover, or same study continuation. Create extension elements and visits only if the extension is part of the planned study design being submitted. SE shows whether a subject actually entered the extension; DS may show completion or discontinuation of core and extension periods. Do not mix core-study visits and extension visits without clear naming. Reviewers need to see which period each visit belongs to.
Adaptive or re-randomized design Find decision points, interim criteria, re-randomization rules, response-based assignment, and amendment-driven design changes. Represent planned decision structure as clearly as possible. Use TA/TE/TV consistently and document assumptions in study specifications. Subject-level data show the actual decision outcome, actual element entry dates, and actual treatment received. Do not hide adaptive complexity in vague arm labels like "Treatment Arm 1". The planned paths need reviewer-readable meaning.
Randomized withdrawal Look for an initial open-label or stabilization period followed by randomization to continue treatment or withdraw/placebo. Use TE for run-in/stabilization, randomized treatment, withdrawal/placebo, and follow-up periods. TA should show the planned post-randomization paths. SE connects a subject's actual entry into stabilization and withdrawal elements. DS captures discontinuation or completion events. Do not treat the stabilization period as subject-level noise. If it is planned for all subjects, it belongs in the design structure.
Run-in or screening enrichment Identify placebo run-in, active run-in, washout, baseline stabilization, or enrichment criteria before randomization. TE should include the planned run-in or washout element. TV should include run-in visits and timing windows. IE/DS/SE help show whether subjects passed criteria, entered randomized treatment, or discontinued before randomization. Do not skip the run-in just because it occurs before treatment. It often explains who reached the randomized population.
Multi-part study Look for Part A/Part B, dose finding versus dose expansion, core versus extension, adult versus adolescent parts. Decide whether parts are represented as separate arms, elements, epochs, or TS parameters based on protocol structure. Subject-level assignment may appear in DM, SE, DS, and EX. Analysis datasets may later derive part-specific flags. Do not make every "Part" an arm automatically. Sometimes part is a study-level or cohort-level descriptor.
Basket, umbrella, or substudy design Read how disease cohorts, biomarkers, subprotocols, and treatment assignments are defined. Keep the main study design, substudy paths, and treatment arms clear. TS may need study-level descriptors; TA/TE should reflect planned treatment paths. Subject-level domains show biomarker status, cohort assignment, treatment received, and actual participation. Do not confuse biomarker group, disease cohort, treatment arm, and analysis subgroup. They may be different concepts.
Protocol amendment changes design Check whether amendments added visits, changed treatment duration, opened new arms, closed arms, or changed eligibility. TDM should reflect the submitted study design with clear handling of amendment-driven paths or visit schedules. Subject-level data may show subjects enrolled under different protocol versions, handled through dates, visits, and reviewer explanations. Do not silently overwrite old design assumptions. If the amendment changes metadata, specs, TV, TA, TI, and reviewer notes may all need updates.
Mentor Question Why It Matters Likely Domain Impact
Is this concept planned by the protocol, or did it happen to one subject? This separates Trial Design metadata from subject-level observations. Planned: TA/TE/TV/TI/TS. Actual: SE/SV/DS/EX/AE/CM and other subject domains.
Can two arms share the same element? Many designs reuse Screening, Follow-up, Washout, or Extension elements across arms. TE can define shared elements; TA orders them differently by arm.
Does the visit exist because it was planned, or because the subject had an unscheduled assessment? TV is planned visit metadata; SV contains actual subject visits. TV for planned visits; SV for actual visits including unscheduled visits.
Is the eligibility text the criterion itself, or the subject's answer? Protocol criteria and subject responses are different data concepts. TI for planned criteria; IE for subject-level inclusion/exclusion assessment.
Would a reviewer understand the subject path from TA alone? TA describes the planned path, but SE often explains actual movement through elements. TA/TE for plan; SE for actual element history.

Mentor warning: Trial Design domains are metadata about the planned trial, not subject observations. If a programmer tries to map TA, TE, TV, TI, or TS from a CRF field, pause and check the protocol and SDTM specification.

Topic 2. Special Purpose Domains>

Special Purpose domains hold records that do not fit the ordinary Interventions, Events, or Findings pattern. They often provide the backbone for subject identity, comments, subject-level participation, and study visit structure.

DM CO SE SV
Domain Plain-English Meaning Programmer Focus
DM Demographics: one record per subject. USUBJID, treatment assignment, RFSTDTC, RFENDTC, age, sex, race, country.
CO Comments: free-text comments that need submission context. Do not overuse CO when a standard variable or SUPP-- structure is more appropriate.
SE Subject Elements: actual subject progress through planned trial elements. Connect actual subject dates to TE elements; useful for complex designs.
SV Subject Visits: actual visits and visit-level timing. Distinguish actual subject visits from planned TV metadata.

DM Domain Deep Dive: The Subject-Level Anchor

Demographics (DM) is the subject-level anchor of an SDTM submission. It is usually the first subject-level dataset a reviewer opens because it tells who each subject is within the study, where the subject came from, what planned treatment arm the subject belongs to, what actual treatment arm was received or started, and what reference dates should be used by downstream domains and ADaM.

The most important practical rule is simple: DM should contain one record per subject per study. If a programmer accidentally creates multiple DM rows for one USUBJID, nearly every downstream domain becomes unstable. Merges to AE, LB, EX, VS, DS, and ADaM may duplicate records, counts may be inflated, and treatment or demographic summaries may become unreliable.

Variable Group Typical Variables What the Programmer Should Understand
Study and Subject Identifiers STUDYID, DOMAIN, USUBJID, SUBJID, SITEID These variables create the key used across SDTM and ADaM. USUBJID must be unique and stable across all domains. SUBJID is usually the site-level subject identifier as collected, while USUBJID is the submission-level unique key.
Treatment Assignment ARMCD, ARM, ACTARMCD, ACTARM ARM/ARMCD describe planned treatment assignment, usually from randomization. ACTARM/ACTARMCD describe actual treatment experience at the subject level. These values must align with randomization, exposure, disposition, and protocol rules.
Reference Dates RFSTDTC, RFENDTC, RFXSTDTC, RFXENDTC, RFICDTC, RFPENDTC Reference dates are used repeatedly in analysis. RFSTDTC is commonly the subject reference start date, often first exposure or another protocol-defined anchor. RFXSTDTC/RFXENDTC focus on study treatment exposure. RFICDTC captures informed consent. RFPENDTC is a sponsor-defined end-of-participation reference date; it may be the same as RFXENDTC or it may be later, depending on the approved specification.
Demographic Characteristics BRTHDTC, AGE, AGEU, SEX, RACE, ETHNIC, COUNTRY These variables usually come from the subject-level CRF or site metadata, then are normalized to controlled terminology. AGE should follow the study-defined reference date. COUNTRY is usually the site or subject country using a standard three-letter country code.
Participation and Death DTHDTC, DTHFL Death information may come from disposition, survival follow-up, serious adverse event data, or external reconciliation. If DTHFL is Y, DTHDTC should be traceable and consistent with source records.
RFPENDTC: End of Participation Is Not Always End of Treatment

RFPENDTC is the subject reference end date/time. In plain language, it is used to represent the sponsor-defined end of the subject's participation reference period, when that concept is defined by the protocol and SDTM specification. Programmers often confuse RFPENDTC with RFENDTC or RFXENDTC, but these dates answer different questions and should not be substituted for one another without specification support.

Variable Plain-English Meaning Typical Source Common Mistake
RFPENDTC Sponsor-defined end-of-participation reference date/time. May come from end of study disposition, final follow-up, death, withdrawal, lost to follow-up, study completion, or another rule defined in the specification. Assuming it must always equal last dose, final follow-up, or any one fixed source without reading the spec.
RFENDTC End date/time of the subject reference period used by the study. Study-specific rule defined in the SDTM specification, often based on the end of the subject-level reference period used for study day calculations or other cross-domain timing anchors. Assuming it always equals RFPENDTC, RFXENDTC, last contact, or last observation without reading the sponsor-defined rule.
RFXENDTC End of study treatment exposure. Last non-missing exposure end date/time from EX or treatment administration data. Using it as the study participation end date when follow-up continues after last dose.

A useful rule of thumb is: RFXENDTC is about treatment exposure, while RFPENDTC is about the sponsor-defined participation reference period. In some studies, RFPENDTC may be the same as RFXENDTC because participation is considered complete when treatment exposure ends. In other studies, a subject can stop treatment, enter post-treatment follow-up, complete a safety visit, and only later reach the defined end of participation; in that case, RFPENDTC may be later than RFXENDTC.

How RFPENDTC Is Commonly Derived
  • Completed subject: Use the date/time of study completion or final protocol-defined participation visit when that record is available.
  • Early discontinuation: Use the date/time the subject discontinued from the study, not merely the date treatment stopped, unless the protocol defines those as the same event.
  • Lost to follow-up: Use the last known study contact date only if the specification defines it as the participation end date.
  • Death: If the subject dies during study participation, RFPENDTC may align with DTHDTC or the disposition death date, depending on the study rule.
  • Same as treatment end: If the sponsor defines the participation reference end as the last study treatment date/time, RFPENDTC may equal RFXENDTC.
  • Ongoing participation: If participation has not ended at data cutoff, follow the specification. RFPENDTC may be missing or set to a cutoff-related date only when explicitly defined.

Mini Case: Treatment Ends Before Study Participation Ends

A subject receives the last dose on 2026-04-10, completes an end-of-treatment visit on 2026-04-12, enters 30-day safety follow-up, and completes the final follow-up phone call on 2026-05-10. The programmer should not automatically set RFPENDTC to 2026-04-10 or 2026-05-10 by personal judgment. If the sponsor defines participation through final safety follow-up, RFXENDTC may be 2026-04-10 while RFPENDTC may be 2026-05-10. If the sponsor defines the participation reference end as treatment end, RFPENDTC may instead equal RFXENDTC.

RFPENDTC QC Checks
Check Why It Matters Example Query
RFPENDTC should not precede RFSTDTC Participation cannot end before the study reference start. RFPENDTC = 2026-02-01 but RFSTDTC = 2026-02-03.
RFPENDTC versus RFXENDTC RFPENDTC may equal RFXENDTC or may be later, depending on the sponsor-defined rule. RFPENDTC equals last dose for all subjects even though the specification defines participation through follow-up visits.
Consistency with DS Disposition records usually explain completion, withdrawal, lost to follow-up, or death. DS shows study completion on 2026-05-10 but RFPENDTC is missing.
Cutoff handling Ongoing subjects at data cutoff need consistent rules. Some ongoing subjects use cutoff date while others leave RFPENDTC missing without specification support.
DM and DC: When One Person Participates More Than Once

Most beginner examples assume that one person participates in one study only once. In real clinical research, that is not always true. A person may screen fail and later rescreen, participate in a rollover or extension study, enter more than one protocol in a platform program, or contribute records to a multi-part trial where each participation must remain traceable. In those situations, the Demographics (DM) domain alone may not be enough to explain the relationship between the person and each study participation.

The Demographics Contact (DC) concept is used to support this problem: it connects subject-level study participation records back to the same underlying individual when multiple participations exist. A simple way to teach the distinction is: DM describes the subject's participation in a specific study context, while DC helps describe the person-level continuity across participations.

Question DM Answer DC Answer
Who is this subject in this submitted study? USUBJID, SUBJID, SITEID, ARM, ACTARM, RFSTDTC, demographic attributes. Not the primary place to describe the study participation record.
Is this the same person as another participation? Usually not enough, because USUBJID is study-specific and should remain unique to the participation. Provides a person-level link or contact-level identifier so related participations can be understood.
How do we avoid collapsing separate participations into one DM row? Keep one DM record per USUBJID for each study participation. Use DC to document the relationship across participations instead of forcing one USUBJID to represent multiple entries.
What should downstream programmers remember? DM is still the anchor for merges within that study participation. DC is useful for cross-participation review, de-duplication logic, rollover interpretation, and reviewer explanation.
DC to DM Example: Multiple Participations, One Submitted DM Record Illustrative XPT View

In this example, the same person appears in DC three times: two earlier screening attempts and one later treated participation. DC preserves the participation history, while DM carries the submitted subject-level record used as the anchor for analysis and downstream merges.

dc.xpt

USUBJID SUBJID RFICDTC RFSTDTC BRTHDTC SEX ARM ARMNRS
02356-003-004 003-004 2024-03-25 1997-08-25 M SCREEN
FAILURE
02356-003-004 003-011 2024-07-12 SCREEN
FAILURE
02356-003-004 003-027 2025-02-07 2025-03-10 TRT A

dm.xpt

USUBJID SUBJID RFICDTC RFSTDTC BRTHDTC SEX ARM ARMNRS
02356-003-004 003-004 2025-02-07 2025-03-10 1997-08-25 M TRT A

Teaching point: DC can show multiple contact or participation records for the same person. DM should still remain one submitted subject-level record. The final DM values may come from different DC rows depending on the study's rescreening rule, the accepted participation, and the approved SDTM specification.

Mini Case: Screen Failure and Rescreening

A patient signs consent at Site 01 as subject 001-014, fails screening due to a temporary lab abnormality, and later rescreens as subject 001-027. The second screening attempt leads to randomization and treatment. From a study participation perspective, these may be two different subject records with different SUBJID or USUBJID values. From a person-level perspective, they are the same individual.

Record DM Representation DC / Person-Level Interpretation Programming Risk
First screening attempt USUBJID = DUMMY-101-001-014; ARM may be Not Assigned; disposition shows screen failure. Linked to the same person-level contact as the later successful participation. Do not overwrite this record with the later participation just because the person is the same.
Second screening attempt USUBJID = DUMMY-101-001-027; ARM is populated after randomization; exposure records may exist. Linked to the same person-level contact as the earlier screen failure. Do not merge both attempts into one DM row unless the protocol and SDTM specification explicitly require that structure.

The practical lesson is that a repeated person is not automatically a duplicate subject. The programmer must follow the protocol, SDTM specification, rescreening rules, and sponsor standard for whether the repeated attempt becomes a new USUBJID and whether DC is used to preserve the person-level relationship.

Programming Rules for DM/DC Situations
  • Do not reuse USUBJID casually: USUBJID should remain unique and stable for the submitted subject participation. A repeated person may need a different USUBJID if the protocol treats the attempts as separate participations.
  • Do not solve person matching by free text: Names, initials, birth dates, or other direct identifiers should not be used casually in programming datasets. Use approved pseudonymized linkage fields or the study's defined DC structure.
  • Keep DM one-record-per-participation: DM should not become a many-row person-history table. If multiple participations need to be explained, use the designated relationship structure.
  • Document rescreening and rollover logic: The specification should say how repeated screening, extension studies, rollover studies, and re-enrollment are represented.
  • Check downstream impact: ADaM ADSL, population flags, exposure summaries, disposition counts, and safety summaries may all need to distinguish subject-level participation from person-level duplication.
DM/DC QC Checks
Check Why It Matters Example
USUBJID uniqueness in DM Each participation represented in DM should still be uniquely keyed. A screen failure and rescreened subject accidentally share one USUBJID.
Person-level link consistency Related participations should link consistently when DC or a sponsor-defined linkage is used. Two rescreening attempts have different person-link IDs even though the source system identifies them as the same person.
Participation status clarity Reviewers need to understand which participation was randomized, treated, discontinued, or screen failed. Both attempts show randomized dates, but only the second attempt has exposure records.
Privacy boundary Cross-participation linkage must not expose direct identifiers. A programmer tries to use patient name or full birth date as the linkage key in a deliverable dataset.
Common Raw Sources for DM

DM is rarely built from one raw table. A realistic DM program often merges several subject-level and event-level sources, then applies a hierarchy from the SDTM specification.

Source Typical Contribution to DM Programming Watch Point
Subject / Enrollment Table SUBJID, SITEID, BRTHDTC, SEX, RACE, ETHNIC, consent status. Preserve collected identifiers and leading zeros. Do not invent a new SUBJID when the site identifier already exists.
Randomization / IRT ARMCD, ARM, randomization date, planned treatment assignment. Confirm whether randomized but never treated subjects should still have ARM populated according to the specification.
Exposure / Treatment Records RFXSTDTC, RFXENDTC, sometimes RFSTDTC/RFENDTC and ACTARM. First dose and last dose derivations must handle partial dates, multiple periods, treatment interruptions, and records marked not done.
Disposition / End of Study RFPENDTC, completion/discontinuation context, death date if collected there. End of study and end of treatment are not the same thing. Keep DS logic separate from EX logic.
Site / Country Metadata COUNTRY and sometimes site-level region variables. Use an approved lookup table for ISO country codes and reconcile site IDs before merge.
How to Think About Key DM Derivations
  • USUBJID: Build using the company standard, commonly STUDYID + site + subject identifier. Once created, USUBJID must match exactly across every domain.
  • AGE: Calculate age using the reference date defined in the specification, not by personal preference. If the birth date is partial or the reference date is insufficient, follow the missing or imputation rule in the spec.
  • ARM and ACTARM: Do not assume they are always identical. A subject may be randomized to one arm but receive no treatment, incorrect treatment, partial treatment, or crossover treatment in complex designs.
  • RFSTDTC: Read the study definition carefully. In many treatment studies it is first exposure date/time, but some studies may define a different reference start.
  • RACE and ETHNIC: Use study-controlled terminology. If multiple races are collected, do not force them into a single value without following the study's DM/SUPPDM rule.

Mini Case: Why One DM Record Matters

A subject has two rows in the raw enrollment export because the site corrected the birth date after query resolution. If the programmer simply sorts and keeps both records, DM will contain duplicate USUBJID values. When AE is merged to DM, every adverse event for that subject may double.

  1. Check the raw audit trail or latest-record flag to identify the final approved enrollment record.
  2. Confirm whether the corrected date changes AGE and AGEU.
  3. Ensure only one DM record is output for the subject.
  4. Run duplicate-key checks on STUDYID and USUBJID before sending DM to QC.
DM QC Checks Programmers Should Run
Check Why It Matters Example Finding
One record per USUBJID DM is the subject-level anchor. Duplicates create downstream duplication. USUBJID DUMMY-101-001-003 appears twice due to multiple raw enrollment rows.
USUBJID consistency across domains All subject-level records must join back to DM. AE contains a subject ID not present in DM because site ID was padded differently.
Controlled terminology Sex, race, ethnicity, arm codes, and country codes must follow the study standard. Raw value "Male" was submitted as "Male" instead of standardized value "M".
Date hierarchy Reference dates drive study day and analysis timing. RFSTDTC was taken from randomization even though the spec defines first dose as the reference start.
ARM versus exposure reconciliation Randomization and actual treatment must be explainable. Subject randomized to Active but EX shows placebo kit dispensed; ACTARM needs review.

SE Domain Deep Dive: The Subject's Actual Path Through the Study

Subject Elements (SE) is the SDTM domain that records how a subject actually moved through the planned trial elements. If DM tells the reviewer who the subject is, SE tells the reviewer where the subject was in the study design over time. This is especially important for screening, treatment periods, washout, follow-up, crossover studies, dose escalation, rescue therapy, open-label extension, and any design where the actual subject path may not be obvious from DM alone.

A clinical programmer should think of SE as the subject-level bridge between the planned Trial Design Model and the subject's real timeline. Trial Elements (TE) defines the planned building blocks, Trial Arms (TA) describes the planned sequence of elements for each arm, and SE records the subject's actual start and end dates for those elements. SE is not usually mapped from one CRF page. It is commonly derived from a combination of informed consent, randomization, exposure, disposition, visit, and protocol-defined milestone records.

Question SE Answer Related Domain or Document
Which planned study element did the subject enter? ELEMENT and ETCD identify the actual element, such as SCREEN, RUN-IN, TREATMENT, WASHOUT, or FOLLOW-UP. TE defines the planned element names and codes.
When did the subject enter and leave that element? SESTDTC and SEENDTC store the actual start and end date/time for the element. Derived from DS, EX, SV, consent, randomization, or protocol milestone records.
Which broader study period does the element belong to? EPOCH connects the element to a larger planned phase, such as SCREENING, TREATMENT, WASHOUT, or FOLLOW-UP. TA and protocol schedule of events define the planned epoch structure.
Was the subject's actual path different from the planned arm? SE can show shortened, skipped, repeated, or early-ended elements when the specification defines how to represent them. DM, DS, EX, SV, protocol deviations, and reviewer-guide explanations may all be needed.
Before Building SE: Read TE and TA First

A common mistake is to build SE directly from a few raw dates and then try to label the intervals afterward. A stronger workflow starts from the planned trial design. Trial Elements (TE) defines the planned building blocks and their start/end rules. Trial Arms (TA) defines the planned sequence of those elements for each arm. Only after those rules are clear should the programmer derive the subject-level SE records.

For example, in a crossover design, the planned elements might include Screening, Treatment A, Washout, Treatment B, and Follow-up. TA describes whether an arm follows Sequence AB or Sequence BA. SE then records what actually happened to each subject: whether the subject entered screening, started period 1, crossed over, discontinued, or completed follow-up.

Planned Design Inputs Before SE TE and TA XPT Views

TE defines the planned elements and their boundary rules. TA defines the planned order of those elements for each arm. SE should be derived from a subject's actual milestones using these planned design inputs.

te.xpt

STUDYID DOMAIN ETCD ELEMENT TESTRL TEENRL
DUMMY-101 TE SCRN Screening Informed consent or first screening procedure Day before first treatment element starts
DUMMY-101 TE TRTA Treatment A First exposure to Treatment A Last exposure to Treatment A or day before washout
DUMMY-101 TE WASH Washout Day after period 1 treatment ends Day before period 2 treatment starts
DUMMY-101 TE TRTB Treatment B First exposure to Treatment B Last exposure to Treatment B or transition to follow-up
DUMMY-101 TE FU Follow-up Day after treatment or washout ends Final follow-up, withdrawal, death, or cutoff rule

ta.xpt

STUDYID DOMAIN ARMCD ARM TAETORD ETCD ELEMENT EPOCH
DUMMY-101TASEQABSequence AB1SCRNScreeningSCREENING
DUMMY-101TASEQABSequence AB2TRTATreatment ATREATMENT 1
DUMMY-101TASEQABSequence AB3WASHWashoutWASHOUT
DUMMY-101TASEQABSequence AB4TRTBTreatment BTREATMENT 2
DUMMY-101TASEQABSequence AB5FUFollow-upFOLLOW-UP
DUMMY-101TASEQBASequence BA1SCRNScreeningSCREENING
DUMMY-101TASEQBASequence BA2TRTBTreatment BTREATMENT 1
DUMMY-101TASEQBASequence BA3WASHWashoutWASHOUT
DUMMY-101TASEQBASequence BA4TRTATreatment ATREATMENT 2
DUMMY-101TASEQBASequence BA5FUFollow-upFOLLOW-UP

se.xpt

USUBJID SESEQ ETCD ELEMENT SESTDTC SEENDTC
DUMMY-101-001 1 SCRN Screening 2026-01-10 2026-02-02
DUMMY-101-001 2 TRTA Treatment A 2026-02-03 2026-03-02
DUMMY-101-001 3 WASH Washout 2026-03-03 2026-03-16
DUMMY-101-001 4 TRTB Treatment B 2026-03-17 2026-04-14
DUMMY-101-001 5 FU Follow-up 2026-04-15 2026-05-12

Teaching point: TA is the planned sequence; SE is not a copy of every TA row for every subject. In this example, the subject followed Sequence AB, so SE contains the actual entered elements in that order: SCRN, TRTA, WASH, TRTB, and FU.

Subject-Level Examples: How Actual Paths Become SE

The most useful way to teach SE is through subject paths. The same planned design can produce very different SE records depending on whether the subject screens out, discontinues early, skips a period, or completes the full sequence.

Subject Scenario Actual Milestones Expected SE Pattern Programming Risk
Screen failure Consent collected; eligibility fails; no randomization and no exposure. One SCRN record, ending at screen failure or screening disposition date according to the spec. Creating treatment or follow-up records just because the planned arm has those elements.
Randomized but never treated Randomization exists; first dose is missing; subject withdraws before treatment. SCRN plus possibly a short randomized/pre-treatment element if defined; no treatment element unless the spec defines one. Using randomization date as treatment start without exposure evidence or specification support.
Completes crossover A then B Starts A, completes A, completes washout, starts B, completes B, enters follow-up. SCRN, TRTA, WASH, TRTB, FU in chronological order. Losing the washout interval or labeling both treatment periods with a generic TREATMENT element when TE/TA define separate elements.
Discontinues after period 1 Starts A, stops A early, never enters washout or period 2, has final contact. SCRN and TRTA, then FU only if follow-up is defined and occurred. Forcing the full planned TA sequence into SE even though the subject never entered later elements.
Extension or rollover Completes core treatment and enters a long-term extension period. Core elements followed by extension or follow-up elements, if defined in TE/TA or sponsor standard. Hiding extension participation inside a single treatment element, making reviewer interpretation difficult.
Core SE Variables
Variable Plain-English Meaning Programming Focus
STUDYID, DOMAIN, USUBJID Standard identifiers. USUBJID must match DM exactly. DOMAIN is "SE" for all records.
SESEQ Sequence number for subject element records. Assign in chronological element order within subject. Do not map directly from CRF unless the specification explicitly says so.
ETCD Element code. Should align to TE. Example: SCRN, RUNIN, TRT, WASH, FU. Use sponsor standard element codes.
ELEMENT Element name. Human-readable planned element name corresponding to ETCD. Keep consistent with TE.
SESTDTC Actual start date/time of the subject's element. Derived from the earliest protocol-defined date/time for entering the element.
SEENDTC Actual end date/time of the subject's element. Derived from the next element start, disposition milestone, visit date, exposure stop, or other specification-defined boundary.
EPOCH Planned study period. Connects the actual SE record to the broader planned period. Must be consistent with TA/TE and downstream domain EPOCH derivations.
Common Raw Sources for SE

SE often feels difficult because it is not collected as "SE" in the Electronic Data Capture system. The programmer usually builds it by interpreting multiple operational milestones.

Source Typical Contribution to SE Programming Watch Point
Informed Consent / Enrollment Start of screening or pre-treatment period. Confirm whether screening starts at consent, first screening procedure, or another protocol-defined date.
Randomization / IRT May define transition from screening or run-in to randomized treatment. Randomization is not always treatment start. Check whether the treatment element starts at randomization or first dose.
Exposure / Administration First and last treatment dates for treatment elements. Handle treatment interruptions, multiple periods, crossover treatment, and records marked not done.
Disposition End of study, early discontinuation, completion, lost to follow-up, death, or follow-up completion. Do not treat end of treatment and end of study as the same milestone unless the specification defines them that way.
Subject Visits Visit dates may support element boundaries when exposure or disposition records are incomplete. Visit dates need careful window and missing-date handling; they should not override the specified hierarchy casually.
Protocol Deviations / Operational Data May explain skipped, repeated, or unexpected elements. SE should represent the planned element framework; unusual actual behavior may also need DS, DV, or reviewer-guide explanation.
How to Think About SE Derivations
  • Start with the planned design: Read TE and TA first. SE records should use element codes and names that come from the planned Trial Design Model, not new labels invented inside the program.
  • Build a subject timeline: Collect candidate milestone dates for each subject, sort them chronologically, then assign element intervals according to the specification.
  • Do not create overlapping elements unless specified: For most designs, a subject should not be in two mutually exclusive planned elements at the same time.
  • Handle gaps deliberately: If one element ends before the next begins, determine whether the gap is allowed, should be absorbed into an adjacent element, or indicates missing data.
  • Keep EPOCH consistent: EPOCH in findings, events, interventions, and ADaM often depends on the same planned timing logic. SE can help explain why a record belongs to SCREENING, TREATMENT, WASHOUT, or FOLLOW-UP.
  • Document assumptions: If the study design has rescue therapy, re-randomization, crossover, dose escalation, or extension periods, document exactly how those paths are represented in SE.
A Practical SE Build Pattern

For production work, the cleanest SE programs usually separate the work into three layers: collect milestone dates, convert milestones into planned element starts, then derive element ends from the next element start or from a sponsor-defined terminal event. This keeps the logic reviewable and makes QC easier.

Build Step What the Programmer Does Example
1. Gather candidate dates Create a subject-level milestone table from consent, randomization, exposure, disposition, visit, and follow-up sources. CONSENTDTC, RANDDTC, first dose date, washout start, period 2 first dose, study completion date.
2. Assign element starts For each candidate milestone, assign ETCD, ELEMENT, and EPOCH according to TE/TA and the mapping specification. First dose of Drug A becomes ETCD = TRTA, ELEMENT = Treatment A, EPOCH = TREATMENT 1.
3. Sort within subject Order planned element starts chronologically and assign SESEQ. SCRN first, then TRTA, then WASH, then TRTB, then FU.
4. Derive element ends Use the day before or time before the next element starts when the specification defines adjacent intervals; otherwise use the explicit terminal milestone. Screening end is day before treatment start; final follow-up end is the final contact date.
5. Reconcile exceptions Review screen failures, randomized-not-treated subjects, missing exposure dates, discontinuations, deaths, and protocol-specific special paths. A screen failure should not be forced into treatment elements just because the assigned arm has them.
SAS Pattern for SE Boundary Thinking SE 
/* Training pattern only: derive SEENDTC from the next element start.
   Production code must follow the study-specific TE/TA and SDTM spec. */

proc sort data=se_starts;
  by studyid usubjid sestdtc seseq;
run;

data se_ready;
  set se_starts;
  by studyid usubjid;

  length seendtc $19;
  next_start = lead_sestdtc;  /* created in prior SQL/data step or by self-merge */

  if not missing(next_start) then do;
    /* If dates are date-only and adjacent periods are defined, use the day
       before next_start. If date-times are used, follow the time rule in spec. */
    seendtc = derive_end_before_next_start(next_start);
  end;
  else do;
    /* Last element usually comes from study completion, discontinuation,
       death, lost-to-follow-up, cutoff, or another sponsor-defined rule. */
    seendtc = terminal_element_end;
  end;
run;
Where SE Usually Goes Wrong
Pitfall Why It Happens Better Practice
Building SE from visits only Visit dates are easy to access, but they may not represent element boundaries. Use the hierarchy in the specification; exposure, randomization, disposition, or consent may be more appropriate.
Creating the full planned arm for every subject Programmer copies TA instead of deriving actual subject progress. Only create elements the subject actually entered, unless the spec defines planned-but-not-entered records.
Ignoring TE start and end rules ETCD/ELEMENT labels are copied, but the boundary logic is improvised. Document start and end rules before coding; QC should compare SE against TE expectations.
Overlapping mutually exclusive elements Multiple raw sources produce competing dates. Use a deterministic hierarchy and query conflicting source dates before finalizing SE.
Missing screen failure logic Screen failures have no exposure and often no randomization record. Represent screening only, with an end date based on disposition or the specified screening-end milestone.
Weak traceability to source SE is derived from many inputs, so reviewers cannot see why a boundary was chosen. Keep intermediate milestone datasets and document source priority in the SDTM specification and reviewer's guide when needed.

Mini Case: Early Treatment Discontinuation With Follow-up

A subject signs consent on 2026-01-10, starts treatment on 2026-02-03, stops treatment early on 2026-04-10 due to an adverse event, and completes the final safety follow-up on 2026-05-10. A programmer may be tempted to create only one record from first dose to final follow-up. That hides the difference between treatment exposure and post-treatment follow-up.

  1. Use the specification to determine whether screening starts at consent or another screening milestone.
  2. Create a treatment element that ends at the treatment-end boundary, commonly last exposure or the day defined by the spec.
  3. Create a follow-up element if the protocol defines post-treatment follow-up as a separate planned element.
  4. Check that DS explains early treatment discontinuation or study discontinuation as applicable.
  5. Confirm that DM reference dates and SE element dates do not contradict one another.
SE QC Checks Programmers Should Run
Check Why It Matters Example Finding
One coherent timeline per subject SE should describe the subject's actual path through planned elements. Subject has two treatment element records with overlapping dates and no crossover rule.
ETCD/ELEMENT consistency with TE SE should use planned element codes and labels. SE uses "TRT1" but TE defines the treatment element as "TRT".
SESTDTC and SEENDTC order Element end should not precede element start. SEENDTC is earlier than SESTDTC due to using randomization as end and first dose as start.
Boundary consistency with EX and DS Treatment and follow-up elements must be explainable from exposure and disposition milestones. SE treatment ends after final exposure, but DS says the subject discontinued treatment earlier.
EPOCH alignment Downstream domains and ADaM may rely on EPOCH assignment. AE after last dose is assigned TREATMENT in AE but FOLLOW-UP in SE without a documented rule.
Missing boundary rules Partial or missing dates can make element construction unstable. Subjects with missing last dose date receive arbitrary SEENDTC values not supported by the specification.

Subject Visits in SDTM: Planned Visits vs. What Actually Happened

Subject Visits (SV) is the SDTM domain that describes visit-level observations for each subject. In current submission practice, SV is not limited to visits that physically occurred at the site. SV can represent planned visits, actual visits, missed planned visits, unscheduled visits, remote contacts, telephone contacts, and protocol-defined changes in how a visit was conducted. This makes SV the subject-level counterpart to the planned Trial Visits (TV) domain.

The practical difference is simple: TV says what visits were planned in the study design; SV says what happened, or was expected to happen, for a specific subject. A programmer should not build SV by copying TV for every subject, and should not build SV only from forms that were completed. The SDTM specification must define which planned visits should appear in SV, how missed visits are represented, how unscheduled visits are named, and how contact mode is captured.

Question TV Answer SV Answer
What visits does the protocol plan? TV contains the planned visit schedule and expected timing. SV does not define the plan; it applies the plan to each subject.
Did the subject have the planned visit? TV cannot answer subject-level occurrence. SVPRESP and SVOCCUR can show whether a planned visit was expected and whether it occurred.
Was the visit done in person, by phone, or remotely? TV may describe planned visit type, but not necessarily what happened for a subject. SVCNTMOD can describe the contact mode used for the subject visit.
Was the visit impacted by an epidemic or pandemic public health emergency? TV usually remains the planned design metadata and does not explain subject-level disruption. SVEPCHGI can indicate that the visit was impacted by an epidemic or pandemic-related public health emergency.
Core SV Variables
Variable Plain-English Meaning Programming Focus
STUDYID, DOMAIN, USUBJID Standard identifiers. USUBJID must match DM exactly. DOMAIN is "SV" for all records.
VISITNUM, VISIT Visit number and visit name. Use the planned TV naming and numbering conventions for scheduled visits. Unscheduled visits need study-standard numbering rules.
VISITDY Planned study day for the visit. Usually comes from TV for scheduled visits. Do not derive from the actual visit date.
SVSTDTC, SVENDTC Start and end date/time of the visit or visit contact. Use actual contact dates when a visit occurs. For missed planned visits, follow the study-specific rule for whether dates are missing or populated.
SVSTDY, SVENDY Study day of the visit start and end. Derive relative to RFSTDTC using the standard study-day rule. Do not derive if SVSTDTC/SVENDTC is missing.
SVPRESP Was this visit pre-specified? Set to "Y" for planned visits. Usually missing for unscheduled visits.
SVOCCUR Did the visit occur? For planned visits, "Y" means it occurred and "N" means it did not occur. Usually missing for unscheduled visits because unscheduled records exist only when they occur.
SVREASOC Reason the visit did not occur. Populate when SVOCCUR = "N" and a reason is available or required by the specification.
SVCNTMOD Contact mode. Describes how the visit/contact occurred, such as in person, telephone, video, home health, or other study-defined modes.
SVEPCHGI Epi/Pandemic Related Change Indicator. Use to flag visit deviations caused by public health emergencies, such as epidemic or pandemic-related changes to the planned visit conduct.
SV Example: Planned Visits, Disposition, and Actual Visit Records TV + DS + SV

The SV example should be read together with other domains. TV defines the planned visit schedule, DS explains subject-level milestones and study participation outcomes, and SV records what actually happened for each subject visit. This example includes a screen failure, a completed subject, a subject who withdrew, missed planned visits, and visits changed to remote contact during a pandemic disruption.

tv.xpt

Row STUDYID DOMAIN VISITNUM VISIT VISITDY TVSTRL TVENRL
1 123456 TV 1 SCREEN Start of Screening Epoch Up to 7 days after start of the Screening Epoch
2 123456 TV 2 DAY 1 1 On the day of, but before, the end of the Screen Epoch On the day of, but after, the start of the Treatment Epoch
3 123456 TV 3 WEEK 1 8 1 week after the start of the Treatment Epoch
4 123456 TV 4 WEEK 2 15 2 weeks after the start of the Treatment Epoch
5 123456 TV 5 WEEK 4 29 4 weeks after the start of the Treatment Epoch
6 123456 TV 6 WEEK 6 43 6 weeks after the start of the Treatment Epoch
7 123456 TV 7 WEEK 8 57 8 weeks after the start of the Treatment Epoch
8 123456 TV 8 FOLLOW-UP The earlier of 14 days after last dose and 10 weeks after start of Treatment Epoch At Trial Exit

ds.xpt

Row STUDYID DOMAIN USUBJID DSSEQ DSTERM DSDECOD DSCAT DSSCAT EPOCH DSDTC DSSSTDTC DSSTDY
1 123456 DS 37 1 INFORMED CONSENT OBTAINED INFORMED CONSENT OBTAINED PROTOCOL MILESTONE SCREENING 2019-09-10 2019-09-10
2 123456 DS 37 2 SCREEN FAILURE SCREEN FAILURE DISPOSITION EVENT STUDY PARTICIPATION SCREENING 2019-09-16 2019-09-16
3 123456 DS 85 1 INFORMED CONSENT OBTAINED INFORMED CONSENT OBTAINED PROTOCOL MILESTONE SCREENING 2019-12-13 2019-12-13 -6
4 123456 DS 85 2 COMPLETED COMPLETED DISPOSITION EVENT STUDY PARTICIPATION TREATMENT 2020-02-27 2020-02-27 72
5 123456 DS 101 1 INFORMED CONSENT OBTAINED INFORMED CONSENT OBTAINED PROTOCOL MILESTONE SCREENING 2020-02-13 2020-02-13 -6
6 123456 DS 101 2 WITHDRAWAL BY SUBJECT WITHDRAWAL BY SUBJECT DISPOSITION EVENT STUDY PARTICIPATION TREATMENT 2020-03-16 2020-03-16 26

sv.xpt

Row STUDYID DOMAIN USUBJID VISITNUM VISIT SVPRESP SVOCCUR SVREASOC SVCNTMOD SVEPCHGI VISITDY SVSTDTC SVENDTC SVSTDY SVENDY SVUPDES
1123456SV371SCREENYYIN PERSON2019-09-102019-09-16
2123456SV851SCREENYYIN PERSON2019-12-132019-12-18-6-1
3123456SV852DAY 1YYIN PERSON12019-12-192019-12-1911
4123456SV853WEEK 1YNSUBJECT LACKED TRANSPORTATION8
5123456SV854WEEK 2YYIN PERSON152020-01-022020-01-021515
6123456SV855WEEK 4YYIN PERSON292020-01-162020-01-163030
7123456SV856WEEK 6YYIN PERSON432020-01-302020-01-304343
8123456SV857WEEK 8YYIN PERSON572020-02-132020-02-135757
9123456SV858FOLLOW-UPYYIN PERSON2020-02-272020-02-277272
10123456SV1011SCREENYYIN PERSON2020-02-132020-02-18-6-1
11123456SV1012DAY 1YYIN PERSON12020-02-192020-02-1911
12123456SV1013WEEK 1YYIN PERSON82020-02-252020-02-2577
13123456SV1014WEEK 2YNCLINIC CLOSED DUE TO BAD WEATHER15
14123456SV1014.1REMOTE AUDIO VIDEOY2020-03-072020-03-071818EVALUATION OF AE
15123456SV1018FOLLOW-UPYYTELEPHONE CALLY2020-03-162020-03-162626

Teaching point: TV supplies the planned schedule, DS explains whether a subject was a screen failure, completed, or withdrew, and SV translates that context into subject-level visit records. For missed scheduled visits, SVPRESP can remain "Y" while SVOCCUR is "N" and SVREASOC explains why. For changed visit conduct, SVCNTMOD records how the contact occurred and SVEPCHGI can mark epidemic or pandemic-related changes to the planned visit.

How to Build SV Without Overbuilding It

A strong SV build starts with TV but does not blindly copy TV for every subject. The programmer should identify which planned visits are relevant to the subject's participation period, then merge actual visit/contact evidence from the clinical database, remote visit logs, telehealth systems, vendor transfers, and disposition records.

Build Step What the Programmer Does Common Risk
1. Start from planned TV Identify planned VISITNUM, VISIT, VISITDY, visit windows, and which visits apply to the subject. Creating missed visits after the subject discontinued and was no longer expected to attend.
2. Bring in actual contact evidence Merge EDC visit forms, visit status forms, remote contact logs, phone visit records, and vendor visit dates. Using only completed CRF pages and missing visits that occurred through phone or remote contact.
3. Assign occurrence Populate SVPRESP and SVOCCUR according to whether the visit was planned and whether it occurred. Setting SVOCCUR = N for unscheduled visits, which usually should not exist unless they occurred.
4. Capture contact mode Populate SVCNTMOD when the visit/contact method is collected or required. Losing the distinction between in-person and telephone visits, especially when assessments differ by mode.
5. Reconcile with DS and SE Check visit records against subject participation, treatment/follow-up periods, discontinuation, death, and cutoff rules. Showing planned visits as missed after death, withdrawal, or end of participation.
SV QC Checks Programmers Should Run
Check Why It Matters Example Finding
Planned visit logic SV should reflect planned visits that are relevant to the subject, not every TV row forever. A subject discontinued at Week 4 but SV creates missed Week 8 and Week 12 visits.
SVPRESP/SVOCCUR consistency Planned missed visits should be represented consistently. SVPRESP = Y but SVOCCUR is missing for a scheduled visit that was not performed.
Reason for non-occurrence Missed visit records need reviewer-readable context when required. SVOCCUR = N but SVREASOC is missing even though the source visit status form has a reason.
Contact mode Remote and telephone visits may affect assessment interpretation. Week 4 was done by telephone, but SVCNTMOD is blank and the reviewer cannot tell why physical exams are missing.
Unscheduled visits Unscheduled records should have consistent visit naming and numbering. Two unscheduled visits share the same VISITNUM or are sorted before the scheduled visit they follow.
Timing against SE/DM Visits should make sense within the subject's participation timeline. SVSTDTC occurs after RFPENDTC without a defined follow-up or cutoff rule.
Topic 3. Interventions Domains>

Interventions describe treatments, medications, exposures, or procedures administered to the subject. These domains usually answer: what was given, when it started, when it ended, how much, and by what route.

EX EC CM PR

Mastering Exposure Domains: EX and EC

In CDISC SDTM, capturing a subject's exposure to a study drug is critical for safety and efficacy analyses. Historically, this was handled solely by the EX (Exposure) domain. However, as protocols grew more complex and regulatory bodies such as FDA and PMDA demanded better data traceability, CDISC introduced the EC (Exposure as Collected) domain.

To map these domains correctly, you must understand their contrasting philosophies. EC Domain (Exposure as Collected) is a literal transcription of your electronic Case Report Form (eCRF) or vendor log. It represents what was observed or recorded at the source. If the site recorded that a bottle was dispensed, or that a patient intended to take a dose but missed it, it belongs in EC. EX Domain (Exposure) is an analytical, derived domain representing what the subject actually took. It is the source of truth for downstream ADaM analysis datasets, such as ADEX. EX records the standard ingredient, standard dose, and precise time intervals of actual drug consumption.

The Platinum Rule of Exposure Mapping

EC captures what was recorded or intended. EX captures what was actually administered or ingested.

Core Variables and Architectural Differences

Understanding how variables behave differently between EC and EX is the most critical checkpoint for passing Pinnacle 21 and CDISC Open Rules Engine (CORE) validation.

Dimension / Variable EC Domain (Exposure as Collected) EX Domain (Exposure)
Topic Variable (--TRT) Can be blinded or raw as collected, such as ECTRT = "DRUG A OR PLACEBO", "MASKED", or "BLINDED STUDY DRUG". After authorized unblinding, must reflect the actual treatment administered, such as EXTRT = "ALPELISIB" or "PLACEBO".
Dose and Units (--DOSE, --DOSU) Retains collected source units, such as ECDOSE = 2 and ECDOSU = "TABLETS", mL of dosing solution, device volume, or pump-delivered amount. Converted to protocol-specified or analysis-ready units, such as EXDOSE = 150 and EXDOSU = "mg", "mg/kg", or "mg/m2".
Dose Modifications Can capture collected variations from planned dosing: dose holds, eliminations, reductions, increases, interruptions, or adjustment notes. Should translate actual administration into exposure intervals; when the actual dose changes, start a new EX interval.
Permissibility of Missing Doses (--OCCUR, --STAT) Allowed. If a subject missed a planned dose, ECOCCUR = "N" and ECSTAT = "NOT DONE"; reasons may use ECREASOC or SUPPEC if needed by the specification. Strictly forbidden. EX must only contain records where the drug was actually taken. You cannot have EXOCCUR = "N".
Data Structure / Rows Typically one row per eCRF log entry or bottle return diary entry. Organized by constant dosing intervals, collapsing continuous daily dosing into simplified date ranges.
Advanced Mapping Scenario 1: Constant Dosing Intervals in EX
