In many trials, a subject takes a pill once a day for six months. Your eCRF, and therefore EC, might track this through monthly capsule counts or weekly check-ins. You should not create 180 rows in EX for each individual day unless the dose changed.

Instead, group the data into continuous blocks where the dose remained constant. If a subject takes 150 mg daily from Day 1 to Day 28, generate one row in EX: EXSTDTC = "2026-01-01", EXENDTC = "2026-01-28", EXDOSE = 150. If the dose is reduced to 100 mg on Day 29 due to an adverse event, start a new row in EX.

EC to EX Example: Collected Records Become Constant Dosing Intervals EC + EX

ec.xpt

USUBJID ECTRT ECDOSE ECDOSU ECSTDTC ECENDTC ECOCCUR ECREASOC ECLNKID
DUMMY-101-001 DRUG A OR PLACEBO 1 TABLET 2026-01-01 2026-01-28 Y LNK001
DUMMY-101-001 DRUG A OR PLACEBO 1 TABLET 2026-01-29 2026-02-28 Y LNK002
DUMMY-101-001 DRUG A OR PLACEBO 2026-03-01 2026-03-01 N FAMILY EMERGENCY

ex.xpt

USUBJID EXTRT EXDOSE EXDOSU EXSTDTC EXENDTC EXLNKID
DUMMY-101-001 ALPELISIB 150 mg 2026-01-01 2026-01-28 LNK001
DUMMY-101-001 ALPELISIB 100 mg 2026-01-29 2026-02-28 LNK002

Teaching point: EC can preserve collected blinded text and missed planned dose information. EX contains only actual administered or ingested exposure, with standardized treatment name and standardized dose.

Advanced Mapping Scenario 2: Double-Blind Study Drug with Multiple Active Dose Levels

In a double-blind study, the site and subject may only see a blinded kit or a generic instruction such as "take two tablets once daily." EC should preserve that collected dosing context. EX, however, is built after the appropriate unblinding step and must show the actual treatment and the actual standardized dose in milligrams. This is especially important when the active drug has several dose strengths but every subject takes the same visible number of tablets.

Example: the active drug has four dose levels: 50 mg, 100 mg, 150 mg, and 200 mg. Matching placebo subjects also take two tablets to preserve the blind. In EC, all subjects can look similar because the collected instruction is two tablets. In EX, the true exposure is different: active subjects receive their actual milligram dose, while placebo subjects are represented as PLACEBO with EXDOSE = 0 mg.

Double-Blind Exposure Example: Same Tablet Count, Different Actual Dose EC + EX

ec.xpt

USUBJID ECDY ECTRT ECDOSE ECDOSU ECROUTE ECSTDTC ECOCCUR ECLNKID
DUMMY-101-001 1 BLINDED STUDY DRUG 2 TABLETS ORAL 2026-01-01 Y LNK101
DUMMY-101-001 2 BLINDED STUDY DRUG 2 TABLETS ORAL 2026-01-02 Y LNK101
DUMMY-101-001 3 BLINDED STUDY DRUG 2 TABLETS ORAL 2026-01-03 Y LNK101
DUMMY-101-002 1 BLINDED STUDY DRUG 2 TABLETS ORAL 2026-01-01 Y LNK102
DUMMY-101-002 2 BLINDED STUDY DRUG 2 TABLETS ORAL 2026-01-02 Y LNK102
DUMMY-101-005 1 BLINDED STUDY DRUG 2 TABLETS ORAL 2026-01-01 Y LNK105
DUMMY-101-005 2 BLINDED STUDY DRUG 2 TABLETS ORAL 2026-01-02 Y LNK105
DUMMY-101-005 3 BLINDED STUDY DRUG 2 TABLETS ORAL 2026-01-03 Y LNK105

ex.xpt

USUBJID EXTRT EXDOSE EXDOSU EXROUTE EXSTDTC EXENDTC EXLNKID
DUMMY-101-001 ACTIVE DRUG 50 mg ORAL 2026-01-01 2026-01-28 LNK101
DUMMY-101-002 ACTIVE DRUG 100 mg ORAL 2026-01-01 2026-01-28 LNK102
DUMMY-101-003 ACTIVE DRUG 150 mg ORAL 2026-01-01 2026-01-28 LNK103
DUMMY-101-004 ACTIVE DRUG 200 mg ORAL 2026-01-01 2026-01-28 LNK104
DUMMY-101-005 PLACEBO 0 mg ORAL 2026-01-01 2026-01-28 LNK105

Teaching point: EC explains what was collected under the blind. EX explains the real exposure after decoding. A patient diary may create one EC row per dosing day, while EX can collapse those daily records into a constant dosing interval. The tablet count alone is not enough for analysis because two tablets can represent 50 mg, 100 mg, 150 mg, 200 mg, or placebo.

Advanced Mapping Scenario 3: Traceability and RELREC

To maintain a clear audit trail between what was collected in EC and what was derived in EX, link records using the Link ID variable (--LNKID). Assign an identifier, such as "LNK001", to ECLNKID on the source record. Assign the exact same identifier to EXLNKID on the corresponding derived EX record. Define this link clearly within RELREC (Relationship Records).

Traceability Step What the Programmer Does Reviewer Benefit
Assign ECLNKID Put a stable link ID on the collected EC source record. The reviewer can identify which collected record fed the derived interval.
Assign EXLNKID Carry the same link ID to the corresponding EX record. The reviewer can trace the standardized exposure back to collection.
Document RELREC Define the relationship between EC and EX records in RELREC. The relationship is explicit, reviewable, and submission-ready.
Advanced Mapping Scenario 4: Missed Doses and Temporary Interruptions

One of the most frequent errors committed by programmers is generating EX rows for periods when a patient stopped taking medication. Consider a subject on a daily IV infusion who misses the Week 4 infusion due to a family emergency.

  • EC Mapping: Create a row in EC for Week 4. Set ECPRESP = "Y", ECOCCUR = "N", and capture the reason in ECREASOC = "FAMILY EMERGENCY".
  • EX Mapping: Do not create a row in EX for Week 4. The EX rows should simply show a gap in dates between Week 3 and Week 5.
Integration with Subject Elements (SE) and Disposition (DS)

Modern validation engines automatically run cross-domain checks. Your EX records must align with the subject's timeline. For epoch verification, ensure that the EPOCH variable in EX matches the actual active treatment phase defined in the SE (Subject Elements) domain. For post-discontinuation checks, run a hard check to ensure no EX or EC records with actual administration, such as EXDOSE > 0, exist after a subject's official date of death or study discontinuation as recorded in DS.

Quality Control Checklist for Programmers

Before submitting EX and EC domains for peer review or Pinnacle 21 validation, execute these five mandatory QC checks.

QC Check Run a Query For Why It Matters
1. The EX Zero-Dose Check Non-placebo records where EXDOSE = 0, or missing EXDOSE where EXSTAT is not used. Placebo may be represented as 0 mg when specified. For active drug, a 0 dose usually means the subject did not receive drug and should not be represented as actual exposure.
2. Unblinded Variable Verification EXTRT values containing strings like "BLINDED", "ACTIVE OR PLACEBO", or "ARM A". EX must reflect the absolute unblinded truth post-lock. Placeholder names in EX are an automatic regulatory rejection risk.
3. Chronological Logic Check EXSTDTC > EXENDTC or overlapping date intervals for the same EXTRT. A subject cannot be on two different constant dosing intervals of the exact same single medication at the same time. Gaps are allowed; overlaps are programming bugs.
4. Cross-Domain Synchronization with DM.ACTARMCD Subjects who have active dosing records in EX but are assigned to ACTARMCD = "SCRNFAIL" or "NOTTRT" in DM. If a subject received a drug, they are by definition treated. Their actual treatment arm must reflect reality.
5. ECSTAT and ECOCCUR Alignment (ECOCCUR = "N" and ECSTAT ne "NOT DONE") or (ECOCCUR = "Y" and ECSTAT ne ""). If the planned dose did not occur, ECOCCUR must be "N" and ECSTAT must be "NOT DONE". If the dose occurred, ECOCCUR is "Y" and ECSTAT must remain blank.

Practice Scenario: The Week 4 Missed Infusion

A subject is expected to receive weekly IV infusions. The Week 4 visit occurred, but the infusion was missed because of a family emergency. The eCRF contains a planned-dose record and a "Was dose administered?" question.

  1. Should EC contain a Week 4 row?
  2. Should EX contain a Week 4 row?
  3. Which variables preserve the reason for the missed dose?
  4. What mistake would create a regulatory concern?
Answer Key
  1. Yes. EC can represent the planned or collected exposure record with ECPRESP = "Y" and ECOCCUR = "N".
  2. No. EX should only contain actual administered or ingested exposure.
  3. Use ECOCCUR = "N", ECSTAT = "NOT DONE", and ECREASOC = "FAMILY EMERGENCY", if collected and specified.
  4. Creating an EX row with zero dose or a non-occurring exposure record would misrepresent actual drug exposure.

Conclusion: EC and EX are not interchangeable. EC protects source traceability and collection context. EX provides the standardized actual exposure record needed for downstream analysis. Respecting that boundary is the heart of exposure programming.

Topic 4. Events Domains>

Events describe things that happened to the subject, such as adverse events, medical history, disposition events, and protocol deviations. These domains usually have a topic term, start and end timing, seriousness or category qualifiers, and sometimes dictionary coding.

AE MH DS DV CE

AE and MH often depend on MedDRA coding. DS can include informed consent, randomization, completion, discontinuation, and death-related disposition records. Do not assume all DS records come from a single CRF page.

Adverse Events in SDTM: From Reported Term to Safety Analysis

The AE (Adverse Events) domain belongs to the Events observation class. It records any untoward medical occurrence experienced by a subject during a clinical trial, regardless of whether the event is considered related to the investigational product. For programmers, the first rule is simple: AE is not a causality-only dataset. If the event happened during the protocol-defined collection period and meets the study's adverse event reporting rules, it belongs in AE even when the investigator says it is not related to study drug.

Core AE Variable Structure

AE variables can be understood by role. The topic is the reported adverse event term; qualifiers describe seriousness, severity, causality, outcome, and coding; timing variables describe when the event started and ended.

Variable Name Variable Label Type Role and Explanation
STUDYID Study Identifier Char Identifier: study number.
USUBJID Unique Subject Identifier Char Identifier: unique subject-level key across the study.
AESEQ Sequence Number Num Identifier / sequence: record sequence number within each subject, usually assigned from 1 upward after sorting AE records by the study specification.
AETERM Reported Term for the Adverse Event Char Topic: original reported term from the Case Report Form (CRF), without programmer rewriting.
AEDECOD Dictionary-Derived Term Char Qualifier: MedDRA Preferred Term (PT) derived through medical coding.
AEBODSYS Body System or Organ Class Char Qualifier: MedDRA System Organ Class (SOC), used heavily in safety summaries.
AESEV Severity/Intensity Char Qualifier: intensity of the symptom, such as MILD, MODERATE, or SEVERE.
AESER Serious Event Char Qualifier: regulatory seriousness flag, normally Y or N.
AEREL Causality Char Qualifier: investigator assessment of relationship to study drug, such as RELATED or NOT RELATED, following the study standard.
AEOUT Outcome of Adverse Event Char Qualifier: outcome such as RECOVERED, NOT RECOVERED, RECOVERING, or FATAL.
AESTDTC Start Date/Time of Adverse Event Char Timing: AE start date/time in ISO 8601 character format.
AEENDTC End Date/Time of Adverse Event Char Timing: AE end date/time in ISO 8601 character format; blank when the event is ongoing or the end date is not collected.
Key Mapping Rules and Industry Practice
Topic Rule Programming Focus
Dictionary Coding AETERM must remain the original reported text from the source CRF. AEDECOD and AEBODSYS come from MedDRA coding. Do not manually hard-code AEDECOD or AEBODSYS in SDTM programs. Merge to the official company medical coding database or approved coding transfer.
Severity vs. Seriousness AESEV is symptom intensity. AESER is a regulatory safety concept. A severe headache is not automatically serious. A mild event can be serious if it causes hospitalization, is life-threatening, causes disability, or results in death.
ISO 8601 Dates AESTDTC and AEENDTC must use SDTM character date/time format, such as YYYY-MM-DD or YYYY-MM-DDThh:mm:ss. Preserve partial dates in SDTM. If the subject remembers only 2026-04, keep AESTDTC = "2026-04"; do not impute 2026-04-01 in SDTM.
Ongoing Events If the AE is still ongoing at the time of collection, AEENDTC is blank and AEOUT should reflect the collected outcome. Do not force an end date from last visit, database lock, or treatment end unless the specification explicitly defines that transformation.
Severity is not Seriousness

AESEV answers: how intense was the event? AESER answers: did the event meet regulatory seriousness criteria? A fracture requiring hospitalization is serious even if the investigator says it is not related to study drug.

Case Study: Two Adverse Events for One Subject

Subject 101-001 has two adverse events. Event 1 is mild headache beginning on 2026-03-15 after breath-holding. No action is taken, the event resolves on 2026-03-18, and the investigator considers it not related to study drug. Event 2 is a severe left leg fracture due to a car accident on 2026-04-02. The subject is hospitalized urgently. The investigator considers the event not related to study drug, but the fracture has not recovered and the subject has not yet been discharged.

Event Programming Interpretation
Event 1: Mild headache AETERM = "Headache". MedDRA coding gives AEDECOD = "Headache" and AEBODSYS = "Nervous system disorders". AESEV = "MILD". Because it did not cause hospitalization, life-threatening risk, disability, or death, AESER is normally "N".
Event 2: Left leg fracture due to car accident AETERM preserves the reported phrase. MedDRA coding may give AEDECOD = "Leg fracture" and AEBODSYS = "Injury, poisoning and procedural complications". Because the event caused hospitalization, AESER must be "Y" even though AEREL = "NOT RELATED". AEENDTC remains blank because the event is ongoing.
SDTM AE Dataset View ae.xpt

ae.xpt

STUDYID USUBJID AESEQ AETERM AEDECOD AEBODSYS AESEV AESER AEREL AEOUT AESTDTC AEENDTC
PROJ001 101-001 1 Headache Headache Nervous system disorders MILD N NOT RELATED RECOVERED 2026-03-15 2026-03-18
PROJ001 101-001 2 Left leg fracture due to car accident Leg fracture Injury, poisoning and procedural complications SEVERE Y NOT RELATED NOT RECOVERED 2026-04-02

Teaching point: AESER is driven by seriousness criteria, not causality. The fracture is unrelated to drug, but it is still serious because it required hospitalization.

Advanced AE Topic: Treatment-Emergent Flow from Protocol to ADaM

One of the hardest safety programming concepts is not simply how to build AE. It is how treatment-emergent logic flows across the full study lifecycle: Protocol, Statistical Analysis Plan (SAP), SDTM, supplemental qualifiers, and ADaM. A programmer may see both SUPPAE.AETRTEM and ADAE.TRTEMFL and assume they are interchangeable. They are not.

The top-level authority for deciding whether an AE is treatment-emergent is the Protocol and SAP, not the SDTM implementation guide alone. The Protocol defines when treatment starts, how long safety follow-up continues, and what visit or exposure anchors matter. The SAP defines the actual Treatment-Emergent Adverse Event (TEAE) algorithm. A common rule is: an AE is treatment-emergent if the AE start date/time is on or after first dose and on or before last dose plus a protocol-defined washout window, such as 28 or 30 days. The SAP may also define that a pre-existing AE becomes treatment-emergent if it worsens after treatment begins.

The end of the TEAE window is not a universal programming constant. Some SAPs clearly state last dose plus 30 days, 28 days, 14 days, or through the end-of-study safety follow-up visit. Other SAPs are less explicit and may only describe "during treatment and follow-up" or defer the rule to the protocol. In that situation, the programmer should not hard-code a personal default such as 30 days. The correct action is to confirm the safety window with the statistician, medical monitor, or approved analysis specification before deriving ADAE.TRTEMFL.

SUPPAE.AETRTEM vs. ADAE.TRTEMFL
Dimension SDTM Supplemental Variable: SUPPAE.AETRTEM ADaM Analysis Variable: ADAE.TRTEMFL
Concept Treatment-emergent flag captured or carried in the SDTM era, usually as a nonstandard supplemental qualifier. Treatment-emergent analysis flag used for safety analysis and TEAE summaries.
Dataset Location Stored in SUPPAE as QNAM = "AETRTEM", because AE has no standard variable for this collected checkbox. Stored directly in ADAE, usually as TRTEMFL, according to the ADaM specification.
Source of Logic Often comes from an investigator or site checkbox on the eCRF, such as "Did this event occur or worsen after first dose?" Derived by the ADaM programmer using the SAP algorithm, exposure dates, AE dates, and imputation rules.
Partial or Missing Dates Cannot reliably solve partial dates by itself. It preserves the collected answer or source judgment. Uses SAP-defined imputation rules, such as conservative or worst-case date handling, before assigning the flag.
Analysis Authority Lower for final statistical reporting. It may be useful for traceability or reconciliation but is not the final analysis rule. Highest for safety Tables, Listings, and Figures (TFLs). TEAE summaries should use the ADaM analysis flag.
Why ADaM Re-Derives Treatment Emergence

SDTM preserves what was collected. ADaM prepares data for analysis. If a CRF checkbox conflicts with dates, or if dates are partial, the final TEAE decision must follow the SAP algorithm. That is why ADAE.TRTEMFL can differ from SUPPAE.AETRTEM.

Interactive Case Study: Partial AE Date and Conflicting CRF Judgment

Subject 101-001 first receives study drug on 2026-07-15. The reported AE is nausea. The CRF collects the AE start date only as 2026-07, with day missing. The investigator remembers the event as pre-treatment and checks "No" for whether the event occurred or worsened after treatment.

Treatment-Emergent Flag Flow SDTM + ADaM

ae.xpt

STUDYID USUBJID AESEQ AETERM AEDECOD AEBODSYS AESTDTC
PROJ001 101-001 1 Nausea Nausea Gastrointestinal disorders 2026-07

suppae.xpt

STUDYID RDOMAIN USUBJID IDVAR IDVARVAL QNAM QLABEL QVAL QORIG
PROJ001 AE 101-001 AESEQ 1 AETRTEM Treatment-Emergent Flag N CRF

adae.xpt

USUBJID AESEQ PARAM TRTSDT AESTDTC ASTDT ASTDTF TRTEMFL
101-001 1 Nausea 2026-07-15 2026-07 2026-07-15 D Y

Teaching point: SDTM keeps the partial date and the collected site judgment. ADaM applies the SAP imputation rule, derives ASTDT, records the imputation flag ASTDTF = "D", and assigns TRTEMFL = "Y".

Lifecycle Stage What Happens Programmer Lesson
Protocol Defines first dose, treatment period, follow-up period, and safety collection windows. Do not derive TEAE without knowing the protocol-defined exposure and follow-up windows.
SAP Defines the TEAE algorithm, washout window, worsening rules, and partial date imputation. The SAP is the direct authority for ADAE.TRTEMFL.
SDTM AE / SUPPAE Preserves collected AE start date and any collected treatment-emergent checkbox. Do not overwrite collected investigator judgment just because it conflicts with later analysis logic.
ADaM ADAE Derives analysis dates, imputation flags, and TRTEMFL according to SAP. Final TEAE TFLs should use ADAE.TRTEMFL, not SUPPAE.AETRTEM.

Discussion: Which Flag Should TEAE Tables Use?

In the case above, SUPPAE.AETRTEM says "N", but ADAE.TRTEMFL is derived as "Y". When generating the final Summary of TEAEs table, which flag should the programmer use?

Answer Key

Use ADAE.TRTEMFL. Final safety TFLs are based on ADaM. SDTM and SUPPAE protect source traceability and collection context; ADAE applies the Protocol and SAP rules needed for scientific analysis. In safety reporting, the ADaM algorithm result is the final statistical basis.

Training Key Takeaway

SDTM, including SUPPAE.AETRTEM, records what was seen or collected. ADaM, including ADAE.TRTEMFL, shows what should be counted based on Protocol and SAP rules. In safety evaluation, use the ADaM-derived flag as the final analysis authority.

Quiz: Unknown AE Start Date

The CRF records the adverse event start date as "UNKNOWN" with no year, month, or day available. What should AESTDTC be in SDTM.AE?

Answer Key

AESTDTC should be left blank. SDTM preserves collected information and does not invent dates. Date imputation, such as assigning the treatment start date or the first day of a month, belongs in ADaM/ADAE according to the Statistical Analysis Plan, not in SDTM.AE.

Topic 5. Findings Domains>

Findings domains store measurements, tests, questionnaire responses, and assessments. They usually follow a test/result structure: the test code identifies what was measured and result variables store what was observed.

Important boundary: IE is a Findings-style eligibility result, not a Special Purpose domain. It records subject-level inclusion or exclusion criteria not met. TI stores the planned criteria; IE stores the subject's result against those criteria.

LB LC VS EG QS IE FA PC
Concept Common Variables Example
Test identity --TESTCD, --TEST VSTESTCD = SYSBP; VSTEST = Systolic Blood Pressure
Original result --ORRES, --ORRESU VSORRES = 120; VSORRESU = mmHg
Standardized result --STRESC, --STRESN, --STRESU LBSTRESN after unit normalization
Timing --DTC, VISIT, VISITNUM VSDTC = 2026-06-28T09:15
Eligibility finding IETESTCD, IETEST, IEORRES IETESTCD = INC01; IEORRES = N for a criterion not met

Laboratory Test Results: LB and LC

Laboratory Test Results (LB) is one of the largest and most safety-critical SDTM Findings domains. A single subject can generate hundreds or thousands of laboratory records across hematology, chemistry, urinalysis, coagulation, biomarkers, pregnancy testing, and local or central lab transfers. Each row represents one test, for one subject, at one collection time.

The programmer's job is not only to move vendor data into SDTM columns. The hard part is preserving the original result, applying controlled terminology, selecting the correct standard unit, converting results and reference ranges consistently, and making the output traceable enough for downstream ADLB, safety tables, shift tables, abnormality listings, and reviewer inspection.

Core Rule for Lab Mapping

Keep the original lab result exactly as received in --ORRES and --ORRESU. Standardize only in --STRESC, --STRESN, and --STRESU, using the approved study conversion metadata.

LB Unit Conversion Practice Notes

Laboratory conversion looks simple until the programmer has to defend every value against the source lab report, the vendor transfer, the SDTM specification, and downstream ADLB logic. These seven mistakes are common because they look like cleanup, convenience, or harmless formatting. In a submitted LB dataset, however, each one can break traceability, distort normal range interpretation, or create inconsistent standardized values.

1. Editing LBORRES During Cleanup

LBORRES is the traceability anchor for the entire LB record. It should preserve what the site or laboratory actually reported, in the same form the source reported it. The moment a programmer trims, normalizes, translates, or re-expresses LBORRES, the submitted dataset no longer reconciles cleanly to the source document.

The most tempting edits are trailing-zero cleanup, case standardization, removal of comparison operators, and replacement of source text with sponsor shorthand. None of those belong in LBORRES. The standardized layer is LBSTRESC and, when numeric, LBSTRESN.

Scenario Source Value Wrong LBORRES Correct LBORRES
Trailing zero trimmed 1.20 mg/dL 1.2 1.20
Operator stripped <0.10 ng/mL 0.10 <0.10
Text normalized Below LOQ BLQ Below LOQ
Case changed NEGATIVE Negative NEGATIVE

Regulatory implication: if LBORRES has been altered from the source value, the reviewer cannot easily trace the submitted record back to the CRF, central lab report, local lab report, or external transfer. That is not merely a formatting issue; it is a data integrity issue.

2. Treating LBSTRESC as a Copy of LBORRES

LBSTRESC is not a second storage place for the original result. It is the character representation of the standardized result. If the source reports glucose as 90 mg/dL and the study standard unit is mmol/L, then LBORRES remains 90, but LBSTRESC should represent the converted standardized value.

For numeric results, LBSTRESC should align with LBSTRESN after applying the approved conversion and rounding rules. For qualitative results, LBSTRESC is where the sponsor-approved standardized wording belongs. This is why LBSTRESC may be cleaner than LBORRES without compromising source traceability.

Source Situation LBORRES LBORRESU LBSTRESN LBSTRESC LBSTRESU
Glucose reported conventionally 90 mg/dL 5.0 5.0 mmol/L
Pregnancy test reported as upper case text NEGATIVE NEGATIVE
Urinalysis protein reported as trace Trace TRACE

3. Converting the Number but Leaving the Wrong Standard Unit

The standardized numeric value, character value, and unit must move together. A record is internally inconsistent if LBSTRESN has been converted but LBSTRESU still shows the original unit. This mistake is dangerous because the value may look mathematically plausible while the unit tells the reviewer the wrong scale.

For each LBTESTCD, the study should define a single SDTM standard unit in the approved specification. Glucose might standardize to mmol/L, sodium to mmol/L, hemoglobin to g/L or g/dL depending on the study convention, and creatinine to umol/L or mg/dL depending on the submission standard. The program should not decide this ad hoc.

lb.xpt
STUDYID DOMAIN USUBJID LBSEQ LBTESTCD LBTEST LBORRES LBORRESU LBSTRESN LBSTRESC LBSTRESU Programming Comment
DUMMY-101 LB DUMMY-101-001 1 GLUC Glucose 90 mg/dL 5.0 5.0 mmol/L Correct: value, character value, and unit were converted together.
DUMMY-101 LB DUMMY-101-002 1 GLUC Glucose 90 mg/dL 5.0 5.0 mg/dL Wrong: numeric result was converted, but LBSTRESU still shows the source unit.
DUMMY-101 LB DUMMY-101-003 1 SODIUM Sodium 140 mmol/L 140 140 mmol/L No conversion needed, but the unit still comes from governed metadata.

QC focus: within each test code, check that standardized units are consistent, the conversion factor matches the metadata table, and the character value agrees with the rounded numeric value when LBSTRESN is populated.

4. Converting the Result but Forgetting the Reference Range

This is one of the most dangerous LB conversion errors because it can be completely silent in conformance validation. The result moves into the standard unit, but the reference range stays in the original unit. Then LBNRIND is derived by comparing a converted result against an unconverted range. The comparison is numerically meaningless, but the output can still look like a legitimate LOW, NORMAL, or HIGH flag.

Here is a concrete worked example. A study collects glucose from US sites in mg/dL and selects mmol/L as the SDTM standard unit. A US lab reports glucose as 95 mg/dL with a normal range of 70 to 100 mg/dL. Using the glucose conversion factor, 95 mg/dL becomes 5.27 mmol/L. That converted result is correct. The failure happens if the programmer leaves the normal range as 70 to 100 while labeling the standard unit as mmol/L.

Variable US Site Record Teaching Point
LBORRES 95 Original source result preserved.
LBORRESU mg/dL Original source unit preserved.
LBSTRESN 5.27 95 mg/dL converted to mmol/L.
LBSTRESU mmol/L The standard unit is SI.
LBNRLO 70 mg/dL Source lower reference limit.
LBNRHI 100 mg/dL Source upper reference limit.
LBSTNRLO 70 Wrong: still on the mg/dL scale.
LBSTNRHI 100 Wrong: still on the mg/dL scale.
LBNRIND LOW Wrong: 5.27 is compared against 70 instead of the converted lower limit.

A glucose value of 5.27 mmol/L is within the expected normal range after conversion, approximately 3.88 to 5.55 mmol/L. But if LBSTNRLO is stored as 70 and LBSTRESU is mmol/L, the dataset is effectively comparing 5.27 against 70. The record is incorrectly flagged LOW, and that flag can travel into ADLB, shift tables, potentially clinically significant abnormality checks, and safety summaries.

Variable Wrong Correct
LBSTRESN 5.27 5.27
LBSTRESU mmol/L mmol/L
LBSTNRLO 70 (mg/dL scale, unconverted) 3.88 (mmol/L, converted)
LBSTNRHI 100 (mg/dL scale, unconverted) 5.55 (mmol/L, converted)
LBNRIND LOW (wrong) NORMAL (correct)

Important: the value 5.27 is correct in both cases. The failure is not the numeric result; it is that the reference ranges were not converted to match the standard unit. The same conversion factor, metadata table, and rounding rule used for LBSTRESN must also be used for LBSTNRLO and LBSTNRHI.

Check to run

For every record where LBORRESU differs from LBSTRESU, verify that LBSTNRLO and LBSTNRHI are numerically consistent with the standard unit, not the original unit. If your glucose LBSTRESN values are in the range 3 to 7 but your LBSTNRHI values are in the range 70 to 110, you probably have unconverted reference ranges.

5. Mishandling Inequality Results and Non-Numeric Values

Laboratory data often contain values such as <0.10, >100, Below LOQ, TRACE, NEGATIVE, or POSITIVE. These values are not the same as exact numeric observations. Removing the operator and placing the remaining number into LBSTRESN can misrepresent the measurement.

A common SDTM approach is to preserve the operator in LBORRES and carry an appropriate standardized character value in LBSTRESC. LBSTRESN should be populated only when the standardized result is a true numeric result according to the study specification. If lower or upper limits of quantification are needed, variables such as LBLOQ, LBULOQ, or study-defined supplemental metadata may be used according to the approved standard.

Source Result Bad Shortcut Better SDTM Thinking
<0.10 Set LBSTRESN = 0.10 without explanation. Keep the operator visible in the character result and follow the specification for numeric handling.
Below LOQ Replace source text with BLQ in LBORRES. Keep LBORRES as reported; use LBSTRESC or supplemental variables for standardized interpretation.
TRACE Convert to 0 or missing by personal preference. Use sponsor-defined qualitative standardization and preserve traceability to the source value.

6. Allowing More Than One LBSTRESU per LBTESTCD

The invariant is simple: for a given LBTESTCD, there should be one standard unit. Every record for that test code, regardless of which site collected it, which lab analyzed it, or when it was collected, should have the same value in LBSTRESU. When that rule is violated, the standardized layer is not truly standardized. Analysis results computed across subjects can become numerically mixed.

This mistake usually happens in three specific ways. The first is unit pass-through: the programmer assigns LBSTRESU directly from LBORRESU, assuming all sites report the same unit. The second is a mid-study lab change: the central lab upgrades its reporting system and starts sending a different unit string, such as mIU/mL instead of uIU/mL. These units may be numerically equivalent, but the submitted standard unit string now varies. The third is a new local lab joining mid-study with a unit that was not anticipated in the original mapping specification; the programmer adds a conditional branch instead of updating the governed conversion metadata.

lb.xpt
STUDYID DOMAIN USUBJID LBSEQ VISIT LBTESTCD LBTEST LBORRES LBORRESU LBSTRESN LBSTRESC LBSTRESU LBDTC
DUMMY-101 LB US-001 1 Week 4 TSH Thyrotropin 2.1 uIU/mL 2.1 2.1 uIU/mL 2026-02-01
DUMMY-101 LB EU-007 1 Week 4 TSH Thyrotropin 2.1 uIU/mL 2.1 2.1 uIU/mL 2026-02-01
DUMMY-101 LB US-001 2 Week 24 TSH Thyrotropin 2.3 mIU/mL 2.3 2.3 mIU/mL 2026-06-20
DUMMY-101 LB EU-007 2 Week 24 TSH Thyrotropin 2.4 mIU/mL 2.4 2.4 mIU/mL 2026-06-20

The highlighted problem is conceptual: the numeric values are correct because 1 mIU/mL equals 1 uIU/mL, but LBSTRESU now contains two different strings for the same LBTESTCD. Pinnacle 21 can validate LBSTRESU against controlled terminology, but it does not enforce a single standard unit per LBTESTCD. The fix is architectural: LBSTRESU should be assigned from a conversion metadata table keyed on LBTESTCD, not mapped directly from LBORRESU.

SAS QC Check LBSTRESU by LBTESTCD 
/* Flag any LBTESTCD with multiple LBSTRESU values */
proc freq data=lb noprint;
  tables lbtestcd * lbstresu / out=unit_check;
run;

data unit_fail;
  set unit_check;
  by lbtestcd;
  if not (first.lbtestcd and last.lbtestcd);
run;

7. Treating Alternate Units as a Late-Stage Decision

This is the structural mistake. The previous issues are implementation errors; this one is a planning failure that cannot be corrected cleanly at database lock. Alternate unit handling should be decided during study design, vendor transfer planning, SDTM specification development, Define.xml planning, and QC design.

Reference basis: FDA study-data technical guidance for clinical submissions describes an SI-standardized LB domain and, when conventional-unit results are needed, an LC domain structured like LB. In that model, LB carries SI-standardized values in LBSTRESU, LBSTRESC, and LBSTRESN. LC carries corresponding conventional-unit values in LCSTRESU, LCSTRESC, and LCSTRESN. The ideal source for both unit systems is the lab vendor itself. If the vendor does not provide both systems, sponsor-side conversion must be governed, documented, reviewed, and traceable.

A post-hoc conversion that is not in the original study specification, not in Define.xml, and not reviewed by the QC programmer is difficult to defend under audit. The time to request dual-unit vendor output, agree on conversion factors, and define rounding rules is during study setup, not after database lock.

Approach Status LB Content Alternate Unit Review Risk
SUPPLB workaround Outdated for FDA clinical lab submissions Sponsor-chosen standard unit only Buried in supplemental qualifiers Poor reviewer access and weak tabulation clarity.
LB only with post-hoc conversion Operationally fragile SI-standardized values Computed later in SAS without clear dataset structure High risk if conversion is absent from spec, Define.xml, or QC plan.
LB + LC Preferred when both SI and conventional units are required SI-standardized result and SI reference range LC contains corresponding conventional-unit result and reference range Clearer reviewer navigation when planned and documented from the start.

Practical takeaway: do not treat alternate units as a programming cleanup item. Decide whether LC is needed before transfer specifications are finalized, confirm whether the lab can provide both unit systems, document conversion metadata and rounding rules, and make the LB/LC relationship visible in Define.xml and the SDTM programming specification.

1. Findings Structure Applied to Laboratory Data
Findings Concept LB Variables Plain-English Meaning Programming Focus
Identifiers STUDYID, DOMAIN, USUBJID, LBSEQ Identify the study, domain, subject, and record sequence. LBSEQ is assigned within subject after sorting by specimen time, category, test, and source order. Do not map sequence from vendor row number unless the specification says so.
Topic LBTESTCD, LBTEST Define what lab test was performed. Map vendor terms such as SGPT to the controlled lab test code ALT and test name Alanine Aminotransferase.
Grouping LBCAT, LBSCAT, LBGRP Organize tests into panels or clinical groupings. Use categories such as HEMATOLOGY, CHEMISTRY, URINALYSIS, or COAGULATION. LBGRP can link related panel records when needed.
Original Result LBORRES, LBORRESU What the lab vendor or source file reported. Preserve verbatim value and unit, including character results, symbols, and source formatting when appropriate.
Standard Result LBSTRESC, LBSTRESN, LBSTRESU The standardized result used for review and analysis. LBSTRESC is character. LBSTRESN is numeric only when the standardized result is numeric. LBSTRESU must be consistent for each LBTESTCD.
Reference Range LBSTNRLO, LBSTNRHI, LBNRIND Normal range and clinical interpretation. Convert reference ranges using the same factor as the result. Do not blindly rederive LBNRIND if the approved source provides a clinical interpretation.
Timing VISIT, VISITNUM, LBDTC, LBDY When the specimen was collected. LBDTC is the specimen collection date/time, not necessarily the lab report date. LBDY is calculated against the subject reference start date.
2. Modifier Variables That Change Lab Interpretation

Lab records are not only a result and a unit. Vendor transfers often carry context variables that determine how the result should be interpreted. New programmers should preserve these attributes when they are collected or provided, because the same test name can mean different things under different specimen, fasting, or method conditions.

Variable What It Captures Programming Example
LBSPEC Specimen type, such as BLOOD, SERUM, PLASMA, URINE, or CSF. Glucose from blood and glucose from urine should not be interpreted as the same clinical concept just because the test name looks similar.
LBLSTAF Fasting status, usually Y or N when collected. Fasting status is especially important for glucose and lipid results. Do not drop it if the vendor provides it or the CRF collected it.
LBMETHOD Laboratory method, such as STRIP, MICROSCOPY, or AUTOMATED ANALYZER. A urine protein result from a dipstick method may not be directly comparable with a quantitative analyzer result.
3. LB and LC: SI Units versus Conventional Units

LB carries the SI-standardized lab result. When a parallel conventional-unit dataset is required, LC is built as the conventional-unit twin of LB. The two datasets follow the same Findings logic: the same subject, same test, same visit, same collection time, and same original result can appear in both places. The variables that diverge are the standardized result and standardized reference range variables.

Area LB LC Training Point
Purpose Laboratory results standardized to SI units. Laboratory results standardized to conventional units, when required. Do not treat LC as a random copy of LB. It is a parallel standardization target.
Original Values LBORRES and LBORRESU preserve the source result and unit. LCORRES and LCORRESU preserve the same source result and unit. The original result normally stays traceable to the same vendor value.
Standardized Values LBSTRESC, LBSTRESN, LBSTRESU target SI units. LCSTRESC, LCSTRESN, LCSTRESU target conventional units. This is where glucose, bilirubin, creatinine, and similar tests may differ.
Reference Ranges LBSTNRLO and LBSTNRHI use SI units. LCSTNRLO and LCSTNRHI use conventional units. Convert ranges with the same conversion logic used for the result.
Sequence LBSEQ identifies the LB record. LCSEQ should remain traceable to the corresponding LB row when the study standard requires paired records. Keep sort order and pairing stable so reviewers can compare LB and LC.
4. Conversion Pipeline
Step What Happens Programmer Check
1. Preserve source Map raw lab result to LBORRES/LBORRESU and LCORRES/LCORRESU. Do not clean, round, translate, or convert the original result columns.
2. Normalize test identity Map vendor test names to controlled LBTESTCD/LBTEST. Vendor SGPT becomes ALT; vendor WBC may become Leukocytes depending on study standards.
3. Apply SI conversion Derive LBSTRESN, LBSTRESC, LBSTRESU, LBSTNRLO, and LBSTNRHI. Use the approved conversion metadata, not hard-coded personal formulas.
4. Apply conventional conversion Derive LCSTRESN, LCSTRESC, LCSTRESU, LCSTNRLO, and LCSTNRHI. If conventional equals original, still populate the standardized columns consistently.
5. Apply precision rules Carry forward the study metadata rule for decimal precision and rounding. Do not casually use code such as round(value, 1). SDTM should preserve adequate precision, or follow the approved metadata exactly.
6. Validate consistency Check units, ranges, numeric/character results, and test code/test name pairing. One LBTESTCD should not have multiple LBSTRESU values unless the specification explicitly supports that design.
5. Worked Example: Glucose Reported in mg/dL

A central lab vendor sends glucose as 90 mg/dL, with a reference range of 70 to 110 mg/dL. The study standard says LB must use SI units for glucose in mmol/L, while LC uses conventional units in mg/dL. The conversion factor from mg/dL to mmol/L is 0.0555. In this dummy study, the metadata keeps three decimals for converted glucose values so the SDTM data do not lose precision before downstream analysis.

Glucose Example: Same Source Record, Two Standardized Unit Targets LB + LC

raw_lab.csv

SUBJID VISIT TEST_RAW RESULT UNIT LOW HIGH COLL_DTC
101-001 WEEK 4 Glucose 90 mg/dL 70 110 2026-03-15T08:20

lb.xpt

USUBJID LBSEQ LBTESTCD LBTEST LBORRES LBORRESU LBSTRESN LBSTRESC LBSTRESU LBSTNRLO LBSTNRHI LBDTC
DUMMY-101-001 1 GLUC Glucose 90 mg/dL 4.995 4.995 mmol/L 3.885 6.105 2026-03-15T08:20

lc.xpt

USUBJID LCSEQ LCTESTCD LCTEST LCORRES LCORRESU LCSTRESN LCSTRESC LCSTRESU LCSTNRLO LCSTNRHI LCDTC
DUMMY-101-001 1 GLUC Glucose 90 mg/dL 90 90 mg/dL 70 110 2026-03-15T08:20

Teaching point: the original result stays 90 mg/dL in both domains. The standardized LB value is converted to SI units, while LC keeps the conventional standardized value. Because the vendor already supplied the conventional unit, LCORRES, LCORRESU, LCSTRESN, LCSTRESC, and LCSTRESU remain aligned to the same mg/dL scale. The LB reference range is converted with the same factor as the result.

6. Case Study: Vendor Alias, Qualitative Result, and Below Limit Result

Laboratory vendors rarely send perfectly standardized data. One transfer may use old test names, local abbreviations, qualitative values, or results with inequality symbols. Programmers should learn to separate three tasks: preserve the source, normalize the test identity, and only create numeric standardized results when the result is truly numeric.

Raw Vendor Record Correct SDTM Handling Why It Matters
TEST_RAW = SGPT, RESULT = 45, UNIT = U/L LBTESTCD = ALT; LBTEST = Alanine Aminotransferase; LBORRES = 45; LBSTRESN = 45 if U/L is the standard unit. Do not submit vendor aliases as the final test code/name when controlled terminology provides the standard term.
Urine protein RESULT = NEGATIVE, UNIT blank LBORRES = NEGATIVE; LBSTRESC = NEGATIVE; LBSTRESN is missing. A qualitative finding should not be forced into a numeric value.
Biomarker RESULT = <0.05, UNIT = ng/mL LBORRES = <0.05; LBSTRESC = <0.05; LBSTRESN is missing; LBLLOQ = 0.05 if supported by the specification. Do not strip the less-than sign and submit 0.05 as if it were the observed numeric result.
Hemoglobin from one site in g/dL and another in g/L Preserve each original unit in LBORRESU; convert all LBSTRESU values to the single study SI unit. Mixed LBSTRESU values for the same LBTESTCD commonly cause validation and review problems.
7. Central Lab, Local Lab, and Reference Ranges

Many studies use a central laboratory for scheduled assessments and local laboratories for urgent safety checks. Central lab data usually come with one controlled transfer structure and a harmonized reference range strategy. Local lab data may vary by hospital, site, method, specimen, age, and sex. The programmer must know which source produced the result before assigning standardized ranges and abnormality indicators.

Situation SDTM Handling Programming Risk
Central laboratory result Use study-standard test identity, unit conversion, and reference range metadata from the central lab transfer. Do not overwrite central lab interpretation with site-specific assumptions.
Local laboratory result Use the local lab range supplied by the site or vendor. Match by site, lab, specimen, method, sex, age, and collection date when those keys are part of the metadata. A reference range from the wrong site or method can make LBNRIND clinically misleading.
Lab source classification Use approved study conventions such as LBCAT, LBSCAT, source flags, or vendor fields to distinguish CENTRAL LABORATORY and LOCAL LABORATORY records. If the source is unclear, reviewers cannot tell why two records with the same test have different ranges or methods.

Reference ranges are not decorative metadata. They drive abnormality listings, shift tables, potentially clinically significant abnormality rules, and reviewer interpretation. At the training level, keep one rule in mind: when a standardized result is converted, the standardized reference limits and units must follow the same approved metadata. Detailed failure patterns are kept in the practice notes below so this main lesson does not repeat the same conversion examples twice.

8. Not Done Laboratory Records

Not every planned or collected lab assessment produces a result. A sample may be hemolyzed, insufficient, lost in transit, or unsuitable for a specific assay. When the study collects the fact that the test was not performed, the record should describe the non-occurrence rather than inventing a result.

Variable Correct Use Common Error
LBSTAT Set to NOT DONE when the lab test was expected or collected as not performed. Leaving LBSTAT blank while also leaving all result variables blank, making the record look incomplete instead of intentionally not done.
LBREASND Capture the reason, such as HEMOLYZED SAMPLE, INSUFFICIENT SAMPLE, SAMPLE LOST, or TEST NOT PERFORMED. Putting the reason into LBORRES or a free-text comment when LBREASND is available.
Result variables Keep LBORRES, LBSTRESC, and LBSTRESN missing when LBSTAT is NOT DONE. Creating a pseudo-result such as NOT DONE in LBORRES or zero in LBSTRESN.
9. When SUPPLB or SUPPLC Is Needed

Standard LB and LC variables should be used first. Supplemental qualifiers are reserved for study-specific or vendor-specific attributes that are important for traceability but do not have an appropriate standard variable.

Scenario Possible Supplemental Handling Training Point
Detailed laboratory vendor name Use a standard variable if available; otherwise a supplemental qualifier may store the extended vendor detail required by the study. Do not create non-standard columns directly in LB just because the transfer has extra vendor fields.
Retest flag A study-specific qualifier such as QNAM = LBRETEST can identify a repeat test when no standard variable captures the exact business meaning. Retests affect listings, baseline selection, and abnormality review; the record should remain traceable to the original lab event.
Extra local-lab context Supplemental qualifiers may preserve site- or vendor-specific interpretation notes when they are required by the specification. Use SUPP-- deliberately. If a standard LB variable fits, use the standard variable instead.
10. QC Checklist for LB and LC
QC Check Run a Query For Why It Matters
Required variables Missing STUDYID, USUBJID, LBSEQ, LBTESTCD, or LBTEST. Required identifiers and topic variables must be present for review and validation.
TESTCD / TEST consistency Same LBTESTCD paired with different LBTEST values. ALT should not appear as both Alanine Aminotransferase and SGPT in submitted standard variables.
Numeric result consistency LBSTRESC looks numeric but LBSTRESN is missing, excluding planned qualitative or inequality cases. Numeric standardized results should support numeric summaries and ADLB derivations.
LB/LC pairing LB record exists without corresponding LC record when LC is required, or LC standardized units do not match the conventional target. LB and LC should remain reviewer-comparable when both are part of the submission package.

Quiz: Which Value Should Be Preserved?

A lab vendor sends creatinine as 1.1 mg/dL. The SI target is umol/L. Should the programmer change LBORRES to the converted SI value?

Answer Key

No. LBORRES must preserve the original reported value, so it remains 1.1 and LBORRESU remains mg/dL. The converted SI value belongs in LBSTRESN/LBSTRESC/LBSTRESU. If LC is required and conventional units are mg/dL, the LC standardized result may remain 1.1 mg/dL.

Topic 6. Relationship and Supplemental Datasets>

Relationship and supplemental datasets preserve traceability when the standard domain structure is not enough. They should solve a real metadata problem, not become a place to hide messy mapping.

SUPP-- RELREC FA
  • SUPP--: Store nonstandard qualifiers linked back to a parent domain record.
  • RELREC: Describe relationships between records across domains or within a domain.
  • FA: Findings About can describe findings about an event or intervention when appropriate.
Topic 7. Controlled Terminology, Units, and Dates>

Matching the variable name is not enough. Values must be normalized so the dataset can be reviewed, validated, and reused downstream.

Task What the Programmer Does Common Trap
Codelist normalization Map raw values such as M, Male, or male to the approved CDISC CT value. Leaving site-specific text values in submission variables.
ISO 8601 date handling Convert dates to --DTC format while preserving partial dates such as 2026-06. Imputing missing day or month in SDTM instead of waiting for ADaM SAP rules.
Standard units Convert original results into --STRESN and --STRESC using approved standard units. Forgetting unit conversion, such as lbs to kg or inches to cm.
Topic 8. SAS Template: Mapping Raw Date Components to --DTC>

Modern EDC systems often store dates as separate year, month, day, hour, and minute components. SDTM --DTC variables are character variables, so programmers must build ISO 8601 strings carefully while preserving partial dates.

SAS date reminder: a SAS date value is a numeric count of days from January 1, 1960. For example, 0 means 1960-01-01, 1 means 1960-01-02, and -1 means 1959-12-31. This is different from an SDTM --DTC value, which is a character string such as 2026-06-28 or a partial date such as 2026-06. Use SAS dates for validation and calculations, then output the submitted SDTM date/time as an ISO 8601 character value.

Concept Example Programming Meaning
SAS numeric date 0 January 1, 1960; useful for date arithmetic and comparisons.
SAS numeric date 24283 Displays as 2026-06-28 if a date format such as YYMMDD10. is applied.
SDTM --DTC 2026-06-28 Submitted character date; do not store it as a SAS numeric date.
Partial SDTM --DTC 2026-06 Preserves a missing day. Do not impute the day in SDTM unless the standard or study rule explicitly requires it.
SAS ISO 8601 Date Template --DTC 
/*****************************************************************************
* Purpose: Build an ISO 8601 compliant SDTM --DTC value from raw eCRF
*          date/time components.
* Handles: Complete dates, partial dates, invalid component combinations,
*          and optional time stamps.
*****************************************************************************/

data sdtm_ae(drop=_:);
  set raw_ae;
  length AEDTC $19;
  call missing(AEDTC);

  length _yyyy $4 _mm _dd _hh _ii $2 _datepart $10 _timepart $5;
  call missing(_yyyy, _mm, _dd, _hh, _ii, _datepart, _timepart);

  /* Year is the anchor. If year is missing, the date is unknown. */
  if not missing(raw_yr) then do;
    _yyyy = put(raw_yr, z4.);

    if not missing(raw_mo) and 1 <= raw_mo <= 12 then do;
      _mm = put(raw_mo, z2.);

      if not missing(raw_dy) and 1 <= raw_dy <= 31 then do;
        _dd = put(raw_dy, z2.);
        _datepart = catx("-", _yyyy, _mm, _dd);

        if not missing(raw_hr) and 0 <= raw_hr <= 23 and
           not missing(raw_mn) and 0 <= raw_mn <= 59 then do;
          _hh = put(raw_hr, z2.);
          _ii = put(raw_mn, z2.);
          _timepart = catx(":", _hh, _ii);
          AEDTC = cats(_datepart, "T", _timepart);
        end;
        else AEDTC = _datepart;
      end;
      else AEDTC = catx("-", _yyyy, _mm);       /* YYYY-MM */
    end;
    else AEDTC = _yyyy;                         /* YYYY */
  end;
  else AEDTC = "";
run;
Review Rule Why It Matters Common Mistake
Use z2. and z4. formats ISO 8601 requires two-digit months and days, such as 2026-01-05. Using a simple PUT format that creates 2026-1-5.
Use the T separator for time A full datetime should look like 2026-06-28T14:30. Joining date and time with a blank, or appending time to a partial date.
Preserve partial dates in SDTM SDTM should keep incomplete dates such as 2026 or 2026-06 when the site did not provide full detail. Guessing missing day or month in SDTM instead of leaving imputation to ADaM rules.
Reject impossible combinations A day without a month cannot be placed on a valid timeline. Creating invalid strings such as 2026--12; the safer output is 2026.
Module 07 CDISC for Complex Study Design Complex design concepts, SDTM Trial Design Model (TDM), ADaM period variables, and case studies from crossover, Single Ascending Dose (SAD), and Multiple Ascending Dose (MAD) examples.

CDISC for Complex Study Design

Complex study designs need a clear bridge between the planned protocol design, SDTM Trial Design Model, subject-level SDTM data, and ADaM analysis timing. This lesson follows the PDF flow: complex design concepts first, then SDTM TDM, then ADaM period variables, then case studies.

Complex Study Designs SDTM Trial Design ADaM Period Variables TDM Examples Crossover Case Studies SAD / MAD / Food Effect

1. Complex Study Designs

A clinical study can include several masking approaches and intervention paths: double-blind, single-blind, open-label, multiple interventions, sequential interventions, combination therapies, adaptive designs, SMART designs, multi-arm studies, and crossover designs. FDA reviewers need a clear view of the design so they can judge whether the design is appropriate and whether the submitted data support the trial objectives.

Parallel Study Design
RA
RControl
Double Blind to Open Label
RAA
RControlA
Adaptive or SMART Design
RA
RB
RC / Control
Crossover Study Design

The programming problem is not only which treatment a subject receives. It is also when masking changes, whether the subject crosses over, whether treatment paths are conditional, and which planned element or epoch each record belongs to.

2. SDTM Trial Design Model

The Trial Design Model is built on the identification of elements, arms, epochs, and visits. A practical build order is to create TE first, then TA, then TV. TI and TS complete the Trial Design package with eligibility criteria and study-level summary metadata.

TDM Vocabulary: Elements, Arms, Epochs, Visits
Elements Screen Run-in Placebo Drug A Drug B
Arm: Drug A Screen Run-in Drug A
Arm: Drug B Screen Run-in Drug B
Visits Visit 1 Visit 2 Visit 3 Visit 4 Visit 5

TE defines the building blocks. TA orders those building blocks within each arm and epoch. TV defines the planned visit structure. These datasets describe design intent, not collected subject observations.

Domain Full Name Role in Complex Design
TE Trial Elements Defines the planned building blocks, such as Screen, Run-in, Placebo, Drug A, Drug B, Rest, and Follow-up.
TA Trial Arms Defines each planned arm as an ordered sequence of elements and epochs.
TV Trial Visits Defines the planned visit schedule, such as Visit 1 through Visit 6.
TI Trial Inclusion/Exclusion Stores the planned eligibility criteria from the protocol.
TS Trial Summary Stores study-level design and submission parameters.

3. SDTM TDM in a Crossover Trial

In a crossover trial, subjects receive treatments in different sequences, usually separated by a rest or washout period. The Trial Design Model must show both planned sequences: for example Placebo then Drug X, and Drug X then Placebo. The same element can appear in different positions depending on the arm path.

Crossover TDM: Same Elements, Different Planned Order
Placebo-Drug X Screen Placebo Rest Drug X Follow-up
Drug X-Placebo Screen Drug X Rest Placebo Follow-up
Visits Visit 1 Visit 2 Visit 3 Visit 4 Visit 5 Visit 6

The TE row for Placebo is the same planned element. TA decides where Placebo appears in each arm. This is why crossover design cannot be understood from a single treatment variable alone.

4. ADaM Variables in a Crossover Trial

ADaM must turn the planned design and actual subject timeline into analysis-ready variables. ADSL often contains treatment sequence variables, planned and actual treatment variables by period, treatment period start/end dates, and analysis period start/end dates. Non-ADSL analysis datasets then use those variables to derive TRTA, APERIOD, APERIODC, APHASE, ASPER, and analysis flags.

ADSL Timing Backbone for a Two-Period Crossover
Planned treatment TRT01P Washout TRT02P
Treatment dates TR01SDT TR01EDT TR02SDT TR02EDT
Analysis periods AP01SDT AP01EDT AP02SDT AP02EDT
TRTSEQPTRTSEQATRT01PTRT02PTRT01ATRT02AAPERIODAPERIODCAPHASEASPERASPERC

Treatment periods describe when treatment was given. Analysis periods define the windows used to attribute analysis records. In complex studies these are related, but they are not always identical.

Variable Family Examples Purpose
Treatment sequence TRTSEQP, TRTSEQA Stores planned and actual treatment sequence.
Treatment by period TRT01P, TRT02P, TRT01A, TRT02A Identifies planned and actual treatment in each period.
Treatment dates TR01SDT, TR01EDT, TR02SDT, TR02EDT Defines treatment exposure windows.
Analysis periods AP01SDT, AP01EDT, AP02SDT, AP02EDT, APERIOD, APERIODC Defines analysis windows and labels records by period.
Analysis phases and subperiods APHASE, APHASEN, ASPER, ASPERC Defines broader phases and within-period subperiods.

5. Case Study: TDM Examples

The case study begins by showing how different planned study structures are represented in TE and TA. The key lesson is that TDM must reflect the protocol design, not simply the CRF or raw data layout.

TDM Example 1
TA (Arms/Elements/Epoch)
DOMAIN ARMCD ARM TAETORD ETCD EPOCH
TAP-APlacebo-DrugA1SCRNSCREENING
TAP-APlacebo-DrugA2PRUN-IN
TAP-APlacebo-DrugA3PTREATMENT1
TAP-APlacebo-DrugA4ATREATMENT2
TAA-ADrugA-DrugA1SCRNSCREENING
TAA-ADrugA-DrugA2PRUN-IN
TAA-ADrugA-DrugA3ATREATMENT1
TAA-ADrugA-DrugA4ATREATMENT2
TAB-BDrugB-DrugB1SCRNSCREENING
TAB-BDrugB-DrugB2PRUN-IN
TAB-BDrugB-DrugB3BTREATMENT1
TAB-BDrugB-DrugB4BTREATMENT2
TE (Elements)
DOMAINETCDELEMENT
TESCRNScreen
TEPPlacebo
TEADrugA
TEBDrugB

TAETORD is the order of planned elements inside the arm. The same element code can appear in different epochs depending on arm design.

TDM Example 2
TA (Arms/Elements/Epoch)
DOMAIN ARMCD ARM TAETORD ETCD EPOCH
TAADrugA1SCRNSCREENING
TAADrugA2ABLINDED TREATMENT
TAADrugA3FUFOLLOW-UP
TABDrugB1SCRNSCREENING
TABDrugB2BBLINDED TREATMENT
TABDrugB3CAOPEN LABEL TREATMENT
TABDrugB4FUFOLLOW-UP
TE (Elements)
DOMAINETCDELEMENT
TESCRNScreening
TEADrugA
TEBDrugB
TECAOpen Label DrugA
TEFUFollow-up

The open-label period is represented as a planned element and epoch, not hidden inside subject-level data.

6. Crossover Trial Case Study 1

The first crossover case study is an open-label, multiple-dose, four-sequence, four-period design. Each sequence has six subjects, and the total number of subjects is twenty-four. ADSL needs the subject's sequence and treatment/date variables for all four periods.

Crossover Case Study 1: Four Sequences, Four Periods
Sequence TRT01P TRT02P TRT03P TRT04P Core Dates
S001: ABDCABDCTR01SDT/TR01EDT through TR04SDT/TR04EDT
S002: CDBACDBAAP01SDT/AP01EDT through AP04SDT/AP04EDT
S003: BCADBCADUsed by ADAE, ADLB, and other BDS datasets
S004: DACBDACBSupports treatment attribution by period window
ADAE Occurrence

Derive ASTDT from SDTM AE start date. Assign TRTA, APERIOD, and APERIODC by checking whether the event date falls inside AP01SDT/AP01EDT, AP02SDT/AP02EDT, and so on.

ASTDTTRTAAPERIODAPERIODCTRTEMFL
ADAEDIFF / ADLB

ADAEDIFF can use one row per subject, period, and treatment with AVAL/AVALC. ADLB assigns lab records by comparing ADT to the same analysis period windows.

USUBJIDTRTSEQPTRTAAVALAVALCADT

7. Crossover Trial Case Study 2

The second crossover case study is an open-label, two-part, one-sequence, three-period design. Period 2 is split into subperiods, so the analysis needs APERIOD plus ASPER/ASPERC and subperiod date/time variables.

Crossover Case Study 2: One Sequence, Three Periods, Split Period 2
Treatment TRT01A A TRT02A D TRT03A DA
Analysis period APERIOD 1 APERIOD 2 APERIOD 3
Subperiods P02S1 P02S2
TR01SDTMTR02SDTMTR03SDTMAP01SDTMAP02SDTMP02S1SDMP02S1EDMP02S2SDMP02S2EDMASPERASPERC

When one analysis period has clinically meaningful subperiods, BDS datasets need both the broader period assignment and the finer subperiod assignment. This is why ADAE may carry APERIOD, APERIODC, ASPER, and ASPERC together.

8. Crossover Trial Case Study 3: SAD/MAD and Food Effect

The third case study combines single ascending dose, multiple ascending dose, and food effect evaluation. The ADEG example shows how ECG analysis records can require APHASE, APERIODC, APERIOD, baseline flags, baseline values, and change from baseline.

Crossover Case Study 3: SAD / MAD / Food Effect
Design View

The same study can contain SAD, MAD, food effect evaluation, and drug treatment periods. A record may need both a broad phase and a period-specific analysis assignment.

SADMADFood EffectDrug Period
ADEG View

QTcF records need treatment, visit, analysis phase, analysis period, baseline flag, baseline value, and change from baseline. Baseline may be period-specific, not just one global subject baseline.

TRTAPARAMEGDTCAVISITAPHASEAPERIODCABLFLBASECHG
Variable Group Examples Why It Matters
PhaseAPHASESeparates SAD, MAD, food effect, and follow-up logic.
PeriodAPERIOD, APERIODCAssigns the ECG result to the correct treatment period.
BaselineABLFL, BASE, CHGSupports period-specific baseline and change from baseline.

9. Programmer Takeaways

  • Complex design programming starts with the protocol design, not with raw datasets.
  • TE, TA, and TV describe the planned design; they do not contain subject-level data.
  • ADSL carries the subject-level treatment sequence, period dates, phase dates, and subperiod dates needed by downstream ADaM datasets.
  • ADAE, ADLB, ADEG, and similar datasets derive treatment attribution by comparing record dates to analysis period windows.
  • Baseline, treatment-emergent flags, carryover, and subperiod assignment must be defined by the SAP and ADaM specification.
  • A reviewer should be able to trace from protocol design to TDM, from TDM to ADSL period variables, and from ADSL to each analysis record.
Module 06 Introduction to Analysis Data Model (ADaM) Why analysis datasets are built from SDTM and how variables such as baseline, change, and flags support analysis.

1. Why ADaM Exists

The Analysis Data Model (ADaM) is the bridge between standardized clinical observations and final statistical reporting. Study Data Tabulation Model (SDTM) datasets describe what happened to each subject in a standardized submission format. ADaM datasets explain how those observations should be analyzed according to the Statistical Analysis Plan (SAP).

This distinction matters because Tables, Listings, and Figures (TFLs) should not each reinvent baseline rules, visit windows, imputation rules, treatment grouping, or population definitions. ADaM follows a practical rule: derive once, analyze many times. Complex decisions are made in the analysis dataset build, then reused consistently across outputs.

Protocol / SAP
Define objectives, endpoints, populations, windows, and analysis rules.
SDTM
Standardized record of collected clinical events, interventions, and findings.
ADaM
Analysis-ready datasets with flags, baseline, changes, periods, and derived values.
TFLs
Final statistical outputs generated from approved ADaM datasets.

2. The Three ADaM Design Promises

Traceability Every analysis value should be traceable from the final output back to ADaM, SDTM, and the source record or derivation rule.
Reproducibility A reviewer or independent programmer should be able to rerun the code and regenerate the same result from the submitted datasets and metadata.
Analysis Readiness TFL programs should summarize approved analysis variables, not contain hidden visit-windowing, baseline, or imputation algorithms.

3. ADaM Dataset Structures

Structure Record Pattern Typical Datasets Programmer Focus
Subject-Level Structure One record per subject. ADSL Treatment, populations, important dates, strata, demographics, and study-level anchors.
Basic Data Structure (BDS) One or more records per subject, parameter, and analysis time point. ADVS, ADLB, ADEG, ADEF PARAM/PARAMCD, AVAL, BASE, CHG, visit windows, analysis flags, and imputation indicators.
Occurrence Data Structure (OCCDS) One record per subject per event or occurrence. ADAE, ADCM, ADMH Event counting, treatment-emergent flags, seriousness, severity, occurrence flags, and safety summaries.
Other Study-specific structure when standard structures do not fit. Special survival, exposure-response, or complex design datasets. Must be explained clearly in specifications, Define.xml, and the Analysis Data Reviewer's Guide (ADRG).

4. ADSL: The Subject-Level Anchor

The Subject-Level Analysis Dataset (ADSL) is the foundation of an ADaM package. It has exactly one record per subject and is merged into almost every other analysis dataset. If ADSL treatment dates, population flags, or treatment assignments are wrong, the error spreads into safety, efficacy, disposition, and exposure outputs.

ADSL Subject-Level Example adsl.xpt

adsl.xpt

STUDYID USUBJID TRT01P TRT01A SAFFL FASFL TRTSDT TRTEDT AGE SEX
DUMMY-101 DUMMY-101-001 Placebo Placebo Y Y 2026-02-03 2026-04-10 57 F
DUMMY-101 DUMMY-101-002 YGN-101 YGN-101 Y Y 2026-02-05 2026-04-12 63 M
DUMMY-101 DUMMY-101-003 YGN-101 N Y 44 F

Teaching point: ADSL separates planned treatment from actual treatment. A subject can be randomized and included in an efficacy population while not entering the Safety Population if no study treatment was received.

5. BDS: Parameters, Values, Baseline, and Change

The Basic Data Structure (BDS) is used when analysis depends on a parameter measured over time. Vital signs, laboratory values, electrocardiogram results, and many efficacy endpoints follow this pattern. The programmer must make the record analysis-ready by selecting analysis visits, deriving baseline, calculating change, and setting analysis flags.

Variable Plain-English Meaning Programming Focus
PARAMCD / PARAM Analysis parameter code and label. One PARAMCD should map consistently to one PARAM within the study.
AVAL / AVALC Numeric or character analysis value. Use AVAL for numeric summaries and AVALC for character analysis results.
ADT / ADTM Analysis date or analysis datetime. May be derived from SDTM --DTC values and SAP imputation rules.
AVISIT / AVISITN Analysis visit label and order. Usually derived by visit windowing, not copied blindly from SDTM VISIT.
BASE / CHG / PCHG Baseline, change from baseline, and percent change. Baseline rules come from the SAP; CHG is usually AVAL - BASE.
ABLFL / ANL01FL / DTYPE Baseline flag, analysis flag, and derivation type. Make selected records and imputed or derived records visible to reviewers.
BDS Example: Vital Signs Change from Baseline advs.xpt

advs.xpt

USUBJID PARAMCD PARAM AVISIT ADT AVAL BASE CHG ABLFL ANL01FL
DUMMY-101-001 SYSBP Systolic Blood Pressure (mmHg) Baseline 2026-02-03 136 136 Y Y
DUMMY-101-001 SYSBP Systolic Blood Pressure (mmHg) Week 4 2026-03-03 128 136 -8 Y
DUMMY-101-001 DIABP Diastolic Blood Pressure (mmHg) Week 4 2026-03-03 78 84 -6 Y

Teaching point: the TFL program can summarize AVAL and CHG directly. It should not need to search SDTM VS again to rediscover baseline.

6. OCCDS: Event Data Prepared for Counting

Occurrence Data Structure (OCCDS) is used for event-style records such as adverse events, concomitant medications, and medical history. These datasets usually start from SDTM event or intervention domains and add analysis logic: treatment-emergent flags, analysis start dates, occurrence flags, severity grouping, seriousness grouping, and population-ready treatment information.

OCCDS Example: Adverse Events for Safety Summaries adae.xpt

adae.xpt

USUBJID AESEQ AETERM AEDECOD ASTDT AENDT TRTEMFL SAFFL AESER AOCCFL
DUMMY-101-001 1 Headache Headache 2026-02-08 2026-02-10 Y Y N Y
DUMMY-101-001 2 Nausea Nausea 2026-01-28 2026-02-04 Y N Y
DUMMY-101-002 1 Left leg fracture Lower limb fracture 2026-03-12 Y Y Y Y

Teaching point: ADAE does not simply copy SDTM AE. It adds SAP-driven analysis dates, treatment-emergent logic, population information, and occurrence flags so safety TFLs count events consistently.

7. Traceability Walk-Through

A reviewer should be able to start from a value in a final output and walk backward through the analysis dataset, the derivation rule, SDTM, and the source. This is why ADaM specifications must name not only the target variable but also the source dataset, source variable, algorithm, and any assumptions.

Final Output Question ADaM Variable Derivation Logic Trace Back To
Mean change from baseline in systolic blood pressure at Week 4 ADVS.CHG CHG = AVAL - BASE for PARAMCD = SYSBP and AVISIT = Week 4. ADVS -> SDTM VS -> source vital signs form.
Number of subjects with at least one treatment-emergent adverse event ADAE.TRTEMFL and ADAE.AOCCFL Apply SAP treatment-emergent window and count first occurrence per subject. ADAE -> SDTM AE + EX + ADSL treatment dates.
Safety population denominator ADSL.SAFFL Set to Y for subjects who received at least one dose of study treatment. ADSL -> SDTM EX/EC + randomization and exposure records.

8. Common Beginner Mistakes

  • Programming TFLs directly from SDTM: This repeats derivations in multiple output programs and can create inconsistent results.
  • Putting analysis imputation into SDTM: Missing date imputation, baseline selection, and visit windowing belong in ADaM according to SAP rules.
  • Creating flags without specifications: Population flags, treatment-emergent flags, and analysis flags must be tied to approved definitions.
  • Losing traceability: A derived value without a clear source path is difficult to defend in review.
  • Changing PARAMCD meanings: Once a PARAMCD is defined, its PARAM label, units, and analysis meaning must remain stable.

Practice Check

  1. If BASE is 136 and AVAL is 128, what is CHG?
  2. Which document usually defines the baseline rule and TEAE window?
  3. Which dataset should provide the denominator for a safety table?
Answer Key
  1. CHG is -8.
  2. The Statistical Analysis Plan (SAP) defines the baseline rule and treatment-emergent window.
  3. ADSL usually provides population flags and denominators, such as SAFFL for the Safety Population.
Module 08 Tables, Listings, and Figures (TLF) Basics How tables, listings, and figures differ, and how shells guide statistical programming outputs.

1. What TLF Means

Tables, Listings, and Figures (TLF, also commonly written as TFL) are the final statistical reporting outputs that turn analysis datasets into clinical interpretation. They support the Clinical Study Report (CSR), regulatory review packages, internal decision making, and sometimes manuscripts. A programmer should think of TLFs as the visible end of the whole programming chain: Protocol and Statistical Analysis Plan (SAP) define the question, Study Data Tabulation Model (SDTM) records what happened, Analysis Data Model (ADaM) prepares the analysis-ready variables, and TLF programs present the approved result.

Output Type What It Shows Typical Examples Programmer Focus
Tables Aggregate summaries and statistical inference. Tables convert many subject records into counts, percentages, descriptive statistics, estimates, confidence intervals, p-values, odds ratios, hazard ratios, or adjusted means. Demographics and baseline characteristics; primary efficacy analysis; treatment-emergent adverse event summaries; laboratory shift tables; vital sign changes. Use the correct population denominator, treatment grouping, parameter selection, decimal precision, and analysis flag. Tables should read from ADaM whenever possible.
Listings Subject-level records that let reviewers trace what happened to an individual participant. Listings are not meant to replace tables; they explain the details behind them. Subject disposition; serious adverse events; adverse events leading to discontinuation; concomitant medications; protocol deviations; deaths. Preserve traceability, sort records logically, include important dates and terms, and avoid hiding subject-level information that explains a table count.
Figures Visual displays of patterns, trends, distributions, and time-to-event behavior that are harder to read in a dense table. Kaplan-Meier curves; forest plots; longitudinal mean plots; spaghetti plots; box plots for biomarkers or laboratory values. Use the same analysis dataset, population flag, visit windows, treatment grouping, and censoring or summary rules used by the matching tables.

2. TLF Deliverables in the Reporting Package

TLFs are not just "pretty outputs." They are the final evidence layer used in the Clinical Study Report, submission packages to agencies such as the FDA, EMA, and PMDA, internal benefit-risk review, and sometimes journal manuscripts. Each output type answers a different reviewer question: tables answer "what is the overall result?", listings answer "which subject records support that result?", and figures answer "what pattern can be seen over time or across subgroups?"

Deliverable Reviewer Question Example Output Programming Detail to Protect
Demographics table Were treatment groups comparable at baseline? Table 14.1.2: Demographic and Baseline Characteristics. Use the correct analysis population, planned treatment group, and one record per subject from ADSL.
Primary efficacy table Did the investigational product work for the pre-specified endpoint? Table 14.2.1: Primary Endpoint Analysis with estimate, confidence interval, and p-value. Use the SAP-defined estimator, analysis visit, endpoint parameter, population flag, and analysis record flag.
Safety summary table What adverse events, laboratory abnormalities, or vital sign changes occurred after treatment? Table 14.3.1.1: Treatment-Emergent Adverse Events by System Organ Class and Preferred Term. Use safety population denominators, actual treatment variables such as TRTA or TRT01A, and treatment-emergent flags such as TRTEMFL.
Subject-level listing Can the reviewer trace an individual subject's history? Listing 16.2.7: Adverse Events; Listing 16.2.8: Concomitant Medications; Listing 16.2.1: Disposition. Keep dates, terms, sequence numbers, causality, severity, action taken, and outcome traceable to SDTM/ADaM.
Figure Is there a visual pattern that a table would hide? Kaplan-Meier curve for time-to-event endpoints; forest plot for subgroup effects; longitudinal line plot for biomarkers. Use the same population, censoring rules, visit windows, subgroup definitions, and treatment labels used in the matching tables.

3. What a Shell Provides

A shell is a mock output layout prepared before final data are analyzed. It is not decoration. It is a programming specification for the final report. A shell tells the programmer what the output should look like, which population it uses, what rows and columns are expected, what precision must be displayed, and which footnotes explain the statistical rules.

Shell Area What It Controls Programming Impact
Header and title Output number, endpoint or safety topic, population, treatment labels, page identity, and sometimes cutoff date. Confirms output name, title lines, population flag, treatment variable, sorting, and title consistency with the SAP.
Column headers Treatment groups, total columns, visit columns, subgroup columns, and N values. Drives grouping variables and denominators. For efficacy, planned treatment such as TRT01P is often used; for safety, actual treatment such as TRT01A or TRTA is often used.
Body matrix Rows, indentation, section breaks, statistics, and row order. Drives PARAMCD selection, SOC/PT ordering, visit structure, descriptive statistics, and row templates.
Mock numbers Placeholders such as xx, xx.x, xx (xx.x), x.xx, or xx.x [xx.x, xx.x]. Defines display precision. Counts normally have 0 decimals, percentages often 1 decimal, and p-values commonly 3 decimals unless the SAP says otherwise.
Footnotes and source line Abbreviations, denominator rules, baseline rules, models, imputation notes, and source datasets. Often determines real code logic. Footnotes must match SAP, ADaM specifications, and output programming specifications.

4. Example Shell Reading

A shell should be read as a complete output instruction, not just the body rows. The header tells the population and table identity; the body tells the statistics and layout; the footnotes tell the rules that often drive the code.

YIAN GROUP   |   Protocol YGN-101-02 Page 1 of 1
Table 14.3.4.1
Summary of Vital Signs at Baseline and Change from Baseline
Safety Population
Visit / Analysis Timepoint Statistical Summary Placebo
N = xx		 YGN-101 50 mg
N = xx		 Total
N = xx
Systolic Blood Pressure (mmHg)
Baseline nxxxxxx
Mean (SD)xxx.x (xx.x)xxx.x (xx.x)xxx.x (xx.x)
Medianxxx.xxxx.xxxx.x
Min, Maxxxx.x, xxx.xxxx.x, xxx.xxxx.x, xxx.x
Change from Baseline
at Week 4		 nxxxxxx
Mean (SD)xx.x (xx.x)xx.x (xx.x)xx.x (xx.x)
Medianxx.xxx.xxx.x
Min, Maxxx.x, xx.xxx.x, xx.xxx.x, xx.x
Note: Percentages, if displayed, use the number of subjects in the treatment group as the denominator unless otherwise specified.
Baseline is defined as the last non-missing assessment before the first dose of study treatment.
Change from baseline is calculated as post-baseline value minus baseline value.
SD = standard deviation; Min = minimum; Max = maximum.
Source: ADVS, ADSL. Program: t_14_3_4_1_vs_summary.sas.
Output: t_14_3_4_1_vs_summary.rtf Generated: YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM

5. How ADaM Flags Drive TLFs

Production Tables, Listings, and Figures (TLFs) should not rediscover analysis rules from raw data. They should consume the analysis-ready variables and flags created in Analysis Data Model (ADaM) datasets. The practical rule is simple: derive once in ADaM, display many times in TLFs.

Core Paradigms Paired With Table Shells

When reading a shell, the programmer should immediately ask which ADaM dataset, population flag, treatment variable, parameter, visit, and analysis flag will drive each row and column. A shell is not only a layout; it is a set of programming instructions expressed visually.

01. Denominator: Population Baselines

The goal: populate the column headers with the correct total subject count (N) for the specified analysis population.

The ADaM mechanism: filter ADSL where the population flag, such as SAFFL or FASFL, equals "Y", then count subjects by the treatment variable requested by the shell, such as TRT01P for planned treatment or TRT01A for actual treatment.

Protocol: YGN-101-01 Page 1 of X
Table 14.1.2
Demographic and Baseline Characteristics
Safety Population
Characteristic Placebo
(N = xxx) [1]		 YGN-101 10 mg
(N = xxx) [1]		 Total
(N = xxx)
Age (Years)
nxxxxxx
Mean (SD)xx.x (xx.xx)xx.x (xx.xx)xx.x (xx.xx)
Medianxx.xxx.xxx.x
Min, Maxxx, xxxx, xxxx, xx
Sex, n (%)
Femalexx (xx.x)xx (xx.x)xx (xx.x)
Malexx (xx.x)xx (xx.x)xx (xx.x)
[1] Denominators are derived from ADSL where SAFFL = "Y", grouped by TRT01A.
Percentages are based on the number of subjects in each treatment group unless otherwise specified.
Source: ADSL. Program: t_14_1_2_demog.sas.
02. Efficacy Tables

The goal: display statistical summaries, least-squares means, standard errors, confidence intervals, and p-values for specific parameters at designated analysis visits.

The ADaM mechanism: filter a Basic Data Structure (BDS) dataset, such as ADEFF, where PARAMCD equals the endpoint code and ANL01FL equals "Y". The shell wording should mirror PARAM and AVISIT, not force the TLF program to invent labels or choose records.

Protocol: YGN-101-01 Page 1 of X
Table 14.2.1
